Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447), and because the "convincing mosaic" of evidence shows the administration acted in concert to isolate and threaten the Plaintiff, summary judgment is improper.

**ARGUMENT**

Count I alleges that Duquesne University was deliberately indifferent to known sexual harassment in violation of Title IX, 20 U.S.C. § 1681. To establish liability under Title IX for student-on-student harassment, a plaintiff must demonstrate that (1) the funding recipient had actual knowledge of the harassment by an official with authority to institute corrective measures; (2) the recipient's response was clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances, constituting deliberate indifference; (3) the harassment was so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it deprived the plaintiff of access to educational opportunities; and (4) the harassment occurred in the recipient's programs or activities. *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629, 650 (1999). The undisputed facts and documentary evidence establish each element.

**I. Duquesne Had Actual Knowledge Through Officials with Authority to Act**

Actual knowledge is established when notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment is provided to "a recipient's Title IX Coordinator" or "any official of the recipient who has authority to institute corrective measures on behalf of the recipient." Exhibit Z-e (Training Slide 30, defining actual knowledge). The preamble to the 2020 Title IX regulations identifies as officials with authority to institute corrective measures: "Provost, VP for Student Affairs, Dean of Students, HR Director, etc." Exhibit Z-e (Slide 32).

Plaintiff's first report of harassment occurred on June 30, 2025, when he emailed Dean Wasilko, the Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students, describing a physical confrontation with his roommate Respondent—a fellow graduate student—during which Respondent "got within inches of my face, and aggressively followed me through the apartment," "pursued me up the stairs toward my room," and "only stopped just short of entering." Exhibit B-0. Plaintiff specifically stated he "no longer feel[s] safe" and requested "emergency housing options, protective campus measures, or relevant reporting mechanisms." *Id.* Dean Wasilko, as Dean of Students, is an official with authority to institute corrective measures, and his receipt of this report constituted actual knowledge. Exhibit Z-e (Slide 32). Furthermore, as noted in *Hall v. Millersville University*, where a university official receives a report of sexual misconduct and "filed it away" or failed to forward it, the university has actual knowledge for purposes of Title IX. *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275, at *12 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022).

On July 24, 2025, Plaintiff escalated his complaint directly to Title IX Coordinator Simpson, copying the university president, provost, and legal affairs, thereby providing actual knowledge to the official specifically designated to receive such reports. Exhibit B-1; Exhibit Z-e (Slide 30). Simpson acknowledged receipt and stated she was "in receipt of your email where you allege that there has been noncompliance with Title IX." Exhibit B-1. On August 13, 2025, Plaintiff submitted an Emergency Removal Request to Simpson, attaching sworn declarations and a physician's letter documenting safety concerns. Exhibit F; Exhibit B-5. Simpson responded the same day, acknowledging that Plaintiff's "concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review." Exhibit B-5. Actual knowledge is therefore undisputed.

**II. Duquesne's Response Was Clearly Unreasonable, Constituting Deliberate Indifference**

The Supreme Court in *Davis* defined deliberate indifference as a response that is "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. The Third Circuit has adopted this standard and emphasized that courts must evaluate whether the university's actions were "clearly unreasonable"—not merely whether alternative responses might have been preferable. *Hall*, No. 19-3275, at *10. As the Sixth Circuit noted in *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, "minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment" are not exempt from being found clearly unreasonable as a matter of law. *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314, at *11 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc). Here, Duquesne's conduct falls squarely within this proscription.

**A. Duquesne Provided No Supportive Measures for 45 Days Despite Repeated Requests**

Between June 30, 2025 and August 14, 2025—a period of 45 days—Defendants provided no supportive measures whatsoever. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts (SUMF) ¶ 8. This is despite Plaintiff's repeated, explicit requests for protective measures, including emergency housing, no-contact orders, and safety accommodations. Exhibit B-0 (June 30 request); Exhibit B-1 (July 24 escalation); Exhibit B-5A (August 13 detailed request specifying mutual no-contact orders, space separation, and Public Safety alert).

The Title IX Coordinator is responsible for "coordinating the effective implementation of supportive measures." Exhibit Z-c (Slide 38). Supportive measures are "available at any time after a report of sexual harassment," are "non-disciplinary" and "non-punitive," and must be "individualized" and "provided at no cost." Exhibit Z-c (Slides 38-39). Critically, these measures "are available with or without a formal complaint." Exhibit B-5A (Plaintiff's August 13 email citing 34 C.F.R. § 106.30). Yet Simpson performed "zero of the five mandatory obligations under § 106.44(a)": she did not promptly contact Plaintiff to discuss supportive measures, did not consider his wishes, did not inform him of the availability of supportive measures, and did not explain the process for filing a formal complaint. SUMF ¶ 10.

Instead, Defendants' communications consisted exclusively of requests to schedule meetings, directives to "engage with" offices, and jurisdictional disclaimers. SUMF ¶ 8. Dean Wasilko's initial response on July 1 was to tell Plaintiff the incident "doesn't sound like a code of conduct violation" and to suggest filing a police report. Exhibit B-0. On July 24, after Plaintiff contacted the Title IX Coordinator, Wasilko again directed Plaintiff to "please engage with these offices" and to schedule meetings. Exhibit B-2. On August 13, Simpson responded to Plaintiff's emergency removal request by again demanding a meeting, writing that "it is necessary for representatives of these offices to meet with you to gather additional information." Exhibit B-5. Conditioning supportive measures on a meeting is directly contrary to the regulatory requirement that such measures be offered promptly and without preconditions. *See* Exhibit Z-c (Slide 38) (supportive measures "available at any time after a report"); 34 C.F.R. § 106.30 (supportive measures "offered as appropriate and as reasonably available, without fee or charge").

**B. Simpson's Reversal of Jurisdiction Without New Facts Was Clearly Unreasonable**

On August 13 at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Plaintiff's concerns "warrant further information gathering and review" and offered to "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures." Exhibit B-5. Nineteen hours later, on August 14 at 9:42 AM, after Plaintiff sent three emails refusing a meeting and demanding written communications, Simpson reversed her position, stating "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested." Exhibit B-6. Critically, "[n]o new factual information about the underlying complaint entered the record between B-5 and B-6." SUMF ¶ 7. The only intervening events were Plaintiff's emails—which consisted entirely of protected activity under § 106.71, including citing Title IX regulations, requesting written responses, and restoring OCR to the CC line. Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C. This reversal in response to Plaintiff's assertion of his procedural rights constitutes a clearly unreasonable response. As the Third Circuit recognized in *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, courts must evaluate whether alleged facts support "a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex." *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203, 209 (3d Cir. 2020). The timing and substance of Simpson's reversal support precisely such an inference.

Furthermore, Simpson's conclusion that "the reported alleged conduct does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true" reflects a fundamental misunderstanding of Title IX's requirements. Exhibit B-6. A student's physical pursuit, confrontation, and stalking—particularly when accompanied by a documented reasonable fear of harm—is precisely the type of conduct that Title IX regulations are designed to address. *See* 34 C.F.R. § 106.30 (defining sexual harassment to include "unwelcome conduct determined by a reasonable person to be so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it effectively denies a person equal access to the recipient's education program or activity").

**C. Duquesne Failed to Follow Its Own Policies and Regulatory Requirements**

Duquesne's failures extended beyond the absence of supportive measures. Simpson failed to offer Plaintiff appeal rights following the B-6 dismissal, as required by § 106.45(b)(8). SUMF ¶ 9. She failed to send simultaneous written notice to the respondent, as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii). *Id.* These procedural failures are consistent with the institutional pattern of non-response that the Third Circuit found actionable in *Hall*—where Millersville's failure to forward a sexual misconduct report to the Title IX Coordinator and its failure to contact the victim constituted a clearly unreasonable response. *Hall*, No. 19-3275, at *12-13. Just as "Sehl did not forward Wiberg's report and did not discuss it with anyone until after Karlie's murder," *id.* at *12, Duquesne officials repeatedly failed to take appropriate action on Plaintiff's reports.

The contrast with *McAvoy v. Dickinson College* is instructive. In *McAvoy*, the court affirmed summary judgment for the university where the school implemented a no-contact directive, provided academic and mental health accommodations, conducted a five-month investigation, and imposed a semester of probation. *McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939, at *2 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024). Here, Duquesne did none of these things. The court in *McAvoy* emphasized that "[d]elays alone do not establish deliberate indifference" where the school "maintained ongoing communication and support." *Id.* But Duquesne maintained no communication or support—it merely demanded meetings and ultimately disclaimed jurisdiction entirely.

**III. The Harassment Was Severe, Pervasive, and Objectively Offensive**

The Supreme Court in *Davis* requires that harassment be "so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 650. Plaintiff's evidence satisfies this standard.

On June 30, 2025, Respondent confronted Plaintiff in the kitchen, "got within inches of my face," "aggressively followed me through the apartment," confirmed she was following him when asked, pursued him up the stairs toward his bedroom stating "I'm coming up," and only stopped when he locked himself in his room. Exhibit B-0; Exhibit D (Signal Transcript & Timeline). This encounter was the culmination of a pattern of escalating conduct: Respondent had previously locked Plaintiff out of the apartment (June 27), engaged in manipulative behavior regarding the air conditioning (June 19-25), and, after Plaintiff's move-out, harassed him throughout the process. Exhibit D.

The severity of the harassment is underscored by Plaintiff's documented fear. Plaintiff informed Dean Wasilko, his department chair, and the DGS that he "no longer feel[s] safe" and that he "fear[s] for my safety in any context where she knows my whereabouts." Exhibit B-0; Exhibit R. He specifically reported that Respondent had "expressed strong enthusiasm for the idea that, in her view, the Second Amendment should mean firearm regulations should be less of a hassle than they are." Exhibit R. Treating Physician, Plaintiff's treating physician, opined that Plaintiff's fear was "reasonable" and recommended "every effort be made to avoid" Plaintiff sharing a living space with Respondent, noting that doing so "would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." Exhibit F (Medical Letter, Aug. 13, 2025). A second medical letter confirmed that Plaintiff's homelessness since June 30 "has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions," and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." Exhibit F (Medical Letter, Oct. 1, 2025).

**IV. The Harassment Denied Plaintiff Equal Educational Access**

The harassment and Defendants' deliberate indifference directly denied Plaintiff access to educational opportunities. Plaintiff was forced to flee his home on July 1, 2025, at substantial personal cost. Exhibit B-0; Exhibit D. His bank account balance fell to $16.65 on July 25 and to negative $2.55 on August 12. SUMF ¶ 11. He lacked permanent housing throughout the 45-day period at issue. *Id.* The physician's letter directly links these conditions to impairment of academic functioning. Exhibit F. The Third Circuit in *Doe v. University of the Sciences* held that a plaintiff need not "superimpose additional doctrinal tests" onto Title IX; rather, "the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex." *Doe*, 961 F.3d at 209. Here, the causal chain is direct: Respondent's harassment forced Plaintiff from his housing; Duquesne's deliberate indifference—its refusal to offer any supportive measures for 45 days—left him homeless, financially destitute, and medically impaired; and these conditions "would impair his academic functioning." Exhibit F.

The harassment occurred in Duquesne's programs and activities, including Plaintiff's on-campus housing and his graduate program in the Department of Philosophy. The university's disciplinary authority over Respondent is not in dispute; as the Supreme Court noted in *Davis*, liability attaches where "the harasser is under the school's disciplinary authority." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 646. Respondent was a fellow graduate student at Duquesne, subject to the same Student Code of Conduct and Title IX policies. Exhibit S (Plaintiff listing multiple potential conduct violations).

**CONCLUSION**

Duquesne University had actual knowledge of severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive sexual harassment directed at Plaintiff. Despite this knowledge, the university failed to offer any supportive measures for 45 days, demanded meetings as a precondition for assistance, reversed its jurisdictional determination in response to Plaintiff's assertion of his rights, and ultimately disclaimed responsibility entirely. This response was clearly unreasonable under the known circumstances and constitutes deliberate indifference as a matter of law. Plaintiff respectfully requests that this Court deny summary judgment as to Count I and proceed to trial.

**ARGUMENT**

**A. Duquesne University Had Actual Knowledge of Actionable Sexual Harassment**

Title IX liability requires that a funding recipient have "actual knowledge" of sexual harassment by an official with authority to institute corrective measures. *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629, 641 (1999); *see also* Exhibit Z-e at Slide 30 (defining actual knowledge as notice to the Title IX Coordinator or any official with authority to institute corrective measures). Actual knowledge is triggered when "any person" reports sex discrimination to the Title IX Coordinator. *Id.* at Slide 31.

Here, actual knowledge was established no later than June 30, 2025, when Plaintiff David Mullins reported a physical pursuit and confrontation by his roommate to Dean Wasilko, an official with authority to institute corrective measures. Exhibit B-0. The report described the harasser approaching within inches of Plaintiff's face, yelling, following him through the apartment, verbally confirming she was following him, pursuing him up the stairs toward his bedroom, and stating "I'm coming up"—forcing Plaintiff to lock himself in his room. *Id.* That same evening, Plaintiff requested emergency housing assistance. *Id.*

By July 24, 2025, actual knowledge was independently established through direct notice to Title IX Coordinator Alicia Simpson. Exhibit B-1. Simpson acknowledged receipt of Plaintiff's allegations, confirming that her office "is notified when there are reported incidents of alleged sexual harassment or sexual misconduct involving students." *Id.* Simpson further acknowledged that Plaintiff alleged "noncompliance with Title IX regarding a situation with another student that has caused [him] to be displaced from [his] housing." *Id.*

On August 13, 2025, Plaintiff sent an Emergency Removal Request to Simpson under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(c), attaching sworn declarations and a physician's letter documenting his reasonable fear of physical harm. Exhibit F; Exhibit B-5. Simpson responded that same day, expressly acknowledging that Plaintiff's "concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review." Exhibit B-5. She offered to "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures"—a term of art defined exclusively in 34 C.F.R. § 106.30. *Id.; see also* Exhibit Z-c at Slide 38 (defining supportive measures as measures designed to "restore or preserve equal access to the education program or activity" and protect safety). Simpson's express acknowledgment that Plaintiff's allegations "warrant further information gathering and review" constitutes actual knowledge that the allegations, if true, could constitute sexual harassment under the University's own policies. Exhibit B-5.

**B. Duquesne University's Response Was Clearly Unreasonable and Thus Deliberately Indifferent**

A school is deliberately indifferent where its "response to the harassment or lack thereof is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. "Neither does [the deliberate indifference standard] require courts to conclude that minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment are not clearly unreasonable as a matter of law." *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc) (Key Passage 2). The Third Circuit has expressly adopted a straightforward pleading standard: "to state a claim under Title IX, the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex." *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203, 209 (3d Cir. 2020). There is "no need to superimpose doctrinal tests on the statute." *Id.*; *see also Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022) (failure to forward reports to Title IX Coordinator as required by policy can constitute clearly unreasonable response).

**1. Duquesne University Imposed Unlawful Conditions on Access to Supportive Measures**

The Title IX Coordinator is responsible for "coordinating the effective implementation of supportive measures," which must be "offered as appropriate and as reasonably available, without fee or charge, designed to restore or preserve access." Exhibit Z-c at Slide 38. Supportive measures are "available at any time after a report of sexual harassment," are "non-disciplinary," and "individualized." *Id.* at Slides 38-39.

Despite actual knowledge, Simpson demanded that Plaintiff participate in a meeting before she would consider providing supportive measures. Exhibit B-5 ("it is necessary for representatives of these offices to meet with you"). This condition was unlawful. The regulatory framework does not permit a Title IX Coordinator to condition the provision of supportive measures—including basic safety measures like mutual no-contact orders, housing changes, and Public Safety alerts—on a meeting. *See* Exhibit Z-c at Slide 39 (supportive measures are available at any time). Indeed, the very purpose of supportive measures is to ensure safety while the process unfolds, not after the victim submits to a screening meeting.

Plaintiff made explicit that he "cannot delay resolution any further," and that his "bank account balance fell to $16.65 (July 25) and negative $2.55 (August 12), while he lacked permanent housing." Undisputed Material Fact ¶ 11. He had already been displaced from his home since June 30. *Id.* at ¶ 1. His physician warned that "to share a living space with this individual would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." Exhibit F (August 13, 2025 letter). In the face of this documented emergency, conditioning supportive measures on a meeting was not merely bureaucratic; it was "clearly unreasonable." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648.

**2. Duquesne University Performed Zero of Its Mandatory Obligations Under § 106.44(a)**

Under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a), upon receiving a report of sexual harassment, the Title IX Coordinator must: (1) promptly contact the complainant to discuss supportive measures; (2) consider the complainant's wishes; (3) inform the complainant of the availability of supportive measures; (4) explain the process for filing a formal complaint; and (5) respond in a manner that is not deliberately indifferent.

Simpson performed none of these obligations. Undisputed Material Fact ¶ 10. She did not promptly contact Plaintiff to discuss supportive measures—she proposed a meeting days later. She did not consider Plaintiff's wishes—she ignored his explicit refusal to meet and his demand for written action. She did not inform Plaintiff of the availability of supportive measures—she conditioned their provision on a meeting. She did not explain the process for filing a formal complaint—she instead reversed jurisdictional determination in a single email that omitted any process explanation. Exhibit B-6. This wholesale failure to comply with the governing regulation is, by definition, "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648.

**3. Duquesne University Reversed Its Jurisdictional Determination Based on Protected Activity**

On August 13, 2025, at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Plaintiff's allegations warranted "further information gathering and review" and offered to discuss supportive measures. Exhibit B-5. Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM that same day, Plaintiff sent three emails containing no new facts about the underlying complaint. Undisputed Material Fact ¶ 5. These emails consisted entirely of protected activity: citing Title IX regulations, demanding written action, and restoring the Office for Civil Rights to the CC line. Exhibit B-5A (citing 34 C.F.R. §§ 106.30, 106.44, 106.71). Retaliation is prohibited under 34 C.F.R. § 106.71. *Id.*

Nineteen hours later—on August 14, 2025, at 9:42 AM—Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination. Exhibit B-6. She now stated that "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested." *Id.* She determined that the conduct "does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true." *Id.*

No new factual information about the underlying complaint entered the record between Simpson's acknowledgment (B-5) and her reversal (B-6). Undisputed Material Fact ¶ 7. The only intervening events were Plaintiff's protected activity. *Id.* A reasonable jury could infer that Simpson was not making a good-faith jurisdictional assessment but was instead retaliating against Plaintiff for exercising his rights under the Title IX regulatory framework. This is the very definition of "clearly unreasonable." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648; *Hall*, No. 19-3275 (finding deliberate indifference where officials "filed away" reports and failed to act).

**4. Duquesne University's Complete Failure to Act for 45 Days Was Clearly Unreasonable**

Between June 30, 2025 and August 14, 2025—a period of 45 days—no supportive measures were offered or provided to Plaintiff through the Title IX process. Undisputed Material Fact ¶ 8. Defendants' communications consisted exclusively of requests to schedule meetings, directives to "engage with" offices, and jurisdictional disclaimers. *Id.* Plaintiff's bank account fell to $16.65 and then negative $2.55 while he lacked permanent housing. *Id.* at ¶ 11.

*McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024), is distinguishable. There, the court affirmed summary judgment for the university where the school provided a no-contact directive, academic and mental health accommodations, a five-month outside investigation, and a semester of probation—all while maintaining "ongoing communication and support." *Id.* (Key Passage 1). Here, Duquesne University provided nothing. No no-contact order was issued. No housing was provided. No Public Safety alert was placed. No academic accommodations were made. No process was initiated. The *McAvoy* court's affirmance was premised on the university's *ongoing provision of support*, not on the mere absence of punishment for delay. The present case is the polar opposite: 45 days of complete inaction while a documented safety emergency persisted.

As the *Foster* dissent recognized—a position the majority did not reject as a matter of law—"minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment are not clearly unreasonable as a matter of law." *Foster* (Key Passage 2). Here, there were *no* efforts at all. A complete failure to respond for 45 days to known sexual harassment is the archetype of "clearly unreasonable" conduct. *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648.

**C. The Harassment Was Severe, Pervasive, and Objectively Offensive, and Denied Plaintiff Equal Educational Access**

For Title IX liability, the harassment must be "so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 650.

Plaintiff's harasser physically cornered him in the kitchen, got within inches of his face, yelled at him, followed him through the apartment, verbally confirmed she was following him, pursued him up the stairs toward his bedroom, and stated "I'm coming up"—forcing Plaintiff to lock himself in his room for safety. Exhibit B-0. This was not a single incident of unpleasantness; it was a physical pursuit and confrontation that created an objectively reasonable fear of imminent physical harm. *Id.* Plaintiff's physician confirmed this fear was reasonable, stating: "I believe its reasonable for him to have this fear" and recommending law enforcement involvement. Exhibit F (August 13, 2025 letter).

The harassment denied Plaintiff equal educational access. He was forced to flee his home on July 1. Undisputed Material Fact ¶ 1. His bank account fell to $16.65 (July 25) and negative $2.55 (August 12). *Id.* at ¶ 11. His physician warned that the situation would "cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." Exhibit F (August 13, 2025 letter). A second medical letter confirmed that "homelessness since June 30th which has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions," and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." *Id.* (October 1, 2025 letter). Plaintiff's department chair assured him "it won't be a problem to ensure you don't share an office," Exhibit R, confirming that the harassment required separation to preserve Plaintiff's ability to participate in the academic program.

This case is controlled by *Davis*, which held that a student's denial of access to educational opportunities is actionable where the harassment "so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive" that it effectively excludes the victim from school. 526 U.S. at 650. Plaintiff was displaced from his housing, financially devastated, medically impaired, and unable to safely participate in academic activities for 45 days without any institutional response.

**D. Duquesne University Exercises Substantial Control Over the Harasser and the Context**

The *Hall* court held that "it is an intentional violation of Title IX's terms for a funding recipient to act with deliberate indifference to known sexual harassment where the recipient exercises substantial control over the context in which the harassment occurs and the harasser, even if they are a third party." *Hall*, No. 19-3275 (Key Passage 1). Here, the harasser was a fellow graduate student at Duquesne University, subject to the University's disciplinary authority, code of conduct, and Title IX policies. The harassment occurred in University-provided housing—a program or activity of the University. The University's own Title IX policy, reproduced at Exhibit P, expressly covers student-on-student harassment and provides mechanisms for housing changes, no-contact orders, and emergency removals. Duquesne University exercises substantial control over its graduate students, its housing, and the safety of its campus. This element is satisfied.

**CONCLUSION**

Duquesne University had actual knowledge of severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive sexual harassment against Plaintiff David Mullins. For 45 days, it did nothing. It conditioned supportive measures on a meeting, reversed its jurisdictional determination based on Plaintiff's protected activity, performed zero of its mandatory obligations under § 106.44(a), and failed to provide any safety measures, housing assistance, or academic accommodations while Plaintiff was displaced, financially destitute, and medically at risk. This response—or lack thereof—was "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. Plaintiff has satisfied all elements of Count I for deliberate indifference under Title IX.

**ARGUMENT**

Plaintiff David Mullins is entitled to relief under Title IX because Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit (“Duquesne”) had actual knowledge of severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive sexual harassment, yet responded in a manner that was clearly unreasonable—effectively ignoring Plaintiff’s pleas for safety and supportive measures for 45 days. The undisputed facts, when measured against the controlling case law from the Supreme Court and the Third Circuit, establish each element of deliberate indifference.

**I. D uquesne Had Actual Knowledge of Sexual Harassment Through Officials with Authority to Institute Corrective Measures**

Actual knowledge is established when notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment is provided to “a recipient’s Title IX Coordinator; or any official of the recipient who has authority to institute corrective measures on behalf of the recipient.” *See* Exhibit Z-e, Slide 30 (PCHE Training, defining “Actual Knowledge”). Duquesne’s own training materials identify the Dean of Students, Title IX Coordinator, Provost, and Vice President for Student Affairs as officials with such authority. *Id.* at Slide 32.

Here, Plaintiff first reported the harassment to Dean Wasilko on June 30, 2025, describing a physical pursuit and confrontation by his roommate, and requesting emergency housing. (Exhibit B-0.) Dean Wasilko, as Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students, possessed authority to institute corrective measures. On July 24, 2025, Plaintiff escalated the matter directly to Title IX Coordinator Alicia Simpson, who acknowledged receipt and stated that her “primary roles is to help ensure your health and safety.” (Exhibit B-1.) By that date, both the Dean of Students and the Title IX Coordinator had actual knowledge of the harassment.

On August 13, 2025, Plaintiff sent an Emergency Removal Request to Simpson, attaching sworn declarations and a physician’s letter documenting a reasonable fear of physical harm. (Exhibit F; SAC ¶¶ 168-172.) Simpson responded at 2:02 PM that same day, explicitly stating: “Your concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review.” (Exhibit B-5.) Simpson’s acknowledgment itself constitutes actual knowledge that harassment allegations existed. *See* Exhibit Z-e, Slide 30 (“actual knowledge means notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment”). Simpson is the Title IX Coordinator—the primary official charged with receiving and acting on such reports. *See* Exhibit P (Appendix C, listing Simpson as Title IX Coordinator).

Thus, from June 30, 2025 onward, Duquesne had actual knowledge of actionable sexual harassment through multiple officials with authority to act.

**II. The Harassment Was Severe, Pervasive, and Objectively Offensive**

Under *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629, 650-51 (1999), a plaintiff must show harassment that is “so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school.” Here, Plaintiff reported that his roommate: (1) yelled at him and got within inches of his face; (2) aggressively followed him through the apartment and up the stairs, confirming she was following him; (3) said “I’m coming up” as he fled to his room; (4) locked him out of the apartment on a prior occasion; and (5) engaged in a pattern of manipulation and retaliation after Plaintiff sought help from the landlord. (Exhibit D, Signal Transcript & Timeline; Exhibit B-0.)

The conduct was not a single isolated incident. The June 30 confrontation was preceded by a escalating pattern of harassment over several weeks, including the lockout on June 27 and refusal to allow Plaintiff to control the temperature in his own living space. *Id.* After Plaintiff moved out on July 1, the harasser continued to follow and harass him. (Exhibit S, Mullins email describing “predator vibes” and being followed while moving out.) The medical provider, Treating Physician, confirmed that Plaintiff’s fear was reasonable and that sharing a living space with the harasser would “cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning.” (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 1.) Such conduct—physical pursuit, verbal confrontation, and ongoing intimidation—is objectively offensive and would cause a reasonable student to fear for their safety.

**III. Duquesne’s Response Was Clearly Unreasonable Under the Known Circumstances**

Under *Davis*, a school is liable where its response to known harassment “is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances.” 526 U.S. at 648. The Third Circuit has repeatedly applied this standard, holding that a “funding recipient may be liable for deliberate indifference to known sexual harassment where the recipient exercises substantial control over the context and the harasser.” *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275, slip op. at 10 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022). In *Hall*, the Third Circuit found that a university’s failure to forward a sexual misconduct report to the Title IX Coordinator and failure to contact the victim constituted a “clearly unreasonable” response. *Id.*

Duquesne’s response—or lack thereof—during the critical 45-day period from June 30 to August 14, 2025, was far more egregious than the conduct in *Hall*. The record shows:

- **No supportive measures were ever offered or provided.** 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a) requires a school to promptly contact a complainant to discuss supportive measures, consider the complainant’s wishes, inform the complainant of available measures, and explain the process for filing a formal complaint. Simpson performed *zero* of these obligations. (Undisputed Fact 10.) Although Simpson stated in her August 13 email that she could “discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures,” she conditioned any action on a meeting—despite Plaintiff’s clear written refusal to meet. (Exhibit B-5; Exhibit B-5A.)

- **The only substantive action was to reverse jurisdiction without new facts.** On August 13, Simpson acknowledged that the allegations “warrant further information gathering and review.” (Exhibit B-5.) Nineteen hours later, after receiving three emails from Plaintiff that contained *no new facts* about the underlying complaint but merely invoked his rights under § 106.71, Simpson reversed her preliminary determination and declared that the Title IX Office “does not have jurisdiction” and “cannot take action or provide assistance as requested.” (Exhibit B-6.) There were no intervening facts. The only change was Plaintiff’s refusal to meet and his insistence on written communications. Such a rapid and unexplained about-face—especially after acknowledging that the allegations warranted review—is the epitome of a clearly unreasonable response. *Cf. Hall*, slip op. at 10 (failure to forward report and failure to contact victim was clearly unreasonable).

- **Simpson failed to follow mandatory Title IX procedures.** She did not offer Plaintiff appeal rights after the dismissal, as required by § 106.45(b)(8). She did not send simultaneous written notice to the respondent, as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii). (Undisputed Fact 9.) These procedural failures demonstrate that Duquesne’s response was not merely delayed but deliberately dismissive.

- **The school did nothing in the face of escalating risk.** While Plaintiff became homeless, his bank account fell to $16.65 and then negative $2.55, and his doctor warned of worsening medical conditions, Duquesne’s only communications were demands to meet, referrals to other offices, and jurisdictional disclaimers. (Exhibits B-0 through B-6; Undisputed Fact 11; Exhibit F.)

The Third Circuit’s decision in *McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024), is distinguishable. In *McAvoy*, the university actually issued a no-contact directive, provided academic and mental health accommodations, conducted a five-month investigation, and imposed a semester of probation. Here, Duquesne provided *nothing*. As the *McAvoy* court implicitly recognized, a “clearly unreasonable” response includes doing nothing at all. Moreover, the *Foster* dissent—adopted by the Third Circuit in *Hall*—makes clear that “minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment” can be clearly unreasonable as a matter of law. *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc) (dissent).

Duquesne’s response was not merely slow; it was a deliberate refusal to act. Simpson had the authority to issue a no-contact order or emergency removal without a meeting. See Exhibit Z-c, Slide 40 (supportive measures include “mutual no contact orders” and can be implemented “at any time after a report”). She chose not to. This is the very definition of deliberate indifference.

**IV. The Harassment and Duquesne’s Response Denied Plaintiff Equal Access to Educational Opportunities**

Under *Davis*, the harassment must be “so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school.” 526 U.S. at 650. Here, Plaintiff’s academic functioning was directly impaired. His physician stated that his homelessness and fear of his abuser “likely worsens his mental health conditions,” and that “worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education.” (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2.) Plaintiff was forced to flee his home, incurred substantial financial costs, and could not participate in a planned academic trip to Germany. (Exhibit S.) He was unable to safely share office or classroom space with the harasser, and his department chair confirmed that steps would be taken to avoid contact—but the Title IX office itself refused to ensure safety. (Exhibit R.)

Moreover, Duquesne’s failure to provide supportive measures left Plaintiff without housing or financial stability, directly impeding his ability to focus on his doctoral studies. A student who is homeless, fearful, and medically compromised is denied equal access to education. The Third Circuit, applying *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203 (3d Cir. 2020), requires only that “the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex.” *Id.* at 209. Here, the inference is overwhelming: Duquesne treated Plaintiff’s safety concerns as an administrative inconvenience, dismissed them without investigation, and left him to fend for himself—all because he reported sex-based harassment.

**Conclusion**

Duquesne had actual knowledge of severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive harassment. Its response—a 45-day silence punctuated by demands for meetings, a jurisdictional reversal based on no new facts, and a complete refusal to provide supportive measures—was clearly unreasonable and deliberately indifferent. Plaintiff has satisfied each element of a Title IX deliberate indifference claim. This Court should deny any motion for summary judgment and permit the claim to proceed to trial.

A Title IX deliberate indifference claim requires that a funding recipient have “actual knowledge” of sexual harassment. *Davis v. Monroe County Bd. of Educ.*, 526 U.S. 629, 650 (1999). Actual knowledge exists when notice is provided to “an official of the recipient who has authority to institute corrective measures on behalf of the recipient.” *Id.* at 642; *see also* Exhibit Z-e at Slide 30 (defining actual knowledge under 34 C.F.R. § 106.30). Duquesne’s own training materials confirm that responsible employees with such authority include Deans of Students and Title IX Coordinators. *Id.* at Slides 31–32.

Simpson also acknowledged that Plaintiff had reported being “displaced from your housing” as a result. *Id.*

Notice to either official satisfies the actual knowledge requirement. Under *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. The “clearly unreasonable” standard does not require courts to conclude that “minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment are not clearly unreasonable as a matter of law.” *Foster*, slip op. at *10 (emphasis in original).
