## ARGUMENT

Plaintiff David Mullins is entitled to judgment on Count I because Defendant Duquesne University acted with deliberate indifference to known, severe, and pervasive sexual harassment, thereby denying Plaintiff equal access to educational opportunities in violation of Title IX, 20 U.S.C. § 1681. The undisputed material facts establish each element of a Title IX deliberate indifference claim as articulated by the Supreme Court in *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629 (1999), and as applied by the Third Circuit.

### I. The University Had Actual Knowledge of Sexual Harassment Through Officials with Authority to Institute Corrective Measures

Actual knowledge exists when notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment is provided to a recipient's Title IX Coordinator or any official with authority to institute corrective measures on behalf of the recipient. *See* Exhibit Z-e at Slide 30 (defining actual knowledge under 34 C.F.R. § 106.30). "This could include: Provost, VP for Student Affairs, Dean of Students, HR Director, etc." *Id.* at Slide 32.

Here, actual knowledge was established no later than June 30, 2025, when Plaintiff reported the physical confrontation, pursuit, and threat to Dean Wasilko, the Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students. Exhibit B-0. Dean Wasilko is an official with "authority to institute corrective measures" under any reasonable definition. *See* Exhibit Z-e at Slide 32. Moreover, Title IX Coordinator Alicia Simpson received Plaintiff's detailed report on July 24, 2025, and acknowledged that her office had been "notified" and that she was "in receipt of your email where you allege that there has been noncompliance with Title IX." Exhibit B-1. Simpson further acknowledged that her "primary roles" included ensuring Plaintiff's "health and safety." *Id.* Actual knowledge is therefore indisputable.

### II. The University's Response Was Clearly Unreasonable and Constituted Deliberate Indifference

A school is deliberately indifferent where its "response to sexual harassment is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. The standard does not require courts to excuse "minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond." *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc) (Key Passage 2). Here, the University's response falls far below any reasonable standard.

**A. No Supportive Measures Were Offered for 45 Days Despite Repeated Requests.**

Between June 30, 2025, and August 14, 2025—a period of 45 days—the University offered Plaintiff no supportive measures whatsoever. This is not a case where the school adopted escalating measures or maintained ongoing communication and support. *Cf. McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024) (affirming summary judgment where school issued no-contact directive, provided academic and mental health accommodations, and conducted a five-month investigation). Rather, the University's communications consisted exclusively of requests to schedule meetings, directives to "engage with" offices, and jurisdictional disclaimers. Exhibits B-0 through B-6.

The Title IX Coordinator's mandatory obligations under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a) are clear: she must promptly contact the complainant to discuss supportive measures, consider the complainant's wishes, inform the complainant of the availability of supportive measures, and explain the process for filing a formal complaint. Exhibit Z-b at Slide 64 (required response includes "explaining and providing supportive measures"). Simpson performed zero of these obligations. Even when Plaintiff explicitly requested emergency removal and supportive measures on August 13, 2025, citing the specific regulatory framework, Exhibit B-5A, Simpson's response was not to implement measures but to demand a meeting. Exhibit B-5. She then reversed her preliminary jurisdictional determination just nineteen hours later, citing no new factual information about the underlying complaint, and disclaimed all responsibility. Exhibit B-6. The only intervening events were Plaintiff's three emails consisting entirely of protected activity under 34 C.F.R. § 106.71. Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C. A response that conditions supportive measures on meetings and then withdraws jurisdiction after the complainant engages in protected activity is the epitome of "clearly unreasonable." *See Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022) (Key Passage 1) (failure to forward reports and failure to contact victim can constitute clearly unreasonable response).

**B. Simpson's Jurisdictional Reversal Was Baseless and Procedurally Deficient.**

Simpson's August 14 determination that the conduct "does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true," Exhibit B-6, is indefensible. The conduct reported included physical pursuit, yelling, following, locking Plaintiff out of his apartment, and a verbal confrontation that caused Plaintiff to flee his home. Exhibits B-0, D. Such conduct falls squarely within the definition of sexual harassment because it was sex-based and created a hostile environment. The University's own training materials define supportive measures as "non-disciplinary, non-punitive, individualized" and "available at any time after a report of sexual harassment." Exhibit Z-c at Slides 38-39. Simpson's blanket refusal to provide any assistance—including a no-contact order, housing modifications, or safety planning—violated the University's duty to respond promptly and equitably.

Furthermore, Simpson failed to comply with mandatory procedural requirements. She did not offer Plaintiff appeal rights following the dismissal, as required by § 106.45(b)(8). She did not send simultaneous written notice to the respondent, as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii). Exhibit B-6. These failures, combined with the substantive refusal to act, demonstrate a response that is "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648.

**C. The University's Conduct Is Not Saved by Its Offers to Meet.**

The University's insistence on meetings as a precondition to providing supportive measures is itself unreasonable. Supportive measures are administrative directives that can be implemented immediately—"email orders to Public Safety/Registrar/Access Control; written notice from Title IX." Exhibit B-5A. The Title IX Coordinator's duty to "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures," Exhibit B-5, is not a license to delay or deny those measures. *See* Exhibit Z-c at Slide 39 (supportive measures are "provided at no cost" and "available at any time after a report"). Conditioning safety measures on a meeting with the complainant who has already provided sworn declarations and a physician's letter, Exhibit F, is comparable to the conduct condemned in *Hall v. Millersville*, where the university "filed away" a report and failed to contact the victim. *Hall*, Key Passage 2. Here, Simpson acknowledged that Plaintiff's "concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review," Exhibit B-5, but then did nothing—no contact, no investigation, no interim safety measures—before reversing course entirely.

### III. The Harassment Was Severe, Pervasive, and Objectively Offensive

To establish deliberate indifference under Title IX, a plaintiff must show that the harassment was "so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 650. Plaintiff's evidence satisfies this standard.

The harassment began with a pattern of manipulation regarding air conditioning, escalated to a lockout, and culminated in a physical confrontation where the harasser "approached me in the kitchen; when I calmly stated that I was not interested in talking, she began yelling at me, got within inches of my face, and aggressively followed me through the apartment. . . . She pursued me up the stairs toward my room, saying 'I'm coming up,' and only stopped just short of entering." Exhibit B-0. Plaintiff was forced to lock himself in his room. *Id.* The harasser had previously expressed enthusiasm for firearm access, Exhibit R, and Plaintiff reasonably feared for his safety. He fled his home, became homeless, and his bank account balance fell to $16.65 and then negative $2.55. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 11. His physician attested that sharing a living space with the harasser "would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." Exhibit F (August 13 letter). The harassment was objectively severe; no reasonable person would discount the physical pursuit and threat of invasion of a private room.

### IV. The University's Deliberate Indifference Denied Plaintiff Equal Educational Access

A school's deliberate indifference "must cause students to undergo harassment or make them liable or vulnerable to it" and must "subject" the student to discrimination. *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 644-45. Here, the University's failure to act directly caused Plaintiff's continued deprivation. Because of the harassment and the University's refusal to provide supportive measures, Plaintiff was forced to live in unstable housing, suffered severe financial distress, and experienced worsening mental health that negatively impacted his graduate education. Exhibit F (October 1 letter: "Worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education"). The University's inaction effectively ratified the harassment and left Plaintiff without protection, denying him equal access to the educational programs and activities at Duquesne.

### V. The University Exercised Substantial Control Over the Context and the Harasser

*Davis* requires that the recipient exercise "substantial control over both the harasser and the context in which the known harassment occurs." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 645. The harasser was a fellow graduate student at Duquesne, subject to the University's disciplinary authority. The harassment occurred in University-affiliated housing and in a context where the University exercised control through its student conduct code, housing policies, and Title IX procedures. See Exhibit S (Plaintiff identifying multiple student conduct violations that the University refused to enforce). The Third Circuit has held that a university exercises substantial control over a third-party harasser where the university's "Title IX Policy also required that victims of domestic or dating violence on campus be contacted by someone at [the university]." *Hall*, Key Passage 3. Here, Duquesne's own policies required a prompt and equitable response. The University's failure to act falls squarely within the scope of conduct that *Davis* and *Hall* hold actionable.

### VI. The University's Reliance on *McAvoy* and *Foster* Is Misplaced

In *McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, the Third Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the university where the school's response included a no-contact directive, academic accommodations, a five-month outside investigation, and a semester of probation. No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024) (Key Passage 1). The court held that "delays alone do not establish deliberate indifference where school maintained ongoing communication and support." *Id.* Here, the University provided no support at all—no no-contact order, no housing assistance, no safety planning, and no investigation. The only "communication" consisted of demands for meetings and jurisdictional disclaimers. *McAvoy* is therefore inapposite.

Similarly, *Foster v. Board of Regents* stands for the proposition that a school's response may be clearly unreasonable when it "knew its initial response was ineffective" and failed to take adequate subsequent action. *Foster*, Key Passage 3. Here, the University's initial response was nonexistent. It never even began a response that could be judged ineffective; it simply refused to act. The *Foster* majority's holding that genuine disputes may preclude summary judgment is not a shield for total inaction.

### CONCLUSION

For 45 days, Duquesne University had actual knowledge that Plaintiff was being sexually harassed by a fellow graduate student. For 45 days, the University did nothing—no supportive measures, no safety planning, no investigation, no contact with the harasser. When Plaintiff finally demanded action under the governing regulations, the Title IX Coordinator acknowledged that his concerns warranted review but then reversed jurisdiction hours later, citing no new facts and violating multiple procedural requirements. This conduct is "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances," *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648, and constitutes deliberate indifference as a matter of law.

Plaintiff respectfully requests that the Court find Defendant liable on Count I for deliberate indifference to sexual harassment under Title IX.

**ARGUMENT**

Plaintiff David Mullins is entitled to judgment on Count I because the undisputed record establishes that Duquesne University (“Duquesne”) had actual knowledge of severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive sexual harassment; responded in a manner that was clearly unreasonable under the circumstances; and thereby denied Mullins equal access to educational opportunities. Each element is satisfied.

**I. Duquesne Had Actual Knowledge of Sexual Harassment Through Officials with Authority to Institute Corrective Measures**

Actual knowledge arises when notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment is provided to a recipient’s Title IX Coordinator or any official with authority to institute corrective measures. *See Davis v. Monroe County Bd. of Educ.*, 526 U.S. 629, 650 (1999) (actual knowledge triggers duty). The record here demonstrates multiple avenues of actual knowledge.

On June 30, 2025, Mullins reported to Dean Wasilko that his roommate “got within inches of my face, and aggressively followed me through the apartment,” “pursued me up the stairs toward my room,” and that Mullins “no longer feel[s] safe sharing a living space.” (Ex. B-0). Dean Wasilko is the Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students—an official with authority to institute corrective measures, including emergency housing and conduct referrals. (Ex. B-0). Under the definition adopted in Duquesne’s own Title IX training, “actual knowledge” includes notice to “any official of the recipient who has authority to institute corrective measures on behalf of the recipient.” (Ex. Z-e, Slide 30). Dean Wasilko’s receipt of this report conferred actual knowledge on Duquesne.

Moreover, on July 24, 2025, Mullins sent a formal escalation to Title IX Coordinator Alicia Simpson, her supervisor, the university president, and legal affairs. (Ex. B-1). Simpson acknowledged receipt and stated that “[m]y office is notified when there are reported incidents of alleged sexual harassment or sexual misconduct.” (Ex. B-1). Simpson, as Title IX Coordinator, is the “official of the recipient who has authority to institute corrective measures” *par excellence*. (Ex. Z-e, Slide 30; *see* 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a)). From July 24 onward, Simpson—and thus Duquesne—had undisputed actual knowledge of the alleged harassment.

Finally, on August 13, 2025, Mullins submitted an Emergency Removal Request attaching sworn declarations and a physician’s letter documenting safety concerns. (Ex. F; Ex. B-5). Simpson again acknowledged the report and conceded that “your concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review.” (Ex. B-5). Actual knowledge was continuous and unequivocal.

**II. Duquesne’s Response Was Clearly Unreasonable and Thus Deliberately Indifferent**

Deliberate indifference exists where a funding recipient’s response to known sexual harassment “is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances.” *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. The Third Circuit has held that “[f]ailure to forward sexual misconduct reports to Title IX Coordinator as required by policy can constitute clearly unreasonable response.” *Hall v. Millersville Univ.*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022). And even where a school takes *some* action, it may be deliberately indifferent if its initial response was ineffective and its subsequent actions were not reasonable. *See Foster v. Bd. of Regents of Univ. of Mich.*, No. 19-1314 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc) (minority and majority both agree that “minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts” can be clearly unreasonable).

Duquesne’s response fails at every turn.

**A. Duquesne Never Provided Any Supportive Measures Despite a 45-Day Period of Known Risk**

Between June 30, 2025 and August 14, 2025—a period of 45 days—Duquesne offered Mullins *zero* supportive measures. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 8). The Title IX Coordinator’s mandatory obligations under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a) include promptly contacting the complainant to discuss supportive measures, considering the complainant’s wishes, informing the complainant of the availability of supportive measures, and explaining the process for filing a formal complaint. Simpson performed none of these duties. (¶ 10). Instead, Duquesne’s “response” consisted entirely of requests that Mullins schedule meetings, combined with jurisdictional disclaimers and referrals to other offices. (Exs. B-0, B-1, B-2, B-5, B-6).

The importance of supportive measures cannot be overstated. Duquesne’s own Title IX training defines supportive measures as “designed to restore or preserve equal access to the education program or activity” and states that they are “available at any time after a report of sexual harassment,” are “non-disciplinary, non-punitive, individualized, and provided at no cost.” (Ex. Z-c, Slides 38-40). The training emphasizes that the Title IX Coordinator is “responsible for coordinating the effective implementation of supportive measures.” (Ex. Z-c, Slide 38). Simpson never coordinated any measures. She did not issue a no-contact order, did not adjust housing or class schedules, and did not even offer counseling referrals until Mullins’ urgent August 13 request—and then only conditioned such discussion on a meeting. (Ex. B-5). This is the antithesis of a “prompt” response. *See Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648 (response must not be clearly unreasonable).

**B. Duquesne Conditioned All Assistance on a Meeting, Despite Knowing of Mullins’ Trauma and Safety Fears**

Simpson’s August 13 email stated: “it is necessary for representatives of these offices to meet with you to gather additional information. Additionally, during our meeting, we can discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures.” (Ex. B-5). Duquesne’s consistent pattern—from Wasilko on June 30 through Simpson on August 13—was to demand an in-person or Zoom meeting before providing any support. But the Title IX regulations do not permit a recipient to condition the provision of supportive measures on the complainant’s willingness to meet. Supportive measures are “available at any time after a report of sexual harassment,” (Ex. Z-c, Slide 39), and the Title IX Coordinator must “promptly contact the complainant to *discuss* the availability of supportive measures”—not demand a meeting as a prerequisite. *See* 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a). Moreover, Mullins had repeatedly made his preference for written communications clear. (See Exs. B-5A, B-5B, B-5C). Conditioning safety-related support on a face-to-face meeting when the complainant has stated he feels unsafe and refuses to meet is the definition of “clearly unreasonable.”

**C. Duquesne Retaliated Against Mullins’ Protected Activity by Immediately Dismissing His Complaint**

On August 13, Mullins sent three emails (Exs. B-5A, B-5B, B-5C) that consisted entirely of protected activity: he refused the meeting, demanded written communications, restored OCR to the CC line, and cited §§ 106.44, 106.30, and 106.71. No new facts about the underlying harassment were provided. Yet nineteen hours later, on August 14, Simpson reversed her prior jurisdiction determination and stated: “the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested.” (Ex. B-6). The temporal proximity between protected activity and an adverse action—here, the complete withdrawal of any promised assistance—raises an inference of retaliation, which itself constitutes deliberate indifference. *See* 34 C.F.R. § 106.71 (retaliation prohibited). And Simpson’s dismissal violated multiple regulatory requirements: she failed to provide appeal rights (as required by § 106.45(b)(8)), failed to send simultaneous written notice to the respondent (as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii)), and made a jurisdictional determination without the investigation that her own email two weeks earlier had deemed necessary. (Exs. B-1, B-6). This abrupt about-face, taken without any new factual development, is clearly unreasonable.

**D. Duquesne’s Response Contrasts Sharply with the Cases Finding No Deliberate Indifference**

The cases in which universities avoided liability involved meaningful, escalating interventions. In *McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024), the university issued a no-contact directive, provided academic and mental health accommodations, conducted an outside investigation that found sexual assault, and imposed a semester of probation. Here, Duquesne offered nothing. In *Foster*, the university adopted escalating measures proportionate to the misconduct. Here, Duquesne never took a single measure. And unlike in *Hall*, where the university failed to forward a report but the Third Circuit reversed summary judgment for the university, the facts here are even more egregious: Duquesne did not merely fail to forward a report; it actively declined to act after repeated explicit requests for emergency assistance.

The conduct here more closely resembles the dissent in *Foster*—where the university “knew its initial response was ineffective” and failed to take reasonable subsequent action. *Foster*, No. 19-1314, slip op. at 3 (dissent). Duquesne knew by July 24 that Mullins had been displaced from his home, had a negative bank account, and feared for his safety. (Exs. B-0, B-1, F, G). Yet it persisted in demanding meetings and disclaiming jurisdiction.

**III. The Harassment Was Severe, Pervasive, and Objectively Offensive**

Under *Davis*, actionable harassment must be “so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school.” *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 651. The record demonstrates that Respondent’s conduct meets this standard.

On June 30, Respondent confronted Mullins in the kitchen, screamed at him, got “within inches of my face,” “aggressively followed me through the apartment,” pursued him up the stairs, and explicitly stated she was “coming up” to his room. (Ex. B-0). She only stopped when Mullins locked himself in his bedroom. (Ex. D). This physical pursuit and intimidation was accompanied by a pattern of earlier conduct: locking Mullins out of the apartment (Ex. D), manipulating the air conditioning to his detriment (Ex. D), and expressing enthusiasm for the Second Amendment in a context that Mullins reasonably interpreted as a threat (Ex. R). After Mullins moved out on July 1, Respondent continued to harass him as he attempted to leave. (Ex. S). The harassment was not an isolated incident; it was a course of escalating conduct that Mullins reported repeatedly.

Mullins’ physician, Treating Physician, confirmed that Mullins’ fear was reasonable, that sharing space with Respondent “would cause him a great deal of stress,” would “worsen his medical condition,” and “could impair his academic functioning.” (Ex. F). The harassment was objectively offensive by any measure: it involved physical pursuit, verbal abuse, and threats (whether explicit or implicit) that would intimidate a reasonable person.

**IV. The Harassment Denied Mullins Equal Access to Educational Opportunities**

Because of the harassment and Duquesne’s deliberate indifference, Mullins suffered concrete educational deprivation. He was forced to flee his home and became homeless; his bank account balance fell to $16.65 on July 25 and to negative $2.55 on August 12. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 11). He could not afford to continue his graduate studies. His physician opined that homelessness and proximity to the abuser “worsens his mental health conditions” and that “[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education.” (Ex. F). The harassment directly caused Mullins to lose housing, fall into financial distress, and experience a deterioration in mental health that impaired his academic performance. Duquesne’s failure to provide supportive measures—which could have included a no-contact order, housing modifications, or academic accommodations—directly contributed to this deprivation. *See Davis*, 526 U.S. at 650-51 (deprivation of access includes circumstances where harassment “undermines” a student’s ability to participate in educational activities).

**V. Duquesne Exercised Substantial Control Over Both the Harasser and the Context**

The Third Circuit has held that deliberate indifference liability attaches where “the recipient exercises substantial control over the context in which the harassment occurs and the harasser, even if they are a third party.” *Hall*, slip op. at 10. Here, Respondent was a fellow graduate student at Duquesne, sharing housing arranged through the university. (Ex. D). Duquesne had disciplinary authority over her, as reflected in its Student Code of Conduct. (Ex. S). The harassment occurred in university-affiliated housing and on campus. Duquesne therefore had substantial control over both the harasser and the context. Its failure to exercise that control—by not issuing any conduct charges, not imposing a no-contact order, and not removing the harasser from shared spaces—was clearly unreasonable.

**VI. The Facts Support a Plausible Inference of Sex Discrimination**

Under *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203 (3d Cir. 2020), a Title IX plaintiff need not “superimpose additional doctrinal tests on the statute”; it is sufficient that “the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex.” *Id.* at 209. Mullins’ allegations—that he reported sexual harassment, that the university knew about it, that the university’s response was deliberately indifferent, and that the university’s response caused him to lose access to educational opportunities—plainly support such an inference.

**CONCLUSION**

Duquesne had actual knowledge of severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive harassment. It responded with a forty-five-day pattern of inaction, conditional meeting demands, retaliatory jurisdictional dismissals, and no supportive measures whatsoever. Its response was clearly unreasonable under the known circumstances. Mullins was deprived of educational access as a result. For these reasons, the Court should enter judgment for Plaintiff on Count I.

**ARGUMENT**

**I. Legal Standard for Deliberate Indifference Under Title IX**

Title IX provides a private right of action for damages against federally-funded educational institutions that act with deliberate indifference to known acts of student-on-student sexual harassment. The seminal standard is governed by *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629 (1999), which established that a funding recipient may be held liable "only where the funding recipient acts with deliberate indifference to known acts of harassment in its programs or activities" (Key Passage 1). Critically, the Supreme Court held that liability attaches where "the harasser is under the school's disciplinary authority" (Key Passage 2), and the recipient's response is "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances" (Key Passage 3). The harassment must be "so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school" (Key Passage 4).

The Third Circuit, in *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203 (3d Cir. 2020), adopted a straightforward pleading standard: "to state a claim under Title IX, the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex" (Key Passage 2). The Third Circuit rejected superimposing "additional doctrinal tests on the [Title IX] statute" (Key Passage 1). This Court need only determine whether Duquesne University's actions, as reflected in the undisputed record, support a plausible inference of sex-based discrimination arising from its clearly unreasonable response.

In *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022), the Third Circuit confirmed that a federal funding recipient has "adequate notice under Title IX that it may face monetary liability for deliberate indifference to known sexual harassment perpetrated by a non-student where the recipient exercises substantial control over the context and the harasser." The *Hall* court further held that "failure to forward sexual misconduct reports to Title IX Coordinator as required by policy can constitute clearly unreasonable response" and that filing away a report without action—coupled with failing to contact the victim—satisfies the deliberate indifference standard (Key Passages 2, 3).

**II. Duquesne University Had Actual Knowledge of Sexual Harassment Through Officials with Authority to Act**

The record demonstrates that multiple Duquesne officials with authority to institute corrective measures received actual notice of the harassment Plaintiff David Mullins suffered. On June 30, 2025, Plaintiff reported to Dean Adam Wasilko—Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students—that his roommate, Respondent, had physically pursued him through their apartment, followed him up the stairs, and confirmed she was following him, leaving Plaintiff "feeling physically threatened and unsafe" (Exhibit B-0). Plaintiff specifically requested "any emergency housing options, protective campus measures, or relevant reporting mechanisms" (Exhibit B-0). Dean Wasilko, an official with authority to institute corrective measures as defined by 34 C.F.R. § 106.44 and the university's own training materials (Exhibit Z-e, Slide 32—identifying Dean of Students as having authority), responded by minimizing the threat, stating the incident "doesn't sound like a code of conduct violation" (Exhibit B-0).

Plaintiff's report was further escalated to Title IX Coordinator Alicia Simpson on July 24, 2025 (Exhibit B-1). Simpson acknowledged receipt and stated she was "here to provide you with support" (Exhibit B-1). On August 13, 2025, Plaintiff submitted an Emergency Removal Request with sworn declarations and a physician's letter documenting safety concerns (Exhibit F; Exhibit B-5A). Simpson responded on August 13, acknowledging that Plaintiff's "concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review" and offering to "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures" (Exhibit B-5). Simpson's use of the term "supportive measures" is significant—it is a term of art defined exclusively in 34 C.F.R. § 106.30, confirming her knowledge of the Title IX framework (Exhibit B-5). Under *Hall*, the Title IX Coordinator's actual knowledge triggered the university's duty to respond appropriately, not to file away or dismiss the report.

**III. Duquesne University's Response Was Clearly Unreasonable and Constituted Deliberate Indifference**

Duquesne University's response to Plaintiff's reports of sexual harassment was not merely insufficient—it was "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. The record establishes four independent grounds supporting a finding of deliberate indifference.

**A. Defendants Failed to Provide Any Supportive Measures for 45 Days**

Between June 30, 2025, when Plaintiff first reported the harassment and requested housing assistance, and August 14, 2025, when the Title IX Office disclaimed jurisdiction, Defendants provided zero supportive measures (Statement of Facts ¶ 8; Exhibits B-0 through B-6). This 45-day period included:
- Plaintiff's explicit plea for housing assistance (Exhibit B-0)
- Plaintiff's warning of homelessness sent to senior leadership on July 21, 2025 (Exhibit A)
- Dean Wasilko's formal housing denial on July 24, 2025, despite confirming available rooms (Exhibit G)
- Plaintiff's bank account falling to $16.65 and negative $2.55 while homeless (Statement of Facts ¶ 11)
- Multiple emails demanding supportive measures including no-contact orders, registrar separation, and campus safety escorts (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C)

The university's own Title IX training materials define supportive measures as "available at any time after a report of sexual harassment," are "non-disciplinary" and "non-punitive," and include "mutual no contact orders," "changes in housing," and "extensions of deadlines" (Exhibit Z-c, Slides 38-40). These measures are designed to "restore or preserve equal access to the education program or activity" (Exhibit Z-c, Slide 38). Duquesne's complete failure to provide any such measures for 45 days while Plaintiff descended into homelessness and financial ruin is the archetype of a "clearly unreasonable" response.

In *Hall*, the Third Circuit found deliberate indifference where university officials "did not forward Wiberg's report and did not discuss it with anyone until after Karlie's murder" (Key Passage 2) and where "Karlie was not contacted by someone at Millersville after Wiberg's report" (Key Passage 3). Here, the failures are even more egregious: Duquesne officials not only failed to contact Plaintiff with offers of support but actively denied housing despite confirming availability (Exhibit G), conditioned assistance on mandatory meetings (Exhibits B-2, B-3), and ultimately disclaimed jurisdiction entirely (Exhibit B-6).

**B. Defendants Reversed Their Jurisdictional Determination After Plaintiff Engaged in Protected Activity**

The 24-hour reversal of the Title IX Office's position on August 13-14, 2025, is particularly telling. On August 13 at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Plaintiff's allegations "warrant further information gathering and review" and offered to discuss "supportive measures" (Exhibit B-5). Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM on August 13, Plaintiff sent three emails that consisted entirely of protected activity: demanding written communications, restoring OCR to the CC line, and citing specific Title IX regulatory provisions (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C). No new factual information about the underlying complaint was provided in those emails (Statement of Facts ¶ 7). Yet at 9:42 AM on August 14—just nineteen hours later—Simpson reversed her position, stating "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested" (Exhibit B-6).

The timing of this reversal—immediately following Plaintiff's demand for compliance and restoration of OCR oversight—supports an inference of retaliatory motive. Under *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447 (3d Cir. 2006), "demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity" (Key Passage 1). The Third Circuit has held that "unusually suggestive" temporal proximity can establish a causal connection for retaliation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701 (3d Cir. 1989) (two-day proximity sufficient). Here, the 19-hour proximity between Plaintiff's protected activity and the adverse jurisdictional reversal is highly suggestive.

Moreover, Simpson performed zero of the five mandatory obligations under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a): she did not promptly contact Plaintiff to discuss supportive measures, did not consider Plaintiff's wishes, did not inform Plaintiff of the availability of supportive measures, and did not explain the process for filing a formal complaint (Statement of Facts ¶ 10). She also failed to offer appeal rights as required by § 106.45(b)(8) and failed to send simultaneous written notice to the respondent as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii) (Statement of Facts ¶ 9). These procedural failures demonstrate a pattern of disregard for federal Title IX requirements.

**C. Defendants' Response Failed the "Clearly Unreasonable" Standard Under Governing Precedent**

The Third Circuit's decision in *McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024), is distinguishable. In *McAvoy*, the court affirmed summary judgment for the university because the school's response—a no-contact directive, academic and mental health accommodations, a five-month outside investigation finding sexual assault, and a semester of probation—was not clearly unreasonable (Key Passage 1). Here, by stark contrast, Duquesne provided no supportive measures, no investigation, no no-contact directive, and no accommodations of any kind. The only "response" was a series of requests for meetings, jurisdictional disclaimers, and ultimately a jurisdictional reversal that left Plaintiff without any remedy.

The en banc Sixth Circuit's decision in *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020), is also instructive. The *Foster* majority affirmed summary judgment for the university where it "adopted escalating measures proportionate to the misconduct." But the dissent recognized that "minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment are not clearly unreasonable as a matter of law" (Key Passage 2). Here, Duquesne's efforts were not merely minimal or belated—they were nonexistent. The university did not even attempt to provide any of the supportive measures it was trained to offer (Exhibit Z-c).

**D. Plaintiff Suffered Severe and Pervasive Harassment That Deprived Him of Educational Access**

The harassment Plaintiff experienced was severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive. On June 30, 2025, Respondent confronted Plaintiff in the kitchen, "got within inches of my face, and aggressively followed me through the apartment" (Exhibit B-0). She confirmed she was following him when asked, stated "I'm coming up" to his room, and stopped only when he locked himself in his bedroom (Exhibit B-0). Plaintiff fled his home at substantial cost to protect his physical safety (Exhibit A). Medical documentation from Treating Physician confirms Plaintiff has a "reasonable fear" and that sharing a living space "would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning" (Exhibit F).

The harassment continued through the fall semester. On September 17, 2025, Respondent was "literally waiting outside" Plaintiff's philosophy seminar classroom (Exhibit C). External Faculty Witness, who has known Plaintiff for 19 years, attested that this encounter caused severe distress and required hours of recovery time, directly impeding Plaintiff's educational participation (Exhibit C, Supplemental Declaration ¶¶ 1-4). External Faculty Witness further opined that Plaintiff's "education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability and the conflict with his former roommate" (Exhibit C). The October 1 medical letter from Treating Physician confirms Plaintiff "reports homelessness since June 30th which has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions," and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education" (Exhibit F).

The harassment was not limited to the June 30 incident. Exhibit D establishes a pattern of escalating conduct dating to June 2025, including Respondent locking Plaintiff out of the apartment, manipulating the A/C to Plaintiff's detriment while changing it for her own comfort, and ultimately the physical confrontation on June 30 (Exhibit D). During the move-out on July 1, Respondent harassed Plaintiff as he attempted to leave, repeatedly demanding to know where he was going (Exhibit D).

**IV. Duquesne University's Deliberate Indifference Caused Denial of Equal Educational Access**

Plaintiff's educational access was severely compromised. He was forced to flee his home and became homeless for 90+ days (Exhibit A). His bank account balance fell to $16.65 on July 25 and negative $2.55 on August 12, 2025 (Statement of Facts ¶ 11). He was forced into "precarious couch-surfing arrangements" (Exhibit A). His ability to attend classes in person was foreclosed by the university's refusal to implement basic safety measures—a no-contact order, registrar separation, and campus safety escort—measures that are expressly listed as supportive measures in the university's own Title IX training materials (Exhibit Z-c, Slide 40).

The department chair, Dr. Selcer, imposed a three-week time limit on remote attendance, creating what Plaintiff accurately characterized as a "countdown clock designed to coerce compliance or force attrition" (Exhibit I-1). Selcer's final directive of September 3, 2025, warned that if Plaintiff did not "engage with" Title IX and Student Conduct meetings, he would lose remote access and be required to attend in person without safety measures (Exhibit J). This ultimatum put Plaintiff in an impossible position: attend in person without safety measures and risk encountering his harasser, or lose access to his education entirely.

When Plaintiff later obtained temporary Zoom access from Professor Faculty C on October 6, 2025 (Exhibit W), Selcer intervened, discussed the matter with Faculty C without Plaintiff's knowledge, and then informed Plaintiff that Faculty C "did not identify a compelling reason to continue it" (Exhibit V). The Zoom link was removed the same day Faculty C's authorization was overridden (Exhibit Q). This coordinated removal of Plaintiff's only means of educational access, following his repeated demands for Title IX compliance, constitutes a clear denial of educational opportunities.

**V. The Direct and Circumstantial Evidence Supports a Plausible Inference of Sex-Based Discrimination**

Under *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, this Court need only find that the facts, "if true, must support a plausible inference" of sex-based discrimination (Key Passage 2). The record supports such an inference through multiple avenues.

First, Plaintiff explicitly raised the issue of gender stereotyping. On July 23, 2025, Plaintiff asked Dean Wasilko: "I can't help but wonder whether your response would have been the same had the genders in this situation been reversed" (Exhibit B-0A). Dean Wasilko's minimization of the threat—dismissing physical pursuit and confrontation as "doesn't sound like a code of

**ARGUMENT**

**I. Duquesne University Had Actual Knowledge of Sexual Harassment Through Officials with Authority to Institute Corrective Measures**

The undisputed facts demonstrate that Duquesne University received actual notice of actionable sexual harassment through multiple officials with authority to act. On June 30, 2025, Plaintiff David Mullins reported to Dean Wasilko—the Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students—that a fellow graduate student had physically pursued him through his home, yelled at him within inches of his face, followed him up the stairs when he attempted to retreat, and confirmed she was following him while stating "I'm coming up" to his bedroom. (Exhibit B-0.) Dean Wasilko is precisely the type of official with "authority to institute corrective measures" that triggers institutional knowledge under Title IX. *See* Exhibit Z-e at Slide 32 (identifying "Dean of Students" as an official with actual knowledge).

The Third Circuit has unequivocally held that a federal funding recipient has adequate notice under Title IX "where the recipient exercises substantial control over the context in which the harassment occurs and the harasser, even if they are a third party." *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275, slip op. at *12 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022). Here, both the harasser (Respondent) and the context (Duquesne University housing and campus) were under Duquesne's substantial control.

Moreover, actual knowledge was independently established when Plaintiff sent a formal escalation to Title IX Coordinator Alicia Simpson on July 24, 2025, citing 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a) and demanding supportive measures. (SAC ¶¶ 168-172; Exhibit B-1.) Simpson herself acknowledged this report, stating she was "in receipt of your email where you allege that there has been noncompliance with Title IX regarding a situation with another student." (Exhibit B-1.) This constitutes undisputed actual knowledge by the Title IX Coordinator—the primary official charged with Title IX compliance. *See* Exhibit Z-e at Slide 30 ("Actual knowledge means notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment to: A recipient's Title IX Coordinator").

Plaintiff further submitted an Emergency Removal Request on August 13, 2025, citing § 106.44(c) and attaching sworn declarations and a physician's letter documenting his safety concerns. (Exhibit F; Exhibit B-5A.) Simpson responded on August 13 acknowledging that Plaintiff's "concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review." (Exhibit B-5.) This acknowledgment independently confirms actual knowledge.

Under *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203 (3d Cir. 2020), the Court held that "to state a claim under Title IX, the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex." *Id.* at 209. The undisputed facts here more than satisfy this straightforward pleading standard—Duquesne knew of the harassment through its highest-ranking student affairs and Title IX officials.

**II. Duquesne's Response Was Clearly Unreasonable and Constitutes Deliberate Indifference as a Matter of Law**

Under *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629 (1999), a funding recipient is liable for deliberate indifference where "the recipient's response to the harassment or lack thereof is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Id.* at 648. The Third Circuit has made clear that "neither does [the deliberate indifference standard] require courts to conclude that minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment are not clearly unreasonable as a matter of law." *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc) (dissent, though recognizing that minimal efforts can be clearly unreasonable).

The record establishes a pattern of conduct that is the antithesis of a reasonable response:

**A. Complete Failure to Provide Supportive Measures During 45 Critical Days**

Between June 30, 2025 and August 14, 2025—a period of 45 days—Defendants offered and provided no supportive measures whatsoever through the Title IX process. (Exhibits B-0 through B-6.) The undisputed facts confirm that "Defendants' communications consisted exclusively of requests to schedule meetings, directives to 'engage with' offices, and jurisdictional disclaimers." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 8.)

This stands in stark contrast to what Title IX requires. As Exhibit Z-c makes clear, supportive measures are "available at any time after a report of sexual harassment" and include "mutual no contact orders," "changes in housing," "work or class schedule modifications," and "extensions of deadlines." (Exhibit Z-c at Slides 39-40.) The Title IX Coordinator bears "responsibility for coordinating the effective implementation of supportive measures." (Exhibit Z-c at Slide 38.) Simpson performed "zero of the five mandatory obligations under § 106.44(a): she did not promptly contact Plaintiff to discuss supportive measures, did not consider Plaintiff's wishes, did not inform Plaintiff of the availability of supportive measures, and did not explain the process for filing a formal complaint." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 10; Exhibits B-5, B-6.)

In *Hall v. Millersville University*, the Third Circuit held that failure to forward sexual misconduct reports to the Title IX Coordinator as required by policy "can constitute clearly unreasonable response." *Hall*, slip op. at *13. Here, the failure was far more egregious: the Title IX Coordinator herself received the reports and still took no action.

**B. Retaliatory Jurisdiction Reversal After Protected Activity**

The most damning evidence of deliberate indifference is the 19-hour jurisdiction reversal that occurred between August 13 and August 14, 2025. On August 13 at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Plaintiff's concerns and offered to "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures." (Exhibit B-5.) Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM on August 13, Plaintiff sent three emails refusing the proposed meeting, demanding written communications, and citing §§ 106.44, 106.30, and 106.71. (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C.) Critically, "[n]o new factual information about the underlying complaint entered the record between B-5 and B-6. The only intervening events were Plaintiff's three emails consisting entirely of protected activity under § 106.71." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7.)

On August 14 at 9:42 AM—nineteen hours later—Simpson reversed her preliminary determination, stating "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested." (Exhibit B-6.) She further stated the conduct "does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true." (Exhibit B-6.) This reversal—with no new facts—can only be explained as retaliation for Plaintiff's exercise of his protected rights.

The Third Circuit has repeatedly held that retaliation for demanding Title IX compliance is actionable. *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447, 454 (3d Cir. 2006) ("Demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity"). Moreover, the close temporal proximity between Plaintiff's protected activity and Simpson's adverse jurisdiction determination establishes causation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701, 708 (3d Cir. 1989) (two-day temporal proximity between protected activity and adverse action sufficient to establish causation); *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259, 267 (3d Cir. 2007) ("Temporal proximity combined with contextual evidence of retaliatory animus is sufficient to establish causation").

The Third Circuit applies the Title VII retaliation framework to Title IX retaliation claims. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247 (3d Cir. 2017). Under this framework, at the prima facie stage, "the plaintiff need only show that the protected activity was the likely reason for the adverse action." *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017). Here, the evidence establishes that Simpson's jurisdiction reversal occurred only after Plaintiff engaged in protected activity, and no other intervening facts can explain the change.

**C. Failure to Provide Procedural Protections Following Dismissal**

Following the dismissal of Plaintiff's complaint, Simpson did not offer appeal rights as required by § 106.45(b)(8), and did not send simultaneous written notice to the respondent as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii). (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 9; Exhibit B-6.) This failure to follow even basic procedural requirements further demonstrates the clearly unreasonable nature of Duquesne's response.

**D. The Hollow "Meeting Requirement" as a Barrier to Relief**

Throughout the 45-day period, Duquesne officials conditioned any assistance on Plaintiff's willingness to attend meetings. (Exhibits B-1, B-2, B-3, B-5.) However, Plaintiff had already provided extensive written documentation, including sworn declarations, medical records, and detailed factual accounts. (Exhibits B-0, B-5A, F.) Conditioning supportive measures on additional meetings—especially given Plaintiff's documented safety concerns and the fact that supportive measures are "available at any time after a report" without requiring a formal complaint—constitutes "clearly unreasonable" conduct. *See* Exhibit Z-c at Slide 39 ("Available at any time after a report of sexual harassment"); Exhibit Z-c at Slide 38 ("The Title IX Coordinator is responsible for coordinating the effective implementation of supportive measures").

The Third Circuit has recognized that "where the recipient's response to the harassment or lack thereof is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances," liability attaches. *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. Duquesne's singular demand for meetings—repeatedly made while Plaintiff was experiencing homelessness, financial crisis, and documented safety fears—was the functional equivalent of putting up a procedural wall to avoid providing federally mandated relief.

**III. The Harassment Was Severe, Pervasive, and Objectively Offensive**

Under *Davis*, liability requires harassment that is "so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school." *Id.* at 650. The undisputed facts satisfy this standard.

The harassment included: (1) aggressive physical confrontation, including yelling within inches of Plaintiff's face; (2) physical pursuit through the home, with the harasser confirming she was following Plaintiff; (3) a verbal threat to enter Plaintiff's bedroom ("I'm coming up"); (4) continued harassment during Plaintiff's move-out, including following him up and down stairs while ignoring his repeated statements that he did not feel safe; and (5) an encounter on September 17, 2025, where the harasser was "waiting outside" Plaintiff's Philosophy seminar classroom. (Exhibits B-0, D, C.)

Plaintiff's doctor confirmed that Plaintiff's fear was reasonable, stating "I believe its reasonable for him to have this fear" and recommending "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 1, Aug. 13, 2025.) External Faculty Witness, an academic mentor of 19 years, declared that Plaintiff "cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress and require hours of recovery time." (Exhibit C, Supplemental Declaration.)

The severity of the harassment is further evidenced by the fact that Plaintiff fled his housing on June 30 and remained homeless for over 90 days, with his bank account dropping to negative $2.55 during this period. (Exhibits A, Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 11.) This is not a case of mere "teasing" or isolated incidents—it involves physical pursuit, threats, stalking, and psychological intimidation that caused a doctoral candidate to abandon his home and live in a state of financial and housing crisis.

**IV. Duquesne's Deliberate Indifference Denied Plaintiff Equal Educational Access**

The Third Circuit applies a straightforward pleading standard for Title IX claims: "to state a claim under Title IX, the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex." *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d at 209. The harm to Plaintiff's educational access is well-documented.

Plaintiff was forced to flee his housing; experienced homelessness for over 90 days; was denied supportive measures that would have allowed him to safely attend campus; was subjected to a retaliatory three-week cap on remote participation; and ultimately had his Zoom access terminated based on a manufactured "compelling reason" test. (Exhibits A, I, J, Q, V, W.)

His doctor opined that his homelessness "likely worsens his mental health conditions" and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2, Oct. 1, 2025.) Professor External Faculty Witness declared that Plaintiff's "education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability." (Exhibit C, Declaration of External Faculty Witness.)

The *Davis* standard requires denial of "access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school," 526 U.S. at 650. A doctoral candidate who cannot safely attend classes, cannot access campus, cannot focus on research, and is experiencing homelessness and financial destitution has clearly been denied educational access. As the Sixth Circuit recognized in *Foster*, "we are faced with a case where the University knew its initial response was ineffective and we must determine whether its subsequent actions were so reasonable—in light of that knowledge and the surrounding circumstances—as to warrant judgment in its favor as a matter of law. They were not." *Foster*, slip op. at *12.

**V. Duquesne's Conduct Demonstrates Gender-Based Discrimination**

The Third Circuit has rejected any requirement to superimpose "additional doctrinal tests on the [Title IX] statute." *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d at 209. The facts, taken as a whole, support a plausible inference of sex-based discrimination.

Plaintiff specifically raised the gender issue in his July 23, 2025 email to Dean Wasilko, asking "whether your response would have been the same had the genders in this situation been reversed." (Exhibit B-0A.) In his July 26 OCR escalation, Plaintiff stated: "I believe this is chiefly because I am male. Had I been female and my roommate male, the response would have been immediate and protective." (Exhibit B-3A.)

Under *Price Waterhouse v. Hopkins*, 490 U.S. 228 (1989), sex-stereotyping—including assumptions about how male victims of harassment should be treated—constitutes sex discrimination. *Id.* at 251. The Supreme Court held in *Desert Palace, Inc. v. Costa*, 539 U.S. 90 (2003), that circumstantial evidence is sufficient to show discrimination, and no heightened direct evidence standard applies. *Id.* at 101.

The circumstantial evidence here is powerful: Duquesne dismissed a male victim's harassment claims as not reaching the level of a conduct violation (Exhibit B-0), denied him housing and supportive measures despite available resources (Exhibit G), reversed a preliminary determination of Title IX jurisdiction after he engaged in protected activity (Exhibits B-

Moreover, Plaintiff specifically raised the issue of gender discrimination. On July 23, 2025, Plaintiff asked Dean Wasilko: "I can't help but wonder whether your response would have been the same had the genders in this situation been reversed." Exhibit B-0A. This gender-based framing, combined with the University's materially different treatment of Plaintiff's complaint, supports "a plausible inference" that the University discriminated on the basis of sex. *University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d at 209. As *Price Waterhouse v. Hopkins*, 490 U.S. 228, 250 (1989), established, when a plaintiff proves gender played a "motivating part" in a covered entity's decision, the burden shifts to the defendant to show it would have made the same decision absent the gender consideration. Here, the University's response—immediate dismissal of Plaintiff's complaint as not a "conduct violation" (Exhibit B-0), combined with Simpson's 24-hour jurisdictional flip (Exhibits B-5, B-6)—occurs in a context where Plaintiff explicitly flagged the gendered nature of the response.

**II. The University's Response Was Clearly Unreasonable—Not Merely Delayed, But Entirely Absent**

A funding recipient is deliberately indifferent when its "response to the harassment or lack thereof is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. This standard does not require courts to conclude that "minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts to respond to sexual harassment are not clearly unreasonable as a matter of law." *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314, at *9 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc). Indeed, *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022), makes clear that a university's failure to forward sexual misconduct reports to the Title IX Coordinator can itself constitute a clearly unreasonable response.

Here, the University's response was not merely ineffective or belated—it was a complete absence of any meaningful support during the critical 45-day period from June 30 to August 14, 2025. During this entire period, the University offered Plaintiff zero supportive measures. Exhibit A; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 8. Title IX Coordinator Simpson performed none of the five mandatory obligations under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a): she did not promptly contact Plaintiff to discuss supportive measures, did not consider Plaintiff's wishes, did not inform Plaintiff of the availability of supportive measures, and did not explain the process for filing a formal complaint. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 10; Exhibits B-5, B-6.

The University's response consisted exclusively of three categories of non-action: (1) requests to schedule meetings; (2) directives to "engage with" other offices; and (3) jurisdictional disclaimers. Exhibits B-0 through B-6. This pattern is precisely what *Hall* condemned—a "filing away" of complaints without action. *Hall*, slip op. at 15 (describing how university officials "looked over [the] report and then filed it away" and "did not forward [the] report"). Here, Dean Wasiliko did not forward Plaintiff's June 30 report to Title IX. Exhibit B-0 (Wasilko response states "it doesn't sound like a code of conduct violation" and suggests only a police report). When Title IX Coordinator Simpson finally received the report, she spent weeks demanding meetings (Exhibits B-1, B-2, B-3) before reversing her own jurisdictional determination in 24 hours based on no new factual information (Exhibits B-5, B-6; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7).

The University's refusal to provide housing support is particularly egregious given that it had available housing resources. Exhibit G (Wasilko Housing Denial, July 24, 2025: "I heard back from housing, and it looks like right now the only rooms we have are in the first year buildings. Obviously, that is not helpful at this juncture. However, we will continue to work on this and see if we can find something."). Despite acknowledging available housing, the University never provided it. Exhibit A. Meanwhile, Plaintiff's bank account fell to $16.65 on July 25 and negative $2.55 on August 12, 2025. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 11. Medical documentation confirms Plaintiff "reports homelessness since June 30th which has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions." Exhibit F (Treating Physician Medical Letter, October 1, 2025).

The University's response stands in stark contrast to the response deemed reasonable in *McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024), where the college issued a no-contact directive, provided academic and mental health accommodations, conducted an investigation, and imposed probation. Here, the University provided nothing—no no-contact order, no housing assistance, no academic modifications, no safety plan. The University cannot rely on the fact that it offered meetings, because meetings are not supportive measures. The Title IX training materials themselves identify supportive measures as "non-disciplinary, non-punitive, individualized" services such as "work or class schedule modifications," "mutual no contact orders," "changes in housing or work locations," and "leaves of absence." Exhibit Z-c at Slides 38-40. Conditioning these measures on Plaintiff's willingness to submit to further investigatory meetings—while Plaintiff was homeless and without funds—is itself clearly unreasonable.

Most tellingly, Simpson's jurisdictional reversal on August 14, 2025—issued 19 hours after she acknowledged the report warranted "further information gathering and review" and stated she could "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures" (Exhibit B-5)—was based on no new factual information about the underlying complaint. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7. The only intervening events were Plaintiff's three emails consisting "entirely of protected activity under § 106.71" (id.), in which Plaintiff refused a meeting, demanded written communications, and restored the Office for Civil Rights to the email chain. Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C. Simpson then not only dismissed the complaint but also failed to provide appeal rights as required by § 106.45(b)(8) and failed to send simultaneous written notice to the respondent as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii). Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 9. This combination of procedural irregularities and unexplained jurisdictional reversal supports a finding of deliberate indifference.

**III. The University's Response Was Motivated, at Least in Part, by Plaintiff's Gender and His Protected Activity**

*Price Waterhouse* holds that when a plaintiff proves gender played a "motivating part" in an adverse decision, "the defendant may avoid a finding of liability only by proving by a preponderance of the evidence that it would have made the same decision even if it had not taken the plaintiff's gender into account." 490 U.S. at 258. Here, Plaintiff explicitly raised the gender question on July 23, 2025, asking Dean Wasilko whether his response "would have been the same had the genders in this situation been reversed." Exhibit B-0A. The record contains no evidence that the University ever addressed this question.

Furthermore, the University's response—or lack thereof—can be understood as reflecting sex stereotyping. The harasser was female, Plaintiff was male, and the University dismissed the conduct as not rising to the level of a "conduct violation" (Exhibit B-0) and ultimately determined, even if proven true, the conduct "does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct" (Exhibit B-6). The inference that a female complainant reporting male-on-female harassment would have received a different response is supported by the totality of the circumstances.

Additionally, Plaintiff's protected activity—demanding Title IX compliance—was met with retaliation. *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447, 449 (3d Cir. 2006) ("Demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity"). On August 15, 2025, immediately after Plaintiff's protected emails refusing meetings and demanding written process, Dr. Sawa threatened Plaintiff with conduct charges for "sending mass emails to University officials" and stated "you must stop emailing me." Exhibit B-7. Plaintiff correctly identified this as retaliatory under 34 C.F.R. § 106.71. Exhibit I (Mullins response, August 25, 2025). The three-week limit on remote participation imposed by Dr. Selcer (Exhibit I, August 25, 2025) was, as Plaintiff argued, "a countdown clock designed to coerce compliance or force attrition—a textbook example of retaliation masquerading as accommodation." Exhibit I-1 (September 2, 2025). The Chair's subsequent removal of Plaintiff's Zoom access after a Temporary Restraining Order hearing where he had promised to continue it further confirms a pattern of retaliatory conduct. Exhibits Q, V, W.

Under *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173, 177 (3d Cir. 1997), "circumstantial evidence of a 'pattern of antagonism' following the protected conduct can also give rise to the inference" of causation. Here, the pattern is unmistakable: every time Plaintiff asserted his Title IX rights, the University escalated its demands for meetings (Exhibits B-2, B-3), changed its jurisdictional position (Exhibit B-6), threatened conduct charges (Exhibit B-7), or imposed additional conditions on his continued academic participation (Exhibit I).

**IV. Plaintiff Has Suffered a Denial of Equal Educational Access**

To establish liability, Plaintiff must show the harassment was "so severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive that it can be said to deprive the victims of access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 650. The record demonstrates that Plaintiff has been deprived of access in multiple ways: he was forced to flee his home and became homeless (Exhibit A); he was unable to participate in in-person classes for extended periods (Exhibit I); his academic work was severely derailed (Exhibit C, External Faculty Witness Declaration: "his education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability"); his mental health deteriorated as his medical conditions worsened (Exhibit F, October 1, 2025: "worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education"); and his physical safety on campus remained unaddressed (Exhibit C, Supplemental External Faculty Witness Declaration describing September 17, 2025 encounter where harasser waited outside Plaintiff's classroom). The University's refusal to provide supportive measures directly caused these deprivations. As *Davis* makes clear, "the recipient's response to the harassment or lack thereof is clearly unreasonable in light of the

**ARGUMENT**

**I. Defendant Duquesne University Acted with Deliberate Indifference to Known Sexual Harassment, Denying Plaintiff Equal Access to Education Under Title IX**

Title IX provides a private right of action for damages against funding recipients who act with deliberate indifference to known student-on-student sexual harassment. *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629, 650 (1999). To prevail on Count I, Plaintiff must establish: (1) actual knowledge by an official with authority to institute corrective measures; (2) a response that is "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances"; (3) sexual harassment that was "severe, pervasive, and objectively offensive"; and (4) a resulting deprivation of "access to the educational opportunities or benefits provided by the school." *Id.* at 648, 650; *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275, slip op. at 8 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022). The Third Circuit has cautioned against superimposing additional doctrinal tests on the statute; "to state a claim under Title IX, the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex." *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203, 209 (3d Cir. 2020). The undisputed record demonstrates that Duquesne satisfies each of these elements.

**A. Duquesne Had Actual Knowledge of the Sexual Harassment Through Officials with Authority to Institute Corrective Measures**

Actual knowledge is established when a Title IX Coordinator or "any official of the recipient who has authority to institute corrective measures on behalf of the recipient" receives notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment. *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 650; *Hall*, slip op. at 8. Multiple Duquesne officials with such authority received actual notice of Plaintiff's harassment.

On June 30, 2025, Plaintiff reported to Dean Wasilko—the Associate Vice President for Student Life and Dean of Students—that a fellow graduate student had pursued him through his home, confirmed she was following him, stated "I'm coming up" as she ascended the stairs toward his bedroom, and that he had locked himself in his room because he felt physically threatened. (Exhibit B-0). Dean Wasilko is an official with authority to institute corrective measures, including referring matters to Public Safety, Student Conduct, and Title IX. (Exhibit B-0). As the *Davis* Court recognized, a school's actual knowledge arises when officials who can "address the alleged discrimination and to institute corrective measures" receive notice. *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 650.

On July 24, 2025, Plaintiff escalated his complaint directly to Title IX Coordinator Alicia Simpson, the president, provost, and legal affairs, explicitly citing 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a) and demanding supportive measures. (SAC ¶¶ 168-172). Simpson acknowledged receipt and her role: "My office is notified when there are reported incidents of alleged sexual harassment or sexual misconduct involving students. . . . One of my primary roles is to help ensure your health and safety. The Title IX office provides oversight to the University processes for Title IX sexual harassment and non-Title IX sexual misconduct, and I am here to provide you with support and talk with you about resources." (Exhibit B-1). Simpson is the Title IX Coordinator—the official with the *highest* authority to implement Title IX corrective measures. Actual knowledge is thereby established. *See Hall*, slip op. at 8 (actual knowledge established when Title IX Coordinator receives report).

On August 13, 2025, Plaintiff submitted an Emergency Removal Request under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(c), attaching sworn declarations and a physician's letter documenting his reasonable fear of physical harm and the resulting homelessness. (Exhibit F; Exhibit B-5A). Simpson responded at 2:02 PM the same day, acknowledging that "your concerns and allegations warrant further information gathering and review" and specifically offering to "discuss your current needs relating to supportive measures"—the statutory term of art defined in 34 C.F.R. § 106.30. (Exhibit B-5). This acknowledgment constitutes actual knowledge of actionable sexual harassment.

Moreover, Duquesne itself trained its Title IX personnel that a Title IX Coordinator and officials like the Dean of Students have actual knowledge upon receipt of a report, and that a Responsible Employee program means "every employee who receives a report = actual knowledge to the institution." (Exhibit Z-e, Slides 30-34). Duquesne cannot now disclaim what it trained itself to know.

**B. Duquesne's Response Was "Clearly Unreasonable" and Thus Deliberately Indifferent**

A school is deliberately indifferent when its response "is clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. This standard does not require courts to conclude that only "minimal, ineffective, or belated efforts" are clearly unreasonable. *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314, slip op. at 10 (6th Cir. Dec. 11, 2020) (en banc). The Third Circuit has held that "failure to forward sexual misconduct reports to Title IX Coordinator as required by policy can constitute clearly unreasonable response." *Hall*, slip op. at 8. Here, Duquesne's response was not merely inadequate—it was nonexistent for 45 days, followed by a retributive jurisdictional flip-flop, and then outright retaliation.

**1. Duquesne's 45-Day Failure to Offer Any Supportive Measures Was Objectively Unreasonable**

From June 30, 2025, through August 14, 2025, Duquesne offered Plaintiff zero supportive measures through the Title IX process. (Exhibits B-0 through B-6). The record shows that between these dates, Duquesne's communications consisted exclusively of requests to schedule meetings, directives to "engage with" various offices, and eventual jurisdictional disclaimers. *Id.* During this same period, Plaintiff's bank account fell to $16.65 (July 25) and negative $2.55 (August 12), while he lacked permanent housing. (Bank records). A physician certified that Plaintiff's homelessness "worsens his mental health conditions" and that "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing" were necessary. (Exhibit F). The *Davis* court recognized that a school cannot simply "do nothing" in the face of known harassment, and "in effect, 'subject' [its] students to discrimination" by refusing to act. *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 654.

Simpson performed zero of the five mandatory obligations under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a): she did not promptly contact Plaintiff to discuss supportive measures, did not consider Plaintiff's wishes, did not inform Plaintiff of the availability of supportive measures, and did not explain the process for filing a formal complaint. (Exhibits B-5, B-6). The preamble to the regulations makes clear these are not optional. Duquesne's own Title IX training material defines supportive measures as "available at any time after a report of sexual harassment," "non-disciplinary," "non-punitive," and required to be offered "promptly." (Exhibit Z-c, Slides 38-40). The training explicitly states that these measures are "Available to both Complainants and Respondents" and "Designed to restore or preserve equal access to the education program or activity." *Id.* Simpson's failure to offer *any* supportive measure for 45 days—despite Plaintiff's explicit requests for housing, no-contact orders, and safety measures—was thus not only contrary to the regulatory framework but contrary to Duquesne's own training.

**2. The Jurisdictional Reversal Constituted a Clearly Unreasonable Response**

On August 13, 2025, at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged jurisdiction, stating that the allegations "warrant further information gathering and review" by the Title IX Office, Student Conduct, and Public Safety, and offered to discuss "supportive measures." (Exhibit B-5). At 4:20 PM, Plaintiff replied refusing the proffered meeting, demanding written communications, restoring OCR to the CC line, and citing §§ 106.44, 106.30, and 106.71. (Exhibit B-5A). Plaintiff sent two additional emails that evening, none of which contained any new facts about the underlying complaint. (Exhibits B-5B, B-5C).

Nineteen hours later—on August 14 at 9:42 AM—Simpson reversed course entirely, declaring: "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested." (Exhibit B-6). She stated the conduct "does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true." *Id.* No new factual information about the underlying complaint entered the record between B-5 and B-6. The only intervening events were Plaintiff's three emails consisting entirely of protected activity. (Exhibits B-5, B-5A, B-5B, B-5C, B-6).

This sequence—a Title IX Coordinator initially accepting a complaint, then reversing jurisdiction in 19 hours with no new facts—is "clearly unreasonable" by any measure. As the *Hall* court recognized, a school's failure to follow its own Title IX policy and procedures is evidence of deliberate indifference. *Hall*, slip op. at 8. Here, Simpson not only failed to follow her own procedures, but she also acknowledged that she made her "preliminary determination" "without a meeting" because Plaintiff was "unwilling to meet." (Exhibit B-6). Yet the regulations require a recipient to "respond promptly" to a report of sexual harassment, not condition that response on a meeting. *See* 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a). Simpson also failed to offer Plaintiff appeal rights following the dismissal, as required by § 106.45(b)(8), and failed to send simultaneous written notice to the respondent, as required by § 106.45(b)(3)(iii).
