At the prima facie stage, Plaintiff need only show that protected activity was the "likely reason" for the adverse action. *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017).

*Temporal Proximity*: The timing here is extraordinary. Two days after Plaintiff's OCR filing, Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line. (Exhibit B-3). Nineteen hours after Plaintiff sent three protected-activity emails on August 13, Simpson reversed her jurisdiction determination. (Exhibits B-5, B-5A, B-5B, B-5C, B-6). Two days after Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing, Selcer revoked Zoom access. (TRO transcript; Timeline DB). This sequence of unusually suggestive timing alone establishes causation. *Jalil*, 873 F.2d at 708 (two-day gap sufficient).

*Pattern of Antagonism*: Even setting aside the tight temporal proximity, the record reveals a clear pattern of antagonism following Plaintiff's protected activity. Defendant retaliated against Plaintiff for including OCR on emails, reversed jurisdiction after protected emails, revoked accommodations after litigation activity, and fabricated performance issues after litigation testimony. This pattern independently establishes the causal link. *Kachmar*, 109 F.3d at 177.

*Inconsistent Explanations as Evidence of Pretext*: Defendant's shifting justifications further demonstrate causation. Selcer provided contradictory reasons for the Zoom denial and ultimately admitted he "can't explain why." (Exhibit L). Selcer's February 13, 2026 reassignment letter (Exhibit X-8) fabricated "performance issues raised by two of the three faculty members," yet Faculty B's same-day email stated there was "no performance complaint" and described reassignment as keeping a "door open." (Exhibit X-7). Selcer himself had testified that Plaintiff had no performance issues. (TRO Tr.). These inconsistent explanations establish a causal link and demonstrate pretext. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 286 (inconsistent explanations establish causal link); *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022) ("The employer's motivation for investigating the plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext").

*Evidence Looked at as a Whole*: The "proffered evidence, looked at as a whole, may suffice to raise the inference" of causation. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 281. Here, the evidence includes: (1) continuous protected activity, including federal litigation; (2) eight-plus removals of OCR from correspondence; (3) a nineteen-hour jurisdictional reversal; (4) Zoom revocation two days after a TRO hearing; (5) a fabricated performance issue threat to stipend through 2029; and (6) shifting, contradictory explanations. Together, this evidence forms a "convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence" sufficient to establish causation. *Canada*, No. 20-2747; *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259 (3d Cir. 2007) (temporal proximity plus contextual evidence sufficient).

**CONCLUSION**

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff has established a prima facie case of retaliation under Title IX. The evidence demonstrates continuous protected activity, multiple adverse actions, and a clear causal connection supported by temporal proximity, a pattern of antagonism, and inconsistent explanations. Summary judgment on Count II must be denied.

## ARGUMENT

Plaintiff David Mullins satisfies each element of his Title IX retaliation claim under the framework adopted by the Third Circuit. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247 (3d Cir. 2017) (Title IX retaliation uses Title VII burden-shifting framework). The evidence—viewed as a whole—establishes that Mullins engaged in protected activity, suffered multiple adverse actions that would deter a reasonable person, and that a causal connection exists between the two. *Farrell v. Planters Lifesavers Co.*, 206 F.3d 271 (3d Cir. 2000) (proffered evidence “looked at as a whole” may suffice to raise inference of causation). Indeed, the record reveals a “convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence” sufficient to prove causation, *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022), and Duquesne’s shifting, pretextual justifications for its actions further confirm retaliation. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271 (inconsistent explanations establish causal link).

### I. Mullins Engaged in Extensive Protected Activity

Title IX protects individuals who “oppose” unlawful sex discrimination or “participate” in Title IX proceedings. *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447 (3d Cir. 2006) (“Demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity”). The protected conduct need only be based on an objectively reasonable, good-faith belief that the opposed conduct violates the statute. *Kengerski v. Harper*, No. 20-1307 (3d Cir. 2021). Mullins satisfied this standard continuously from June 30, 2025, onward through multiple acts:

- **June 30, 2025:** Reporting sex-based harassment to Dean Wasilko (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 1; Exhibit B-0; ECF docket);
- **July 23, 2025:** Sending a Gender Reversal Hypothetical to Wasilko that expressly questioned whether Wasilko’s response would have differed “had the genders in this situation been reversed” (Exhibit B-0A). This is a direct demand for equal treatment under Title IX, squarely protected under *Atkinson*;
- **July 24, 2025:** Formal Title IX escalation citing 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a) (SAC ¶ 1; ECF docket);
- **July 26, 2025:** Filing a formal complaint with the U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights (OCR), detailing sex discrimination and retaliation (Exhibit B-3A). That complaint explicitly invoked Title IX and accused Duquesne of gender-based disparate treatment;
- **September 9, 2025:** Filing the instant federal lawsuit (SAC ¶ 1; ECF docket);
- **October 8, 2025:** Filing a motion for a temporary restraining order (SAC ¶ 1; ECF docket);
- **October 15, 2025:** Testifying at the TRO hearing (SAC ¶ 1; ECF docket).

Additionally, Mullins repeatedly included OCR on institutional correspondence, demanding that Duquesne comply with its Title IX obligations (Exhibits B-3A, I, I-1). Each of these acts is protected activity under *Atkinson* and *Kengerski*.

### II. Duquesue Took Multiple Adverse Actions That Would Deter a Reasonable Person

An adverse action in the retaliation context is one that “well might have dissuaded a reasonable worker from making or supporting a charge of discrimination.” *Canada*, No. 20-2747. Duquesne engaged in a sustained campaign of retaliation, including:

- **Systematic Removal of OCR from Email Communications:** Between July 28 and October 2025, four institutional actors (Wasilko, Simpson, Sawa, and others) removed OCR from the CC line on eight or more occasions, each time after Mullins restored OCR. No federal authority or Duquesne policy authorized removal of a federal enforcement agency from institutional correspondence (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 3; Exhibits B-3 through B-7 series). This was an unambiguous attempt to silence Mullins’s access to federal oversight.
- **Unexplained Reversal of Title IX Jurisdiction:** On August 13, 2025, Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction and used the § 106.30 term of art “supportive measures” (Exhibit B-5). Within hours after Mullins sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity and zero new facts (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C), Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination on an identical factual record on August 14 (Exhibit B-6). The gap was nineteen hours. This reversal directly deprived Mullins of Title IX protections and remedies.
- **Retaliatory Zoom Revocation:** On October 6, 2025, Faculty C authorized Zoom access for Mullins. On October 8, Mullins filed the TRO motion. On October 15, he testified at the TRO hearing. Two days later—on October 17—Selcer manufactured Zoom revocation (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 5; TRO transcript; ECF docket; Timeline DB). Selcer provided shifting justifications and ultimately admitted he “can’t explain why” Zoom was denied (Exhibit L; SJ 7.3). The revocation directly followed Mullins’s most aggressive protected activity—litigation and testimony.
- **Threat of Academic Failure:** In September 2025, Dean Blair threatened Mullins with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer’s class in person while active federal litigation against Selcer was pending (SAC ¶¶ 66, 76, 336). This threat was designed to force Mullins to choose between his safety and his academic standing.
- **Fabricated TA Reassignment with Stipend Threat:** On February 13, 2026, Selcer reassigned Mullins’s TA position and claimed “performance issues raised by Faculty B” (Exhibit X-8). Yet Faculty B’s own email that same day described the reassignment request as keeping a “door open” with “no performance complaint” (Exhibit X-7). Selcer’s email mid-sentence recharacterized “tensions and confrontations” as “performance issues” and threatened Mullins’s stipend through Spring 2029 (Exhibit X-8). Selcer had testified at the TRO hearing that Mullins had no performance issues (TRO Tr. at *:*). This was a transparent effort to punish Mullins for his ongoing litigation.

Each of these actions would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity. *Canada*, No. 20-2747.

### III. The Causal Connection Is Overwhelming

Mullins can establish causation through (i) unusually suggestive temporal proximity, (ii) a pattern of antagonism, (iii) inconsistent explanations, and (iv) the “convincing mosaic” of circumstantial evidence. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271; *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173 (3d Cir. 1997); *Canada*, No. 20-2747. At the prima facie stage, Mullins need only show that protected activity was the “likely reason” for the adverse action, not the but-for reason. *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017).

#### A. Unusually Suggestive Temporal Proximity

Third Circuit law is clear: extremely close temporal proximity can alone establish causation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701 (3d Cir. 1989) (two days sufficient). Here, multiple adverse actions followed Mullins’s protected activity with exceptional speed:

- Two days after Mullins filed his OCR complaint (July 26), Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line (July 28) (Exhibits B-3, B-3A). *Jalil* directly supports this sequence.
- Nineteen hours after Mullins sent three emails of pure protected activity (August 13 afternoon), Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination (August 14 morning) (Exhibits B-5, B-5A, B-5B, B-5C, B-6). The gap is less than one day—even more “unusually suggestive” than the two days in *Jalil*.
- Two days after Mullins filed the TRO motion (October 8) and two days after he testified (October 15), Selcer revoked Zoom access (October 17) (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 5).

Temporal proximity alone establishes causation for these events.

#### B. Pattern of Antagonism

Even where temporal proximity is less precise, a “pattern of antagonism” following protected conduct suffices to prove causation. *Kachmar*, 109 F.3d at 173. Here, the pattern is relentless:

- From July 28 onward, Duquesne officials repeatedly stripped OCR from emails, each time after Mullins restored OCR (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 3).
- On August 15, Sawa threatened Mullins with student conduct charges for continuing to email university officials about his safety—an explicit attempt to silence further protected activity (Exhibit B-7). *See* *Canada*, No. 20-2747 (employer’s motivation for investigating plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext).
- In September, Blair threatened academic failure for missing in-person class during active litigation (SAC ¶¶ 66, 76, 336).
- On October 22, Blair consulted with Selcer to issue a joint determination requiring Mullins to meet with Selcer—the very individual he was suing—to avoid failing the teaching seminar (Exhibit K-2).
- In February 2026, Selcer fabricated “performance issues” and threatened Mullins’s stipend through 2029, despite Faculty B’s contemporaneous email confirming “no performance complaint” and Selcer’s own prior testimony that Mullins had no performance issues (Exhibits X-7, X-8; TRO Tr. at *:*).

This pattern of escalating retaliation, each step triggered by Mullins’s persistent protected activity, independently establishes causation. *Kachmar*, 109 F.3d at 173 (“circumstantial evidence of a ‘pattern of antagonism’ following the protected conduct can also give rise to the inference”); *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259 (3d Cir. 2007) (temporal proximity plus contextual evidence sufficient for Title IX retaliation).

#### C. Shifting and Inconsistent Explanations

Farrell holds that “inconsistent explanations” from the employer establish a causal link. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271. Here, Duquesne’s justifications are demonstrably false:

- **Zoom revocation:** Selcer first claimed a policy constraint, then admitted he “can’t explain why” Zoom was denied (Exhibit L; SJ 7.3). No policy constrained Zoom access (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 6).
- **TA reassignment:** Selcer claimed “performance issues raised by Faculty B” (Exhibit X-8). But Faculty B wrote that same morning that the reassignment was to keep a “door open” with “no performance complaint” (Exhibit X-7). Selcer had also testified under oath that Mullins had no performance issues (TRO Tr. at *:*). The only explanation for this contradiction is retaliatory animus.
- **Jurisdiction reversal:** Simpson acknowledged jurisdiction and used “supportive measures” language on August 13 (Exhibit B-5), then reversed on August 14 without any new facts (Exhibit B-6). The only intervening event was Mullins’s three protected emails. The reversal cannot be explained by the record—only by retaliation.

Under *Canada*, “the employer’s motivation for investigating the plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext.” Here, Duquesne’s shifting, contradictory, and false justifications demonstrate that the stated reasons are pretextual and that retaliation was the real motive.

#### D. The Convincing Mosaic

Finally, “a convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence is sufficient to establish causation for retaliation claims.” *Canada*, No. 20-2747. The sum total of the evidence—temporal proximity, pattern of antagonism, inconsistent explanations, threats, and fabricated performance issues—creates an overwhelming circumstantial case. As *Farrell* instructs, the court must look at the evidence “as a whole,” not compartmentalize it. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271. The whole picture here reveals a university that systematically retaliated against Mullins for demanding his Title IX rights, beginning with small acts (removing OCR from emails) and escalating to threats of academic failure, loss of stipend, and fabricated performance issues.

At the prima facie stage, Mullins need only show that protected activity was the “likely reason” for the adverse actions. *Carvalho-Grevious*, No. 15-3521. He has done so overwhelmingly.

### IV. Duquesne’s Articulated Reasons Are Pretextual

Even if Duquesne offers legitimate, non-retaliatory reasons for its actions, Mullins has ample evidence that those reasons are pretext. As detailed above, Simpson’s jurisdiction reversal had no factual basis; Selcer’s Zoom justification is inexplicable even to himself; and the “performance issues” in the TA reassignment were fabricated by Selcer mid-sentence. Under *Farrell*, “nothing about the McDonnell Douglas formula requires us to ration the evidence between one stage or the other.” Thus, the same evidence that establishes the prima facie case also demonstrates pretext. Duquesne cannot meet its burden at summary judgment or trial.

### CONCLUSION

For these reasons, the Court should deny any summary judgment motion by Duquesne and find that Mullins has established a prima facie case of retaliation under Title IX, with abundant evidence of causation and pretext.

**ARGUMENT**

Count II of Plaintiff's complaint asserts that Defendant Duquesne University retaliated against him for engaging in protected activity under Title IX, in violation of 20 U.S.C. § 1681. To establish a prima facie case of Title IX retaliation, Plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) protected activity; (2) an adverse action that would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity; and (3) a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse action. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247 (3d Cir. 2017) (applying Title VII burden-shifting framework); *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447, 456 (3d Cir. 2006) (holding that "demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity"). As demonstrated below, the undisputed evidence establishes all three elements.

**I. Plaintiff Engaged in Continuous Protected Activity**

Plaintiff engaged in a sustained course of protected activity beginning June 30, 2025, and continuing through the present. The record reflects at least seven discrete protected acts: (1) reporting sex-based harassment to Dean Wasilko on June 30 (Ex. B-0); (2) escalating his Title IX complaint on July 24 (Ex. B-1); (3) filing a complaint with the U.S. Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights ("OCR") on July 26 (Ex. B-3A); (4) filing the instant federal lawsuit on September 9 (ECF docket); (5) filing a motion for temporary restraining order ("TRO") on October 8 (ECF docket); and (6) testifying at the TRO hearing on October 15 (TRO Tr.). *See Atkinson*, 460 F.3d at 456 ("Demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity."). Plaintiff need only have held an "objectively reasonable belief, in good faith" that the conduct he opposed violated Title VII. *Kengerski v. Harper*, No. 20-1307 (3d Cir. 2021). Plaintiff's multiple reports to university officials, his OCR complaint, and his federal lawsuit all satisfy this standard.

**II. Plaintiff Suffered Multiple Adverse Actions That Would Deter a Reasonable Person**

The record is replete with adverse actions that a reasonable person would find deterring. First, university actors systematically removed OCR from the CC line on institutional correspondence. On July 28—just two days after Plaintiff filed his OCR complaint—Dean Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line (Ex. B-3). This pattern continued through at least eight separate occasions (Exs. B-3 through B-7 series). No federal authority or university policy authorized this conduct. Second, on August 13-14, Title IX Coordinator Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction over Plaintiff's complaint at 2:02 PM (Ex. B-5), then reversed her jurisdictional determination just nineteen hours later on August 14 at 9:42 AM, on an identical factual record (Ex. B-6). This reversal occurred immediately after Plaintiff sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity (Exs. B-5A, B-5B, B-5C). Third, on August 15, Executive Director of Student Conduct Sawa threatened Plaintiff with disciplinary charges for seeking help from university officials (Ex. B-7). Fourth, on October 17—just two days after Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing—Chair Selcer revoked Plaintiff's Zoom access to seminars (Ex. V). Selcer provided shifting justifications and ultimately "c[ould not] explain why" Zoom was denied (Ex. L; SJ 7.3). Fifth, in September 2025, Dean Blair threatened Plaintiff with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer's class in person during active federal litigation against Selcer (SAC ¶¶ 66, 76, 336). Sixth, on February 13, 2026, Selcer reassigned Plaintiff's TA position, fabricating "performance issues raised by Faculty B" that Faculty B herself never raised (Exs. X-7, X-8). Faculty B's same-day email described the reassignment as keeping a "door open" with "no performance complaint" (Ex. X-7). Selcer then threatened Plaintiff's stipend through Spring 2029 (Ex. X-8). Each of these actions—stripping Plaintiff of access, threatening discipline, fabricating performance issues, and threatening financial harm—"would deter a reasonable person" from engaging in protected activity. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247.

**III. The Causal Connection Is Established by Temporal Proximity and a Pattern of Antagonism**

Causation is established through multiple avenues. First, the temporal proximity between Plaintiff's protected activity and the adverse actions is "unusually suggestive." *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701, 708 (3d Cir. 1989). The removal of OCR from the CC line occurred just two days after Plaintiff's OCR filing (Ex. B-3). The Zoom revocation occurred just two days after Plaintiff's TRO testimony (Ex. V; TRO Tr.). The fabricated performance issues and stipend threat followed immediately after Plaintiff's ongoing federal litigation (Exs. X-7, X-8). Second, the record reveals a clear "pattern of antagonism" following Plaintiff's protected activity. *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173, 178 (3d Cir. 1997). Between July 28 and October 2025, four institutional actors systematically removed OCR from correspondence on eight or more occasions—each time after Plaintiff restored OCR (Exs. B-3 through B-7 series). This pattern demonstrates a coordinated effort to exclude the very agency Plaintiff had invoked. Third, the "inconsistent explanations" for adverse actions independently establish causation. *Farrell v. Planters Lifesavers Co.*, 206 F.3d 271, 286 (3d Cir. 2000). Selcer provided shifting justifications for the Zoom denial (Ex. L; SJ 7.3). Selcer's February 13, 2026 email introduced "performance issues" language that Faculty B's same-day email directly contradicted (Exs. X-7, X-8). At the TRO hearing, Selcer testified that Plaintiff had no performance issues (TRO Tr. at *:*). These inconsistencies establish pretext and causal connection. *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022) ("The employer's motivation for investigating the plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext."). Fourth, the "proffered evidence, looked at as a whole, may suffice to raise the inference" of causation. *Kachmar*, 109 F.3d at 178; *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 286. The entire record demonstrates that each escalation of protected activity was met with immediate retaliation. This "convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence is sufficient to establish causation for retaliation claims." *Canada*, No. 20-2747.

**IV. The University's Response Was Clearly Unreasonable Under Davis**

The retaliatory response to Plaintiff's protected activity is "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629, 648 (1999). The university had actual knowledge of Plaintiff's sex-based harassment and homelessness through multiple reports to Dean Wasilko (Exs. B-0, B-0A), Title IX Coordinator Simpson (Ex. B-1), and other senior leaders. Yet it refused to provide supportive measures—including housing and no-contact orders—that are mandatory under Title IX. *See* 34 C.F.R. § 106.30 (supportive measures are non-disciplinary, non-punitive, individualized, and available without fee or charge); Ex. Z-c (PCHE Training Slide 38-40 describing supportive measures as "mandatory," "non-punitive," and including "changes in housing"). The university's failure to forward Plaintiff's reports to the Title IX Coordinator, its refusal to implement supportive measures, and its active retaliation constitute a "clearly unreasonable" response. *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022) (failure to forward sexual misconduct reports can constitute clearly unreasonable response). At the prima facie stage, Plaintiff need only show that protected activity was the "likely reason" for the adverse action—not the but-for reason. *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017). Plaintiff easily satisfies this standard.

**CONCLUSION**

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff has established a prima facie case of Title IX retaliation. The undisputed evidence demonstrates protected activity, multiple adverse actions, and a causal connection established through temporal proximity, a pattern of antagonism, and inconsistent explanations. Summary judgment should be denied.

**ARGUMENT**

**Count II — Retaliation Under Title IX (20 U.S.C. § 1681)**

**I. Applicable Legal Standard**

Title IX retaliation claims are analyzed under the Title VII burden-shifting framework. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247 (3d Cir. 2017). To establish a prima facie case of retaliation, Plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) protected activity; (2) an adverse action that would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity; and (3) a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse action.

At the prima facie stage, Plaintiff need only show that the protected activity was the "likely reason" for the adverse action—not the but-for reason. *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017). Direct evidence is not required; circumstantial evidence suffices. *Desert Palace, Inc. v. Costa*, 539 U.S. 90 (2003). A "convincing mosaic" of circumstantial evidence is sufficient to establish causation. *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022). Moreover, "it is causation, not temporal proximity itself, that is an element of plaintiff's prima facie case," and the "proffered evidence, looked at as a whole, may suffice to raise the inference." *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173 (3d Cir. 1997); *Farrell v. Planters Lifesavers Co.*, 206 F.3d 271 (3d Cir. 2000).

**II. Plaintiff Engaged in Protected Activity**

Plaintiff engaged in continuous protected activity from June 30, 2025 through the present. Protected activity includes reporting sex-based harassment, formally escalating complaints under Title IX, filing complaints with the Office for Civil Rights, filing a federal lawsuit, and testifying at a TRO hearing. *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447 (3d Cir. 2006) ("Demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity"). Plaintiff need only hold an "objectively reasonable belief, in good faith" that the conduct opposed violates the statute; an actual violation is not required. *Kengerski v. Harper*, No. 20-1307 (3d Cir. 2021).

The record documents the following protected activity:

- **June 30, 2025:** Plaintiff reported sex-based harassment to Dean Wasilko, describing an incident where a female housemate aggressively followed and intimidated him, and requesting emergency housing and protective measures. (Exhibit B-0).

- **July 24, 2025:** Plaintiff formally escalated his complaint under 34 C.F.R. § 106.44(a), engaging with Title IX Coordinator Simpson and demanding supportive measures. (Exhibit B-1).

- **July 26, 2025:** Plaintiff filed a formal complaint with the U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights, specifically alleging sex discrimination and retaliation. (Exhibit B-3A).

- **September 9, 2025:** Plaintiff filed a federal lawsuit against Duquesne University. (Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 1).

- **October 8, 2025:** Plaintiff filed a motion for a Temporary Restraining Order. (Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 1).

- **October 15, 2025:** Plaintiff testified under oath at the TRO hearing. (Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 1; TRO transcript).

Each of these actions constitutes protected activity under Title IX. *Atkinson*, 460 F.3d at 447. Plaintiff reasonably believed he was opposing sex-based harassment and discrimination, satisfying *Kengerski*'s objective reasonableness standard. *Kengerski*, No. 20-1307.

**III. Plaintiff Suffered Adverse Actions That Would Deter a Reasonable Person**

The record demonstrates a sustained pattern of adverse actions following Plaintiff's protected activity, each of which would deter a reasonable person from further protected conduct:

**A. Removal of OCR from Institutional Correspondence**

On July 28, 2025—two days after Plaintiff's OCR filing on July 26—Dean Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line on his responsive email. (Exhibit B-3; compare Exhibit B-2 with Exhibit B-3). Between July 28 and October 2025, four institutional actors (Wasilko, Simpson, Sawa, and others) systematically removed OCR from the CC line on eight or more occasions, each time after Plaintiff restored OCR. (Exhibits B-3 through B-7 series). No federal authority or Duquesne policy authorizes removal of a federal enforcement agency from institutional correspondence. This conduct communicates that Plaintiff's pursuit of federal oversight will be met with institutional obstruction.

**B. Simpson's 19-Hour Jurisdictional Reversal on Identical Facts**

On August 13, 2025, at 2:02 PM, Title IX Coordinator Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction using the § 106.30 term of art "supportive measures." (Exhibit B-5). Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM that same day, Plaintiff sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity—demanding compliance, citing federal regulations, and reiterating his right to supportive measures without meeting requirements—and zero new facts about the underlying complaint. (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C). On August 14, at 9:42 AM—nineteen hours later—Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination on an identical factual record, explicitly disclaiming Title IX jurisdiction. (Exhibit B-6). The reversal came only after Plaintiff's protected emails and was unsupported by any new factual development. The temporal proximity of two days between Plaintiff's protected activity and the reversal establishes causation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701 (3d Cir. 1989) ("Two-day temporal proximity between protected activity and adverse action is sufficient to establish causation").

**C. Sawa's Retaliation Threat**

On August 15, 2025, Executive Director of Student Conduct Sawa threatened Plaintiff with disciplinary charges—including potential code of conduct violations—for sending "mass emails to University officials and offices about your concerns." (Exhibit B-7). This threat was issued directly in response to Plaintiff's protected communications about safety and Title IX compliance. A threat of disciplinary action for complaining about discrimination is quintessential retaliation. *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022) (failure to forward sexual misconduct reports to Title IX Coordinator as required by policy can constitute clearly unreasonable response).

**D. Selcer's Zoom Revocation**

On October 6, 2025, Professor Faculty C independently authorized Zoom access for Plaintiff. (Exhibits Q, W). On October 8, Plaintiff filed the TRO motion. On October 15, Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing—where Selcer himself confirmed under oath that Zoom access would continue. (Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 5). On October 17—two days after the hearing—Selcer manufactured Zoom revocation by claiming Faculty C found "no compelling reason" for continued remote attendance. (Exhibit V). Yet Faculty C's own communications show he was confused by Selcer's representation, stating he had "authorized that arrangement at the time" and directing Plaintiff to "ask Dan about any need for continued online attendance." (Exhibit W). Selcer provided shifting justifications for the Zoom denial and ultimately told his own colleague he "can't explain why" Zoom was denied. (Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 6). No university or departmental policy constrains Zoom access. (Exhibit N; Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 6).

The temporal proximity between Plaintiff's TRO testimony and the Zoom revocation is two days, establishing causation. *Jalil*, 873 F.2d at 701. Moreover, Selcer's inconsistent explanations constitute "evidence of pretext." *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271 ("inconsistent explanations establish causal link").

**E. Blair's Academic Threat**

In September 2025, Dean Blair threatened Plaintiff with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer's class in person during active federal litigation against Selcer. (SAC ¶¶ 66, 76, 336; Exhibit K-2). Blair explicitly required Plaintiff to "return to class today in order to complete the course with a passing grade," despite Plaintiff's documented conflict of interest due to ongoing litigation against Selcer. (Exhibit K-2). This threat conditions academic standing on Plaintiff's willingness to be physically present under the instruction of a named defendant in active litigation—a plainly coercive response to Plaintiff's protected legal activity.

**F. Selcer's Fabrication of Performance Issues**

On February 13, 2026, Selcer reassigned Plaintiff's TA position and, for the first time, fabricated "performance issues raised by Faculty B." (Exhibit X-8). Faculty B's own same-day email (Exhibit X-7) made no mention of performance issues and described the reassignment request as keeping a "door open" with "no performance complaint." Selcer's email mid-sentence recharacterized "tensions and confrontations" as "performance issues" and threatened Plaintiff's stipend through Spring 2029. (Exhibit X-8). Moreover, Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had no performance issues. (TRO Tr. at *:*). As of February 10, 2026—just three days before the fabricated email—Selcer himself confirmed to Plaintiff that "Faculty B doesn't make decisions about your stipend" and used no "performance issues" language. (Exhibit X-6). The stark contradiction between Selcer's TRO testimony, Faculty B's own email, and Selcer's February 13 email constitutes compelling evidence of pretext. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271.

**IV. Causal Connection Exists Between Protected Activity and Adverse Actions**

Causation is established through multiple independent pathways:

**A. Unusually Suggestive Temporal Proximity**

Multiple adverse actions occurred within days of Plaintiff's protected activity:
- July 26: OCR filed → July 28: Wasilko removes OCR from CC line (2 days)
- August 13: Plaintiff sends protected emails demanding compliance → August 14: Simpson reverses jurisdiction (19 hours)
- October 15: Plaintiff testifies at TRO hearing → October 17: Selcer revokes Zoom access (2 days)
These intervals satisfy *Jalil*'s "two-day temporal proximity" standard. *Jalil*, 873 F.2d at 701.

**B. Pattern of Antagonism**

Where there is a lack of temporal proximity, "circumstantial evidence of a 'pattern of antagonism' following the protected conduct can also give rise to the inference." *Kachmar*, 109 F.3d at 173. Here, the record demonstrates a sustained pattern of antagonism spanning from July 2025 through February 2026, including: systematic OCR removal from emails; threatened disciplinary charges; jurisdictional reversal on identical facts; coercive academic threats; retaliatory Zoom revocation; and fabricated performance issues. This pattern strongly supports causation. *Kachmar*, 109 F.3d at 173; *Canada*, No. 20-2747.

**C. Evidence of Pretext and Shifting Justifications**

Selcer provided shifting justifications for the Zoom denial (Exhibit V; Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 6). Selcer fabricated performance issues contradicted by Faculty B's own email and his own prior testimony (Exhibits X-7, X-8; TRO Tr.). These inconsistent explanations themselves establish the causal link. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271 ("nothing about the McDonnell Douglas formula requires us to ration the evidence between one stage or the other").

**D. Contextual Evidence of Retaliatory Animus**

The employer's motivation for investigating Plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext. *Canada*, No. 20-2747. Here, Simpson's jurisdictional reversal occurred only after Plaintiff demanded compliance in writing, and Sawa's disciplinary threat explicitly referenced Plaintiff's "mass emails" about his concerns—i.e., his protected activity. (Exhibit B-7). "Temporal proximity combined with contextual evidence of retaliatory animus is sufficient to establish causation." *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259 (3d Cir. 2007).

**V. Defendant's Purported Justifications Are Pretextual**

The record demonstrates that Defendant's proffered justifications for its adverse actions are pretextual:

- **"Need to meet" justification:** Simpson and Sawa conditioned supportive measures on in-person meetings, but the Title IX Coordinator's own training materials confirm that supportive measures are "available at any time after a report" and are "non-disciplinary, non-punitive, individualized" and "provided at no cost." (Exhibit Z-c, Slides 38-40). Conditioning supportive measures on meetings contradicts federal regulations and the training Defendant itself received.

- **"No compelling reason" justification:** Selcer claimed Faculty C found "no compelling reason" for Zoom access, but Faculty C had independently authorized Zoom and was unaware he was administering any "compelling reason" test. (Exhibits Q, W). Selcer's explanation was demonstrably false.

- **"Performance issues" justification:** Selcer fabricated performance issues that Faculty B never raised, and Selcer himself testified that Plaintiff had no performance issues. (Exhibits X-7, X-8; TRO Tr.).

- **"No jurisdiction" justification:** Simpson acknowledged jurisdiction on August 13 but reversed 19 hours later on an identical factual record, after receiving protected communications. (Exhibits B-5, B-6). No new facts justified the reversal.

These shifting, inconsistent, and demonstrably false justifications establish that Defendant's proffered reasons are pretextual, and that retaliation was the "likely reason" for the adverse actions. *Carvalho-Grevious*, No. 15-3521; *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271.

**VI. Conclusion**

The record contains overwhelming evidence—including unusually suggestive temporal proximity, a sustained pattern of antagonism, shifting justifications, demonstrable falsehoods, and contextual evidence of retaliatory animus—establishing a prima facie case of retaliation under Title IX. At minimum, genuine disputes of material fact preclude summary judgment. *Doe v. University of the Sciences*, 961 F.3d 203 (3d Cir. 2020) ("to state a claim under Title IX, the alleged facts, if true, must support a plausible inference that a federally-funded college or university discriminated against a person on the basis of sex").

Plaintiff respectfully requests that the Court deny summary judgment on Count II.

**ARGUMENT**

**I. The Legal Standard for Title IX Retaliation**

Title IX retaliation claims are analyzed under the Title VII burden-shifting framework. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247 (3d Cir. 2017). To establish a prima facie case of retaliation, Plaintiff must show: (1) protected activity; (2) adverse action that would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity; and (3) a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse action. *See id.* At the summary judgment stage, Plaintiff need only demonstrate that protected activity was the "likely reason" for the adverse action—not the but-for reason. *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017).

**II. Plaintiff Engaged in Continuous Protected Activity**

Plaintiff engaged in a sustained pattern of protected activity beginning June 30, 2025 and continuing through the present. Exhibits B-0, B-0A, B-3A. "Demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity." *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447 (3d Cir. 2006). Plaintiff need only hold an "objectively reasonable belief, in good faith" that the conduct opposed violates the statute. *Kengerski v. Harper*, No. 20-1307 (3d Cir. 2021).

Plaintiff's protected activity includes: reporting sex-based harassment to Dean Wasilko on June 30 (Exhibit B-0); formally escalating Title IX concerns citing 34 C.F.R. §106.44(a) on July 24 (Exhibit B-0A); filing a complaint with the Office for Civil Rights on July 26 (Exhibit B-3A); filing a federal lawsuit on September 9; filing a TRO motion on October 8; and testifying at the TRO hearing on October 15. Statements of Undisputed Material Facts ("SUMF") ¶ 1. Each of these actions constitutes protected activity because Plaintiff was reasonably opposing conduct he believed violated Title IX. *Kengerski*, No. 20-1307.

**III. Duquesne University Took Multiple Adverse Actions That Would Deter a Reasonable Person**

The record demonstrates a systematic pattern of adverse actions directly tied to Plaintiff's protected activity. "Adverse action" in the retaliation context is defined broadly: it is any action that would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity. *See Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247.

**A. Removal of OCR from Institutional Correspondence**

On July 28, 2025—two days after Plaintiff's OCR filing—Dean Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line on his responsive email. SUMF ¶ 2; Exhibit B-3. Between July 28 and October 2025, four institutional actors (Wasilko, Simpson, Sawa, and others) systematically removed OCR from the CC line on eight or more occasions, each time after Plaintiff restored OCR. SUMF ¶ 3; Exhibits B-3 through B-7 series. No federal authority or Duquesne policy authorized removal of a federal enforcement agency from institutional correspondence. SUMF ¶ 3. This conduct was designed to sever Plaintiff's connection to his federal enforcement channel—a classic deterrent to future protected activity.

**B. Simpson's 19-Hour Jurisdictional Reversal**

On August 13, 2025, at 2:02 PM, Title IX Coordinator Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction using the §106.30 term of art "supportive measures." Exhibit B-5; SUMF ¶ 4. Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM that same day, Plaintiff sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity and zero new facts about the underlying complaint. Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C. On August 14, at 9:42 AM—just nineteen hours later and on an identical factual record—Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination, explicitly disclaiming jurisdiction over sex discrimination complaints. Exhibit B-6; SUMF ¶ 4.

The timing is dispositive. Simpson's reversal occurred immediately after Plaintiff engaged in protected activity—demanding OCR compliance, restoring OCR to the correspondence chain, and citing federal regulations. No new information supported the reversal; the factual record was identical. This is precisely the kind of "clearly unreasonable" response that constitutes retaliation. *See Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629, 648 (1999) (response is deliberately indifferent if "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances").

**C. Sawa's Threat of Disciplinary Charges**

On August 15, 2025, Executive Director of Student Conduct Sawa threatened Plaintiff with disciplinary charges for seeking help from university officials via mass email. Exhibit B-7. Sawa wrote: "you must stop emailing University officials about your concerns" and warned that "failure to do so may result in charges against you for violating the Student Code of Conduct." *Id.* This threat to punish Plaintiff for communicating with university officials about his Title IX concerns is a paradigmatic adverse action—it directly warns that further protected activity will result in punishment.

**D. Selcer's Zoom Revocation and Shifting Justifications**

On October 6, 2025, Professor Faculty C authorized Zoom access for Plaintiff. Exhibit Q, W. On October 8, Plaintiff filed his TRO motion. On October 15, Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing. On October 17—two days after the hearing—Selcer manufactured Zoom revocation. SUMF ¶ 5; Exhibit V, Q. Selcer provided shifting justifications for the denial and ultimately told Colleague A he "can't explain why" Zoom was denied. SUMF ¶ 6. No university or departmental policy constrained Zoom access. SUMF ¶ 6.

The temporal proximity here is "unusually suggestive." *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701, 708 (3d Cir. 1989) (two-day proximity between protected activity and adverse action sufficient to establish causation). Selcer testified under oath at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff could continue attending via Zoom, then revoked that access immediately after the hearing. Exhibit Q. The shifting explanations—first claiming Faculty C found "no compelling reason," then Faculty C stating he was unaware of the prior authorization—demonstrate pretext. *Farrell v. Planters Lifesavers Co.*, 206 F.3d 271, 286 (3d Cir. 2000) ("inconsistent explanations establish causal link").

**E. Blair's Threat of Academic Failure**

In September 2025, Dean Blair threatened Plaintiff with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer's class in person during active federal litigation against Selcer. SUMF ¶ 7; Exhibits K-2, K-5. After Plaintiff disclosed a conflict of interest because Selcer was a named defendant in the federal lawsuit, Blair consulted Selcer and jointly determined that Plaintiff must remain in the course or face failure and delayed graduation. Exhibit K-2. This is a direct threat to Plaintiff's academic standing for engaging in protected litigation activity.

**F. Selcer's Fabricated Performance Issues and Stipend Threat**

On February 13, 2026, Selcer reassigned Plaintiff's TA position and fabricated "performance issues raised by Faculty B." Exhibit X-8; SUMF ¶ 8. Faculty B's own same-day email made no mention of performance issues and described the reassignment request as keeping a "door open" with "no performance complaint." Exhibit X-7. Faculty B's email stated: "I have asked Dan to reassign your TA position (he says this will not affect your funding). My office door is always open to you during my office hours." *Id.* Selcer's email mid-sentence recharacterized "tensions and confrontations" as "performance issues" and threatened Plaintiff's stipend through Spring 2029. Exhibit X-8; SUMF ¶ 8. Critically, Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had "no performance issues." SUMF ¶ 9; TRO Tr. at *:*.

The contrast between Faculty B's actual email and Selcer's mischaracterization—combined with Selcer's prior sworn testimony that no performance issues existed—establishes that the "performance issues" claim was a pretextual justification for retaliation. *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 312 (3d Cir. 1999) (employer acting in bad faith cannot increase responsibilities and then document failures).

**IV. Strong Causal Connection Exists Between Protected Activity and Adverse Actions**

**A. Temporal Proximity**

The temporal proximity between Plaintiff's protected activity and each adverse action is striking:

- **OCR removal (July 28):** Two days after OCR filing (July 26). *See Jalil*, 873 F.2d at 708 (two days sufficient).
- **Sawa threat (August 15):** One day after Simpson's reversal and two days after Plaintiff's protected emails citing §106.44.
- **Zoom revocation (October 17):** Two days after Plaintiff's TRO testimony (October 15) and nine days after TRO filing (October 8). *Id.*
- **Fabricated performance issues (February 13, 2026):** Months after federal litigation began, but in the midst of active litigation. The Supreme Court recognizes that causation can be established through ongoing proximity. *See Price Waterhouse v. Hopkins*, 490 U.S. 228, 252 (1989) (gender played motivating part).

**B. Pattern of Antagonism**

Even if temporal proximity alone were insufficient, the record demonstrates a "pattern of antagonism" following Plaintiff's protected conduct. *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173, 177 (3d Cir. 1997). The pattern includes: (1) systematic removal of OCR from correspondence; (2) Simpson's 19-hour jurisdictional flip; (3) Sawa's threat to charge Plaintiff for emailing officials; (4) Selcer's Zoom revocation with shifting explanations; (5) Blair's academic failure threat; and (6) Selcer's fabricated performance issues and stipend threat. As *Kachmar* holds, "circumstantial evidence of a 'pattern of antagonism' following the protected conduct can also give rise to the inference" of causation. *Id.* at 177.

**C. Shifting Explanations Establish Pretext**

Defendant's shifting, inconsistent explanations for adverse actions independently establish the causal link. "The employer's motivation for investigating the plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext." *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022). Selcer claimed Faculty C found "no compelling reason" for Zoom access, but Faculty C told Plaintiff he was unaware of the prior authorization and confused by Selcer's claim. Exhibit Q, W. Selcer claimed "performance issues raised by Faculty B," but Faculty B's same-day email said the opposite. Exhibits X-7, X-8. These inconsistent explanations create genuine disputes of material fact that preclude summary judgment. *Foster v. Board of Regents of University of Michigan*, No. 19-1314 (6th Cir. 2020) (en banc) (genuine disputes precluded summary judgment where university's response was ineffective).

**V. A Convincing Mosaic of Circumstantial Evidence Supports Causation**

The Third Circuit has held that "a convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence is sufficient to establish causation for retaliation claims." *Canada*, No. 20-2747. The evidence here—viewed "as a whole," *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 286—creates a compelling mosaic:

- Defendant removed OCR from correspondence immediately after OCR was added.
- Simpson acknowledged jurisdiction, then reversed after Plaintiff's protected emails—with no new facts.
- Sawa threatened Plaintiff for emailing about his safety concerns.
- Selcer promised Zoom access under oath, then revoked it two days after Plaintiff's testimony.
- Selcer fabricated performance issues despite Faculty B's explicit statement that no performance complaint existed—and despite Selcer's own prior sworn testimony.

"Temporal proximity combined with contextual evidence of retaliatory animus is sufficient to establish causation." *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259, 267 (3d Cir. 2007). The contextual evidence here includes the coordinated nature of Defendants' actions. Four institutional actors systematically removed OCR from eight or more occasions. SUMF ¶ 3. Blair consulted Selcer before threatening Plaintiff with academic failure. Exhibit K-2. Selcer coordinated with Faculty C to revoke Zoom access. Exhibit V, Q. This pattern of coordination and shifting justifications establishes that the adverse actions were causally connected to Plaintiff's protected activity.

**VI. The Deliberate Indifference Standard Further Supports Plaintiff's Claim**

Under *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629 (1999), a funding recipient is liable where it acts with deliberate indifference to known harassment that denies access to educational opportunities. The Hall court clarified that "[i]t is an intentional violation of Title IX's terms for a funding recipient to act with deliberate indifference to known sexual harassment." *Hall v. Millersville University*, No. 19-3275 (3d Cir. Jan. 11, 2022).

Here, Defendant's response was "clearly unreasonable." *Davis*, 526 U.S. at 648. Defendant had actual knowledge of Plaintiff's harassment and safety concerns. Exhibit B-0 (June 30 report); Exhibit B-3A (July 26 OCR filing). Defendant had guidance through training materials requiring supportive measures to be "non-disciplinary, non-punitive, individualized, and available at no cost." Exhibits Z-c, Z-b. Yet Defendant did the opposite: it retaliated against Plaintiff for seeking those measures. The Title IX training materials explicitly state that supportive measures "must be documented" and are "designed to restore or preserve equal access." Exhibit Z-c at Slide 38-40. Defendant's response—threatening discipline, fabricating performance issues, revoking Zoom access—was the antithesis of a supportive measure.

**VII. Genuine Disputes of Material Fact Preclude Summary Judgment**

Summary judgment is inappropriate where, as here, the evidence creates genuine disputes on material facts. *McAvoy v. Dickinson College*, No. 23-2939 (3d Cir. Aug. 16, 2024) (affirming summary judgment only where no genuine dispute existed). Unlike *McAvoy*, where the court found the university's response—including a no-contact directive, accommodations, and a five-month investigation—was not clearly unreasonable, Defendant here provided none of those measures. Instead, Defendant systematically retaliated.

Whether Defendant's actions would deter a reasonable person is a question for the jury. Whether the causal connection exists is a question for the jury. Whether Defendant's shifting explanations are pretextual is a question for the jury. *Carvalho-Grevious*, No. 15-3521 (reversing summary judgment where plaintiff showed protected activity was "likely reason" for adverse action).

**CONCLUSION**

Plaintiff has established a prima facie case of retaliation under Title IX. He engaged in continuous protected activity. Defendant subjected him to multiple adverse actions—OCR removal, jurisdictional reversal, disciplinary threats, Zoom revocation, academic failure threats, and fabricated performance issues—that would deter any reasonable person. The temporal proximity, pattern of antagonism, and shifting explanations provide overwhelming circumstantial evidence of causation. Genuine disputes of material fact preclude summary judgment, and Count II must proceed to trial.

**I. The McDonnell Douglas Burden-Shifting Framework Governs Title IX Retaliation Claims**

Title IX retaliation claims are analyzed under the Title VII burden-shifting framework. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247 (3d Cir. 2017). To establish a prima facie case of retaliation, Plaintiff must show: (1) protected activity; (2) an adverse action that would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity; and (3) a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse action. *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447, 456 (3d Cir. 2006). At the summary judgment stage, Plaintiff need only show that the protected activity was the “likely reason” for the adverse action—not the but-for reason. *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521, slip op. at 12 (3d Cir. 2017). This low threshold is satisfied here.

**II. Plaintiff Engaged in Continuous Protected Activity**

Protected activity includes “demanding compliance with Title IX.” *Atkinson*, 460 F.3d at 456. Each of these acts qualifies as protected activity. Defendants do not—and cannot—dispute that Plaintiff engaged in protected activity. The sole issue is whether Duquesne took materially adverse actions and whether a causal link exists.

**III. Duquesne Took Multiple Materially Adverse Actions That Would Deter a Reasonable Person**

An adverse action in the retaliation context is one that “might have dissuaded a reasonable [person] from making or supporting a charge of discrimination.” *Farrell v. Planters Lifesavers Co.*, 206 F.3d 271, 279 (3d Cir. 2000). The undisputed record reveals at least eight distinct adverse actions:

**A. Systematic Removal of OCR from Institutional Correspondence (July 28–October 2025)**

On July 28, 2025—two days after Plaintiff filed his OCR complaint—Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line on a responsive email. (Ex. B-3.) Over the following months, four institutional actors (Wasilko, Simpson, Sawa, and others) systematically removed OCR on eight or more occasions, each time after Plaintiff restored OCR. (Exs. B-3 through B-7 series.) No federal authority or Duquesne policy authorizes removal of a federal enforcement agency from institutional correspondence. This removal was designed to silence Plaintiff’s federal oversight and constitutes an adverse action that would deter any reasonable person from continuing to report.

**B. Simpson’s 19-Hour Jurisdiction Flip (August 13–14, 2025)**

On August 13, 2025 at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction using the § 106.30 term of art “supportive measures.” (Ex. B-5.) That same afternoon, Plaintiff sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity and zero new facts about the underlying complaint. (Exs. B-5A, B-5B, B-5C.) On August 14 at 9:42 AM—nineteen hours later—Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination on an identical factual record, stating the alleged conduct “does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31’s definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true.” (Ex. B-6.) No new facts were provided between the two emails. The only intervening event was Plaintiff’s insistence on copying OCR and his demand for immediate supportive measures. This reversal is classic retaliatory obstruction: acknowledging jurisdiction, then withdrawing it immediately after the complainant demands compliance.

**C. Sawa’s Threat of Conduct Charges for Continued Reporting (August 15, 2025)**

On August 15, 2025, Executive Director of Student Conduct Anne Sawa threatened Plaintiff with disciplinary charges if he continued emailing university officials about his concerns. (Ex. B-7: “If you want assistance from the University, you are expected to communicate directly with the resources offered to you and to do so in a respectful and civil manner. Failure to do so may result in charges against you for violating the Student Code of Conduct.”) This threat—issued one day after Simpson’s jurisdiction disclaimer—is a textbook example of retaliation: punishing the complainant for engaging in protected speech.

**D. Selcer’s Zoom Revocation Immediately Following TRO Hearing (October 17, 2025)**

On October 6, 2025, Professor Faculty C directly authorized Zoom access for Plaintiff. (Ex. W, email sequence 1-2.) On October 8, Plaintiff filed a TRO motion. On October 15, Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing. On October 17—two days after the hearing—Selcer unilaterally revoked the Zoom authorization, claiming Faculty C had found “no compelling reason” to continue it. (Ex. V.) But Faculty C himself later expressed confusion, stating he was unaware of any such “compelling reason” test and that he thought Zoom was only for one day. (Ex. W, email 5.) **E. Selcer’s Shifting Justifications for Zoom Denial**

No university or departmental policy constrains Zoom access; indeed, other professors have maintained Zoom availability. (Ex. N, Faculty D syllabus: “I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely.”) Shifting justifications are independently evidence of pretext. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 286 (inconsistent explanations support inference of retaliation).

**F. Blair’s Threat of Academic Failure During Active Litigation (September–October 2025)**

In September 2025, Dean Blair threatened Plaintiff with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer’s class in person during active federal litigation against Selcer. (Exs. K-1, K-2, K-5.) When Plaintiff raised a conflict of interest—Selcer was a named defendant—Blair consulted Selcer himself and then announced their joint determination that no conflict existed and Plaintiff must return to class or fail. (Ex. K-2.) This is retaliation by design: forcing a litigant to submit to instruction by his adversary.

**G. Selcer’s Fabrication of “Performance Issues” and Stipend Threat (February 13, 2026)**

On February 5, 2026, Faculty B emailed Plaintiff that she was “upset by what happened” and asked him not to attend class. (Ex. X-4.) Faculty B’s email contains no allegation of performance issues. At a February 10 meeting, Selcer used no “performance issues” language, confirmed that Faculty B did not control Plaintiff’s stipend, and maintained a collegial tone. (Ex. X-6.) On February 13, Faculty B requested reassignment but explicitly stated “this will not affect your funding” and “my office door is always open”—again, no performance complaint. (Ex. X-7.)

Later that same day, Selcer sent a reassignment letter that, for the first time, introduced the phrase “performance issues raised by two of the three faculty members,” and threatened that future “significant performance issues” could result in suspension of Plaintiff’s stipend through Spring 2029. (Ex. X-8.) Faculty B’s own same-day email contradicts Selcer’s characterization. Selcer fabricated a performance problem to justify punitive action after months of Plaintiff’s protected activity. *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747, slip op. at 8 (3d Cir. 2022) (employer’s motivation for investigating plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext).

**H. Wasilko’s Denial of Housing Assistance Despite Available Resources (July 24, 2025)**

Wasilko told Plaintiff “the only rooms we have are in the first year buildings. Obviously, that is not helpful at this juncture.” (Ex. G.) Yet the University had available housing and declined to provide it. This denial of supportive measures—mandated by 34 C.F.R. § 106.30—was an adverse action that exacerbated Plaintiff’s homelessness and deterred him from seeking further assistance.

**IV. Strong Circumstantial Evidence Establishes the Requisite Causal Connection**

At the prima facie stage, “the proffered evidence, looked at as a whole, may suffice to raise the inference” of causation. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 281. Temporal proximity, pattern of antagonism, and pretextual explanations all support the inference here.

**A. Unusually Suggestive Temporal Proximity**

The two-day gap between the TRO hearing (October 15) and Zoom revocation (October 17) is “unusually suggestive” and alone can establish causation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701, 708 (3d Cir. 1989). Similarly, the five-day gap between Plaintiff’s August 13 protected emails and Simpson’s jurisdiction reversal (Exs. B-5A through B-6) supports an inference of retaliation. *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259, 267 (3d Cir. 2007) (temporal proximity plus contextual evidence sufficient).

**B. Pattern of Antagonism**

The record reveals an escalating pattern of antagonism that directly parallels Plaintiff’s protected activity:

- **June 30**: Protected activity → Wasilko dismisses report. (Ex. B-0.)
- **July 23-24**: Gender-reversal hypothetical → Wasilko denies housing. (Exs. B-0A, G.)
- **July 26**: OCR filing → Two days later, Wasilko removes OCR from CC. (Ex. B-3.)
- **August 13**: Plaintiff restores OCR, demands supportive measures → Nineteen hours later, Simpson disclaims jurisdiction. (Exs. B-5A, B-6.)
- **August 15**: Sawa threatens conduct charges. (Ex. B-7.)
- **October 15**: TRO testimony → October 17: Selcer revokes Zoom. (Exs. Q, V.)
- **February 13, 2026**: Fabrication of performance issues and stipend threat. (Exs. X-7, X-8.)

A “pattern of antagonism” following the protected conduct independently establishes the causal link. *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173, 178 (3d Cir. 1997).

**C. Pretextual and Shifting Explanations**

Selcer provided shifting justifications for the Zoom denial. (Exs. V, W; SJ 7.3.) Simpson reversed jurisdiction on an identical factual record after Plaintiff’s protected emails. (Exs. B-5, B-6.) Selcer fabricated “performance issues” that Faculty B herself never raised. (Exs. X-7, X-8.) These inconsistencies are powerful evidence of pretext. *Canada*, slip op. at 8; *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 286.

**D. Convincing Mosaic of Circumstantial Evidence**

“A convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence is sufficient to establish causation for retaliation claims.” *Canada*, slip op. at 8. The mosaic here includes: (1) temporal proximity on multiple occasions; (2) a pattern of antagonism escalating with each protected act; (3) shifting and contradictory justifications; (4) removal of OCR from communications; (5) fabrication of performance problems; and (6) threats of disciplinary action and stipend suspension. This evidence, taken together, more than satisfies Plaintiff’s burden at the prima facie stage.

**V. Conclusion**

Plaintiff has presented undisputed evidence of continuous protected activity, multiple adverse actions that would deter a reasonable person, and a strong causal connection supported by temporal proximity, pattern of antagonism, and pretext. Because genuine disputes of material fact exist on each element of the retaliation claim, summary judgment must be denied.

Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447 (3d Cir. 2006), as well as reporting sex-based harassment, filing complaints with the Office for Civil Rights, filing a federal lawsuit, and testifying at a hearing. On July 26, he filed a complaint with the Office for Civil Rights (OCR) (Ex. B-3A). On September 9, he filed this federal lawsuit; on October 8, he moved for a temporary restraining order; and on October 15, he testified at the TRO hearing. (ECF docket.) This continuous chain of protected activity—spanning more than 90 days—is undisputed. Each instance constituted a demand for compliance with Title IX and opposition to what Plaintiff reasonably believed to be sex discrimination. *Atkinson*, 460 F.3d at 447; *Kengerski*.

**II. Duquesne Took Multiple Adverse Actions That Would Deter a Reasonable Person**

An adverse action in the retaliation context is one that “well might have dissuaded a reasonable [person] from making or supporting a charge of discrimination.” *Farrell v. Planters Lifesavers Co.*, 206 F.3d 271 (3d Cir. 2000). The record is replete with such actions:

- *OCR Exclusion from Communications:* On July 28—two days after Plaintiff’s OCR filing—Dean Wasilko removed OCR from the CC line on his email (Ex. B-3). Between July 28 and October 2025, four institutional actors (Wasilko, Simpson, Sawa, and others) systematically removed OCR from the CC line on eight or more occasions, each time after Plaintiff restored OCR. No policy or authority justified this removal (Ex. B-3 through B-7). A reasonable person would be deterred from continuing to involve a federal enforcement agency if the university repeatedly excised it from official correspondence.

- *Simpson’s 19-Hour Jurisdictional Reversal:* On August 13 at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction and used the term of art “supportive measures” (Ex. B-5). Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM that same day, Plaintiff sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity—citing federal regulations, demanding compliance, and copying OCR—and zero new facts about the underlying complaint (Ex. B-5A, B-5B, B-5C). On August 14 at 9:42 AM—just 19 hours later—Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination on an identical factual record (Ex. B-6). The temporal proximity of Plaintiff’s protected emails to the reversal alone supports an inference of retaliation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701 (3d Cir. 1989) (two-day proximity sufficient). No reasonable person would continue to press Title IX claims if the coordinator disclaims jurisdiction immediately after receiving protected communications.

- *Selcer’s Zoom Revocation:* On October 6, Professor Faculty C authorized Zoom access for Plaintiff (Ex. Q). On October 8, Plaintiff filed the TRO motion. On October 15, he testified at the TRO hearing. On October 17—just two days after the hearing—Selcer manufactured a revocation, claiming Faculty C found “no compelling reason” to continue remote attendance (Ex. V). Faculty C himself stated he had authorized Zoom for the semester and was confused by Selcer’s intervention (Ex. W). The revocation occurred within 48 hours of Plaintiff’s testimony and within days of the TRO filing—a classic “unusually suggestive” temporal proximity. *Jalil*, 873 F.2d at 701.

- *Blair’s Threat of Academic Failure:* In September 2025, Dean Blair threatened Plaintiff with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer’s class in person, despite knowing that Selcer was a named defendant in the pending federal litigation (Ex. K-2, K-5). Forcing a plaintiff to sit in a classroom taught by the person he is suing under Title IX—and threatening his academic standing if he refuses—is an obvious deterrent to further litigation.

- *Selcer’s Fabricated Performance Issues and Stipend Threat:* On February 13, 2026, Selcer reassigned Plaintiff’s TA position and, for the first time, alleged “performance issues raised by Faculty B” (Ex. X-8). Yet Faculty B’s own same-day email made no mention of performance issues and described the reassignment as keeping a “door open” with “no performance complaint” (Ex. X-7). Selcer’s email mid-sentence recharacterized “tensions and confrontations” as “performance issues” and threatened Plaintiff’s stipend through Spring 2029 (Ex. X-8). At the TRO hearing, Selcer had testified under oath that Plaintiff had no performance issues (TRO Tr.). The discrepancy is powerful evidence that the performance rationale was manufactured in retaliation for Plaintiff’s ongoing litigation—a quintessential adverse action.

Each of these actions—individually and collectively—would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271.

**III. Strong Causal Connection Exists Between Plaintiff’s Protected Activity and Each Adverse Action**

Causation can be established through “unusually suggestive” temporal proximity, *Jalil*, 873 F.2d at 701, a “pattern of antagonism” following protected conduct, *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173 (3d Cir. 1997), or “a convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence,” *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022). At the prima facie stage, Plaintiff “need only show that the protected activity was the likely reason for the adverse action.” *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017).

- *Temporal Proximity:* The OCR removal occurred two days after the OCR filing (Ex. B-3, B-3A). The jurisdictional reversal occurred 19 hours after Plaintiff’s protected emails (Ex. B-5, B-5A, B-5C, B-6). The Zoom revocation occurred two days after the TRO hearing (Ex. V, W; ECF docket). This pattern of near-instantaneous retaliation following each escalation is sufficient to raise an inference of causation. *Jalil*, 873 F.2d at 701; *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259 (3d Cir. 2007) (temporal proximity plus contextual evidence sufficient).

- *Pattern of Antagonism:* The record shows a systematic campaign of retaliation. Every time Plaintiff invoked OCR or federal court, institutional actors responded with a new adverse action: first removing OCR, then disclaiming jurisdiction, then threatening conduct charges, then revoking Zoom, then fabricating performance issues. This uninterrupted “pattern of antagonism” independently establishes a causal link. *Kachmar*, 109 F.3d at 173.

- *Pretext and Shifting Justifications:* Selcer’s justifications for the Zoom revocation shifted and ultimately collapsed: he told Plaintiff that Faculty C found “no compelling reason,” but Faculty C had already authorized Zoom and expressed confusion (Ex. V, W). Selcer later told Colleague A he “can’t explain why” Zoom was denied (SJ 7.3). Similarly, Selcer fabricated “performance issues” from a supervisor who expressly disclaimed any performance complaint (Ex. X-7, X-8). The “employer’s motivation for investigating the plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext.” *Canada*, No. 20-2747. When “the proffered evidence, looked at as a whole,” suffices to raise the inference of causation, summary judgment must be denied. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271.

**IV. Defendant Cannot Satisfy Its Burden of Articulating a Legitimate, Non-Retaliatory Reason; Even If It Could, the Reasons Are Pretextual**

Under the Title VII framework adopted for Title IX retaliation, *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, once Plaintiff establishes a prima facie case, the burden shifts to Defendant to articulate a legitimate, non-retaliatory reason for the adverse actions. Defendant cannot meet that burden here. There is no legitimate reason to remove a federal enforcement agency from institutional correspondence. There is no legitimate reason to reverse a jurisdictional determination 19 hours after receiving protected emails that added no new facts. There is no legitimate reason to revoke Zoom access two days after a plaintiff testifies at a TRO hearing, especially when the instructor who authorized it never withdrew that authorization.

Even if Defendant attempts to proffer reasons—such as “policy compliance” or “performance issues”—the record demonstrates pretext. Simpson’s claim that Plaintiff needed to meet before she could act is contradicted by her own August 13 email acknowledging jurisdiction (Ex. B-5). Selcer’s claim of “performance issues” is directly contradicted by his own testimony and Faculty B’s email (Ex. X-7; TRO Tr.). Where “inconsistent explanations” exist, a jury can infer retaliatory motive. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 271. The same is true for the shifting justifications for Zoom denial (Ex. V, W). “A convincing mosaic of circumstantial evidence” precludes summary judgment. *Canada*.

**CONCLUSION**

Plaintiff has presented undisputed evidence of continuous protected activity, a pattern of adverse actions that would deter any reasonable person, and a causal connection established by temporal proximity, a pattern of antagonism, and overwhelming evidence of pretext. Duquesne University is not entitled to summary judgment on Count II.

# ARGUMENT

Count II of Plaintiff's complaint alleges that Duquesne University retaliated against him for engaging in protected activity under Title IX, 20 U.S.C. § 1681. Title IX retaliation claims are analyzed under the Title VII burden-shifting framework. *Doe v. Mercy Catholic Medical Center*, No. 16-1247 (3d Cir. 2017). To establish a prima facie case of retaliation, a plaintiff must show: (1) protected activity; (2) an adverse action that would deter a reasonable person from engaging in protected activity; and (3) a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse action. *Id.* At the summary judgment stage, Plaintiff need only show that protected activity was the "likely reason" for the adverse action, not the but-for cause. *Carvalho-Grevious v. Delaware State University*, No. 15-3521 (3d Cir. 2017). Direct evidence is not required; a "convincing mosaic" of circumstantial evidence suffices. *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022); *Desert Palace, Inc. v. Costa*, 539 U.S. 90 (2003).

## I. Plaintiff Engaged in Extensive Protected Activity

Plaintiff engaged in continuous protected activity from June 30, 2025 through the present. Protected activity under Title IX includes reporting sex-based harassment, demanding compliance with Title IX, and participating in Title IX enforcement proceedings. *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447 (3d Cir. 2006) ("Demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity"). A plaintiff need only hold an "objectively reasonable belief, in good faith" that the conduct opposed violates the statute; an actual violation is not required. *Kengerski v. Harper*, No. 20-1307 (3d Cir. 2021).

Plaintiff's protected activity includes, at a minimum:

- **June 30, 2025:** Plaintiff reported sex-based harassment and sought emergency housing from Dean Wasilko, describing being followed and intimidated by a female roommate. (Exhibit B-0.)
- **July 23, 2025:** Plaintiff sent the "Gender Reversal Hypothetical" email explicitly questioning whether the university's response would have been different had the genders been reversed, squarely raising a sex discrimination concern. (Exhibit B-0A.)
- **July 24, 2025:** Plaintiff formally escalated to Title IX Coordinator Simpson citing § 106.44(a) and demanding compliance. (Exhibit B-1.)
- **July 26, 2025:** Plaintiff filed a formal complaint with the U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights (OCR), alleging Title IX violations and retaliation. (Exhibit B-3A.)
- **September 9, 2025:** Plaintiff filed the instant federal lawsuit.
- **October 8, 2025:** Plaintiff filed a motion for Temporary Restraining Order.
- **October 15, 2025:** Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing.

Each of these actions constitutes protected activity because they involved reporting sex discrimination, demanding Title IX compliance, or participating in federal enforcement proceedings. Plaintiff's good-faith belief that the university's responses were inadequate and motivated by sex is objectively reasonable given the record.

## II. Defendant Took Multiple Adverse Actions That Would Deter a Reasonable Person

An adverse action in the retaliation context is one that "might have dissuaded a reasonable worker from making or supporting a charge of discrimination." Here, the university took a series of actions escalating over time, each designed to punish Plaintiff for pursuing his Title IX rights and to deter future protected activity.

### A. Systematic Removal of OCR from Institutional Correspondence

On July 28, 2025 — just two days after Plaintiff filed his OCR complaint (Exhibit B-3A) — Dean Wasilko sent an email in which OCR was conspicuously absent from the CC line. (Exhibit B-3.) Over the following months, four institutional actors (Wasilko, Simpson, Sawa, and others) systematically removed OCR from the CC line on eight or more occasions, each time after Plaintiff restored OCR. No federal authority or Duquesne policy authorizes removal of a federal enforcement agency from institutional correspondence. This conduct is facially retaliatory: it seeks to cut Plaintiff off from the very agency charged with enforcing Title IX, thereby chilling his ability to seek federal oversight.

### B. Simpson's 19-Hour Jurisdiction Flip

On August 13, 2025, at 2:02 PM, Simpson acknowledged Title IX jurisdiction, using the § 106.30 term of art "supportive measures" and convening a multi-departmental response. (Exhibit B-5.) Between 4:20 PM and 7:31 PM that same day, Plaintiff sent three emails consisting entirely of protected activity — demanding compliance, citing regulatory provisions, and restoring OCR to the CC line — and containing zero new facts about the underlying complaint. (Exhibits B-5A, B-5B, B-5C.) The next morning, August 14, at 9:42 AM, Simpson reversed her jurisdictional determination on an identical factual record. (Exhibit B-6.) The gap between acknowledgment and reversal was nineteen hours, and the only intervening events were Plaintiff's protected emails. This is a textbook example of pretext: the university punished Plaintiff for demanding his rights by withdrawing Title IX protections that had already been acknowledged.

### C. Sawa's Retaliation Threat

On August 15, 2025 — one day after Simpson's jurisdiction disclaimer — Sawa, the Executive Director of Student Conduct, threatened Plaintiff with disciplinary charges if he continued emailing university officials about his safety and access concerns. (Exhibit B-7.) Sawa wrote: "you must stop sending mass emails to University officials and offices about your concerns. . . . Failure to do so may result in charges against you for violating the Student Code of Conduct." This express threat of punishment for engaging in protected speech is per se retaliatory. The Third Circuit has recognized that threats of discipline for protected activity constitute adverse action. *Canada*, slip op. at 6-7.

### D. Selcer's Zoom Revocation

On October 6, 2025, Professor Faculty C independently authorized Zoom access for Plaintiff. (Exhibit Q.) On October 8, Plaintiff filed the TRO motion. On October 15, Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing. On October 17 — just two days after Plaintiff's testimony — Selcer manufactured Zoom revocation. (Exhibits V, W.) Selcer claimed he "discussed your remote attendance of his graduate seminar with Faculty C and he did not identify a compelling reason to continue it." (Exhibit V.)
