But Faculty C, when later contacted, was confused, stating he thought he had authorized Zoom only for one day for another student. (Exhibit W.) Selcer provided shifting justifications for the denial and ultimately told another administrator he "can't explain why" Zoom was denied. No university or departmental policy constrains Zoom access. (Exhibit L; SJ 7.3.) The temporal proximity to Plaintiff's TRO testimony — two days — is unusually suggestive and alone establishes causation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701 (3d Cir. 1989) (two-day proximity sufficient).

### E. Blair's Threat of Academic Failure

In September 2025, Dean Blair threatened Plaintiff with academic failure if he did not attend Selcer's class in person during active federal litigation against Selcer. (SAC ¶¶ 66, 76, 336.) Blair consulted Selcer before issuing her determination (Exhibit K-2), demonstrating coordination between the decision-makers. She then denied that TAP 33 (conflict of interest policy) applied, refusing to permit Plaintiff to fulfill the requirement through an alternate instructor. (Exhibit K-5.) This threat was designed to force Plaintiff to choose between his safety and his academic standing — precisely the kind of coercive pressure that deters protected activity.

### F. Selcer's Fabrication of Performance Issues and Stipend Threat

On February 13, 2026, Selcer reassigned Plaintiff's TA position and, for the first time, fabricated "performance issues raised by Faculty B." (Exhibit X-8.) Faculty B's own same-day email (Exhibit X-7) made no mention of performance issues and described the reassignment request as keeping a "door open" with "no performance complaint." Selcer's email mid-sentence recharacterized "tensions and confrontations" as "performance issues" and threatened Plaintiff's stipend through Spring 2029. (Exhibit X-8.) Critically, Selcer had testified under oath at the TRO hearing just months earlier that Plaintiff had no performance issues. (TRO Tr. at *:*.) And in a February 10, 2026 meeting — just three days before the reassignment letter — Selcer used no "performance issues" language and confirmed that Faculty B had no authority over Plaintiff's stipend. (Exhibit X-6.) The sudden invention of "performance issues" immediately after Plaintiff continued to pursue his federal lawsuit is a textbook retaliatory fabrication. The Third Circuit has held that an employer's motivation for investigating a plaintiff is itself evidence of pretext. *Canada*, slip op. at 7-8.

All of these actions would deter a reasonable person from continuing to pursue Title IX protections. The systematic removal of OCR from correspondence, the threat of disciplinary charges, the withdrawal of already-authorized safety measures, the threat of academic failure, and the fabrication of performance issues with stipend implications are each independently sufficient to constitute adverse action. Taken together, they demonstrate a campaign of retaliation.

## III. Strong Causal Connection Exists Between Protected Activity and Adverse Actions

Causation may be established through temporal proximity, a pattern of antagonism, inconsistent explanations, or the "convincing mosaic" of circumstantial evidence as a whole. *Farrell v. Planters Lifesavers Co.*, 206 F.3d 271 (3d Cir. 2000); *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173 (3d Cir. 1997); *Canada*, slip op. at 6. At the prima facie stage, the plaintiff need only show that protected activity was the "likely reason" for the adverse action. *Carvalho-Grevious*, slip op. at 8.

### A. Temporal Proximity Is Unusually Suggestive

Multiple adverse actions occurred within hours or days of protected activity:

- **OCR removal (July 28):** Two days after Plaintiff filed his OCR complaint (July 26). (Exhibits B-3, B-3A.)
- **Simpson's jurisdiction reversal (August 14):** Nineteen hours after Plaintiff sent three protected emails (August 13, 4:20-7:31 PM). (Exhibits B-5, B-5A, B-5B, B-5C, B-6.)
- **Sawa's retaliation threat (August 15):** One day after Simpson's reversal and twenty-two days after Plaintiff began formally escalating. (Exhibit B-7.)
- **Zoom revocation (October 17):** Two days after Plaintiff testified at the TRO hearing (October 15). (Exhibits Q, V, W; ECF docket.)
- **Selcer's fabricated performance issues (February 13, 2026):** Four months after the lawsuit was filed and while litigation was ongoing; the fabrication occurred immediately after Plaintiff continued to press his claims.

Two-day temporal proximity is sufficient alone to establish causation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701 (3d Cir. 1989); *Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis*, 480 F.3d 259 (3d Cir. 2007) ("Temporal proximity combined with contextual evidence of retaliatory animus is sufficient to establish causation").

### B. Pattern of Antagonism and Inconsistent Explanations

Even if temporal proximity were insufficient — and it is — the pattern of antagonism independently establishes causation. *Kachmar v. SunGard Data Systems, Inc.*, 109 F.3d 173 (3d Cir. 1997) ("circumstantial evidence of a 'pattern of antagonism' following the protected conduct can also give rise to the inference"). From the moment Plaintiff filed his OCR complaint, the university's tone shifted from passive inaction to active retaliation. The university began removing OCR from correspondence, reversing prior jurisdictional findings, threatening disciplinary charges, and ultimately fabricating performance issues.

The shifting justifications for the Zoom denial — Selcer first claiming Faculty C found "no compelling reason," then Faculty C being unaware of any such determination, and Selcer ultimately admitting he "can't explain why" — demonstrate pretext. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 286-87 (inconsistent explanations establish causal link). Similarly, Selcer's invention of "performance issues" directly contradicts Faculty B's own email stating there was "no performance complaint" and Selcer's own prior testimony that Plaintiff had no performance issues. These inconsistencies are powerful circumstantial evidence of retaliatory motive. *Canada*, slip op. at 7-8.

### C. The Convincing Mosaic of Evidence

Looking at the evidence as a whole, a clear picture emerges: Plaintiff began reporting sex-based harassment and demanding Title IX compliance; within days, the university's tone shifted to obstruction and retaliation; and the retaliation escalated with each new instance of protected activity. The record includes multiple institutional actors coordinating to remove a federal enforcement agency from correspondence, reverse jurisdictional determinations without factual change, threaten discipline for contacting officials, withdraw authorized safety measures immediately after court testimony, and fabricate performance deficiencies to justify punitive action.

This "convincing mosaic" of circumstantial evidence — temporal proximity, pattern of antagonism, shifting justifications, and inconsistent explanations — is more than sufficient to permit a reasonable jury to find that Plaintiff's protected activity was a motivating factor in the university's adverse actions. *Canada*, slip op. at 6; *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 281 ("the proffered evidence, looked at as a whole, may suffice to raise the inference").

## IV. Defendant Cannot Satisfy Its Burden at the Summary Judgment Stage

Because Plaintiff has established a prima facie case of retaliation, the burden shifts to Defendant to articulate a legitimate, non-retaliatory reason for its actions. Even assuming Defendant could proffer such reasons, the record is replete with evidence of pretext. The shifting justifications for Zoom revocation, the false claim that Faculty C found "no compelling reason," the contradiction between Faculty B's "no performance complaint" email and Selcer's "performance issues" fabrication, and the temporal proximity of each adverse action to Plaintiff's protected activity all permit a reasonable jury to disbelieve Defendant's proffered reasons and find retaliation. *Farrell*, 206 F.3d at 286; *Canada*, slip op. at 8.

## CONCLUSION

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff has established genuine disputes of material fact on each element of his Title IX retaliation claim. Summary judgment must be denied as to Count II.

**ARGUMENT**

Plaintiff David Mullins satisfies all five elements of a failure-to-accommodate claim under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, and the Americans with Disabilities Act, as incorporated through the same substantive standard. The Supreme Court recently confirmed in *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025), that "schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims related to their education are not required to make a heightened showing of 'bad faith or gross misjudgment' but instead are subject to the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts." This Court therefore applies ordinary failure-to-accommodate analysis.

**A. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in good academic standing with no disciplinary record. (TRO Tr. at *:*; SJ 7.2.) Treating Physician, Plaintiff's treating physician, has diagnosed Plaintiff with ADHD and depression, and has documented that these conditions are "likely worsens [sic] his mental health conditions" and that "worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (Exhibit F, Oct 1 Letter.) Plaintiff is therefore an individual with a disability within the meaning of Section 504 and the ADA.

**B. Defendant Had Adequate Notice of Plaintiff's Disability and Need for Accommodation**

Under *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296 (3d Cir. 1999), "an employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process." The same standard applies in the educational setting for Section 504 claims. *Osseo*, 605 U.S. ___.

Plaintiff provided Defendant with medical documentation from Treating Physician stating: "I am a physician treating David Mullins. He's informed me he does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus, and from what he's described to me, I believe its reasonable for him to have this fear." (Exhibit F, Aug 13 Letter.) This documentation was provided directly to Defendant. Treating Physician further recommended "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing be made" to "benefit and protect his mental health and his performance in his graduate program." (Exhibit F, Oct 1 Letter.)

Defendant's receipt of this documentation is not disputed. Indeed, Defendant's own departmental chair, Dr. Daniel Selcer, acknowledged Plaintiff's medical concerns and conditioned continued Zoom access on Plaintiff's engagement with university offices. (Exhibit I, Aug 25 Email; Exhibit J, Sep 3 Email.) This constitutes actual knowledge of Plaintiff's disability-related limitations and the need for accommodation.

**C. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation**

On multiple occasions, Plaintiff requested Zoom access for class participation as a medically necessary accommodation. (Exhibit I, Aug 25 Email; Exhibit I-1, Sep 2 Email; Exhibit W, Oct 6 Email.) The requested accommodation—remote attendance via Zoom—was neither unprecedented nor operationally burdensome. Faculty D in the same Philosophy Department already "keep[s] a Zoom meeting running" for comparable graduate coursework, instructing students that "if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely." (Exhibit N, Faculty D Syllabus.) No university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access; the Faculty Handbook governs "faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations." (Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11; SJ 7.3.)

The *Oross* court confronted an analogous situation: "University applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request." *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023). Here, as in *Oross*, Defendant had no written policy prohibiting the requested accommodation—and indeed, Zoom was already in active use in the same department.

**D. Defendant Denied the Reasonable Accommodation Through Its Refusal to Engage in the Interactive Process and Ultimate Termination of Zoom Access**

Defendant's response to Plaintiff's accommodation request failed every requirement of the interactive process established under *Taylor*:

**1. No Individualized Assessment Was Conducted**

Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing that Zoom "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding—but "could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial." (TRO Tr. at *:*.) This is precisely the type of ad hoc, unsubstantiated denial that *Oross* found to be "interference with the interactive process" that rendered the accommodation request "dead on arrival." *Oross*, No. 21-5032.

**2. No Interactive Process Occurred**

As the undisputed facts establish, "no interactive process was conducted. Defendants did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services." (SJ 7.2.) Under *Taylor*, a defendant who has "received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations." 184 F.3d at 312.

**3. The Accommodation Provided Was Conditional, Time-Limited, and Retaliatory**

On August 25, 2025, Selcer "imposed a three-week countdown on Plaintiff's Zoom access, after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person regardless of medical documentation." (Exhibit I, Aug 25 Email; Exhibit J, Sep 3 Email.) This was "a conditional, time-limited measure designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance." (Exhibit I-1, Sep 2 Email.) A conditional accommodation that requires the disabled individual to forfeit their accommodation after a fixed period is no accommodation at all—it is a temporary reprieve that preserves the underlying denial.

**4. The Accommodation Was Ultimately Terminated Without Basis**

On October 17, 2025, after Plaintiff had been attending via Zoom with Professor Faculty C's direct authorization (Exhibit W, Oct 6 Email), Selcer unilaterally terminated remote access, claiming Faculty C "did not identify a compelling reason to continue it." (Exhibit V; Exhibit Q.) However, Faculty C himself was "confused, unaware of prior authorization," and had never been asked to evaluate a "compelling reason" test. (Exhibit Q, Oct 17 Email; Exhibit W, Oct 17 Email.) Selcer "went to a professor who had already authorized Zoom, reframed it as a 'compelling reason' test Faculty C never knew he was administering, and reported the result as Faculty C's independent judgment." (Exhibit Q.)

**5. Defendant Demonstrated a Pattern of Denial with No Articulable Basis**

Colleague A, another student in Selcer's Graduate Teaching Seminar, separately requested Zoom access. Selcer "denied her request and told her he 'can't explain why' Zoom was not available—demonstrating a pattern of denial with no articulable basis." (Exhibit L.) This pattern confirms that Defendant's denial was not based on individualized assessment but on an unwritten, ad hoc preference for in-person attendance that effectively functioned as a blanket prohibition—precisely the type of conduct *Oross* found unlawful.

Under *Taylor*, the Court examines whether "the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations." 184 F.3d at 312. Here, Defendant made no effort whatsoever. It imposed a time-limited conditional access, refused to engage in any interactive process, and ultimately terminated access through an improper process that circumvented the professor who had authorized the accommodation.

**E. Plaintiff Suffered Resulting Harm**

Plaintiff's ability to participate in and benefit from his education was directly impaired by Defendant's denial of his requested accommodation. Treating Physician documented that encountering his abuser on campus "would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." (Exhibit F, Aug 13 Letter.) External Faculty Witness confirmed that Plaintiff "cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress and require hours of recovery time." (Exhibit C, Supplemental Declaration.)

On September 17, 2025, Plaintiff's fear was realized: "Respondent had been waiting outside his Philosophy seminar classroom." (Exhibit C, Facebook Messenger Conversation.) Plaintiff's delayed response of "better now" to Professor External Faculty Witness's inquiry "made [External Faculty Witness] concerned that the encounter had been more serious than [Plaintiff's] initial brief report suggested." (Exhibit C, Supplemental Declaration.)

Plaintiff was ultimately removed from his classes, unable to attend in person due to safety concerns and unable to attend remotely due to Defendant's denial of accommodation. (Exhibit Q; Exhibit V; Exhibit W.) Under *Taylor*, "an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations." 184 F.3d at 315. The same standard applies here: Plaintiff could have continued his education with the simple accommodation of Zoom access—an accommodation already in use in his own department—but for Defendant's refusal to provide it.

**Conclusion**

Defendant failed to engage in any interactive process, denied a reasonable accommodation that was already available in the same department, and ultimately terminated that accommodation through a procedurally improper process. Plaintiff has established each element of a failure-to-accommodate claim under Section 504 and the ADA. *See Osseo*, 605 U.S. ___; *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312; *Oross*, No. 21-5032.

**ARGUMENT**

**A. Legal Standard**

Under the Supreme Court's recent decision in *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025), claims brought under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and the ADA in educational settings are subject to the same ordinary failure-to-accommodate analysis that applies in other disability discrimination contexts. No heightened showing of "bad faith or gross misjudgment" is required. *Id.* (Key Passage 1). "Nothing in the text of Title II of the ADA or Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act suggests that such claims should be subject to a distinct, more demanding analysis." *Id.* (Key Passage 2). Accordingly, Plaintiff David Mullins need only establish the standard elements of a failure-to-accommodate claim: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) Defendant Duquesne University received adequate notice of his disability and his need for an accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) the accommodation was denied; and (5) he suffered harm as a result.

**B. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

Plaintiff's treating physician, Treating Physician, has diagnosed Plaintiff with ADHD and depression—conditions that qualify as disabilities under the ADA and Section 504. (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2, Oct. 1, 2025). Treating Physician attests that these conditions "likely worsen[]" due to ongoing stressors, and that "worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (*Id.*) The University does not contest that Plaintiff is a qualified individual; indeed, Defendant Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had no performance issues and was in good academic standing. (TRO Tr. at *:*; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 2.) Plaintiff therefore satisfies the first element.

**C. Defendant Received Adequate Notice of Plaintiff's Disability and Need for Accommodation**

The Third Circuit has held that "an employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations, increase the disabled employee's job responsibilities, and then simply document the employee's failures." *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 315 (3d Cir. 1999) (Key Passage 1). Here, Plaintiff provided Defendant with medical documentation from Treating Physician explicitly stating: "I am a physician treating David Mullins. He's informed me he does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus, and from what he's described to me, I believe it's reasonable for him to have this fear. I have advised him to involve law enforcement for his protection." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 1, Aug. 13, 2025.) Treating Physician further recommended "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing" and explicitly stated such measures would "benefit and protect his mental health and his performance in his graduate program." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2, Oct. 1, 2025.)

Defendant Selcer's own email correspondence confirms receipt of this information. On August 25, 2025, Selcer acknowledged that Plaintiff "shared that [his] second priority is to be back physically in the classroom" and that Plaintiff "do[es] not feel safe on campus because [he] fear[s] Respondent." (Exhibit I, Aug. 25, 2025 email.) This acknowledgment, combined with the medical documentation, establishes that Defendant had actual knowledge of Plaintiff's disability and his need for accommodation. *See Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 315 (knowledge of disability and desire for accommodation triggers obligation).

**D. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation**

Plaintiff specifically requested Zoom access for class participation as a medically necessary accommodation. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 3.) This request was reasonable on its face, as Zoom was already in active use in the same department by Faculty D for comparable graduate coursework. (Exhibit N, Faculty D Syllabus/Policy, Spring 2025, providing "[a]lthough we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!") No university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access; the Faculty Handbook governs "faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 4; Exhibit Y, Faculty Handbook Course Modality Provision, 2025-2026.) As *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023) held, a "blanket no-remote policy with no written basis" cannot justify denying an individualized accommodation request. (Key Passage 1.)

**E. Defendant Denied the Accommodation**

Despite Plaintiff's documented medical need and the feasibility of the requested accommodation, Defendant imposed a conditional, time-limited measure rather than granting the accommodation. On August 25, 2025, Selcer "temporarily authorized" remote attendance "only for the first three weeks of class," after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person "regardless of medical documentation." (Exhibit I, Aug. 25, 2025 email; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 6.) This constituted a denial of the requested accommodation, not a grant.

Defendant's final directive of September 3, 2025, reinforced this denial, stating: "Until September 12, you may continue to attend your seminars via Zoom if you wish. . . . Otherwise, we expect you to attend seminars in person from September 15 forward." (Exhibit J, Selcer Final Directive, Sept. 3, 2025.) Selcer further testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference—citing that Zoom "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding—but "could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7; TRO Tr. at *:*.)

The denial became absolute in October 2025. After Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that he would continue Zoom access, the Zoom link was removed. (Exhibit Q, Zoom Removal Sequence, Oct. 6-17, 2025.) Selcer then informed Plaintiff that Dr. Faculty C "did not identify a compelling reason to continue" remote attendance—despite Faculty C having already independently authorized Zoom access on October 6, 2025. (Exhibit W, Faculty C Authorization, Oct. 6, 2025, stating "Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine.") Selcer's manipulation of the process—reframing Faculty C's prior authorization as a "compelling reason" inquiry that Faculty C never knew he was administering—demonstrates precisely the kind of bad faith that the Third Circuit condemned in *Taylor*.

The *Oross* court's analysis applies directly here. The accommodation request was "dead on arrival" because of "interference with the interactive process." *Oross*, No. 21-5032 (Key Passage 2). Defendant "applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request." *Id.* (Key Passage 1). No individualized assessment occurred here; indeed, "no interactive process was conducted." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 5.) Defendants "did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services." (*Id.*)

**F. Defendant Failed to Engage in the Interactive Process**

*Taylor* sets forth a four-part test for interactive process failure: "(1) the employer knew about the employee's disability; (2) the employee requested accommodations or assistance for his or her disability; (3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations; and (4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer's lack of good faith." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 315 (Key Passage 2). All four elements are satisfied here.

First, as established above, Defendant knew about Plaintiff's disability. Second, Plaintiff requested Zoom access as an accommodation. Third, Defendant made no good faith effort to assist. Selcer's response was to impose a "three-week countdown" and demand that Plaintiff engage with Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety offices—offices that had already disclaimed jurisdiction (Title IX stated "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested," Exhibit I, Aug. 25 email) or threatened retaliation (Student Conduct "threatened that if I continued emailing University officials about my safety and access, she would initiate conduct charges against me," *id.*). This was not a good faith attempt to assist; it was an attempt to "coerce compliance or force attrition," as Plaintiff correctly identified. (Exhibit I-1, Sept. 2, 2025 email.) Fourth, Plaintiff could have been accommodated—Zoom was available in the same department, for the same type of coursework, and no policy barred it. (Exhibit N; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶¶ 3-4.)

*Taylor* is explicit: "an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 315 (Key Passage 3). Here, the University's bad faith is manifest: Selcer admitted his objection was based on personal preference, not policy or pedagogy; no interactive process occurred; and the accommodation was already in use in the department. A reasonable factfinder could easily conclude Plaintiff could have participated in his education with the requested Zoom accommodation.

**G. Plaintiff Suffered Harm as a Result of the Denial**

The denial of accommodation directly caused Plaintiff harm. The three-week countdown itself was harmful: as Plaintiff explained, "Every day the countdown remains, I have to wonder whether my access will suddenly be cut off," creating "destabilizing" effects on "education and health." (Exhibit I-1, Sept. 2, 2025 email.) When the accommodation was ultimately revoked in October 2025, Plaintiff was excluded from class participation. (Exhibit Q, Zoom Removal Sequence, Oct. 16, 2025, showing Plaintiff unable to join class.) Treating Physician documented that the resulting stress "worsens his mental health conditions" and "is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (Exhibit F, Oct. 1, 2025.) Professor External Faculty Witness, an academic mentor, confirmed that Plaintiff's "education and research have been severely derailed" and that "David cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress." (Exhibit C, External Faculty Witness Declarations, Sept. 30 & Oct. 1, 2025.)

This harm flows directly from Defendant's failure to accommodate. Had Plaintiff been permitted to continue Zoom access—a readily available, feasible accommodation already in use in the same department—he would have been able to continue his education without the destabilizing threat of in-person encounters that caused his documented distress.

**H. Conclusion**

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff has established all elements of a failure-to-accommodate claim under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and the ADA. Defendant Duquesne University, a recipient of federal financial assistance, was on notice of Plaintiff's disability and need for accommodation, refused to engage in any meaningful interactive process, imposed a retaliatory time-limited measure rather than granting a reasonable accommodation that was readily available in the same department, and ultimately revoked access entirely. The Supreme Court's decision in *Osseo* confirms that no heightened standard applies. The Third Circuit's decision in *Taylor* confirms that Defendant's bad faith in the interactive process establishes liability. This Court should accordingly find Defendant liable for failure to accommodate under Count III.

**ARGUMENT**

**COUNT III: FAILURE TO ACCOMMODATE UNDER SECTION 504 AND THE ADA**

**A. Applicable Legal Standard**

The Supreme Court recently clarified that student claims under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and the Americans with Disabilities Act are subject to the same standards applied in other disability discrimination contexts. *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025). There is no heightened "bad faith or gross misjudgment" requirement for educational accommodations claims. *Id.* Plaintiff need only establish the ordinary elements of a failure-to-accommodate claim: (1) that he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) that the defendant had notice of his disability and his need for an accommodation; (3) that he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) that the defendant denied that accommodation; and (5) that he suffered resulting harm.

**B. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in good academic standing in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University. Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had no performance issues. (TRO Tr. at *:*; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 2.) Plaintiff has been diagnosed with ADHD and depression by his treating physician, Treating Physician, who has opined that these conditions are worsened by Plaintiff's housing instability and proximity to his abuser. (Exhibit F, Medical Letter dated Oct. 1, 2025.) Treating Physician further documented that Plaintiff's mental health conditions "likely worsens his mental health conditions" due to homelessness and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (*Id.*) These conditions substantially limit major life activities, including learning, concentrating, and interacting with others, thereby qualifying Plaintiff as an individual with a disability under Section 504 and the ADA. Duquesne University is a private university receiving federal financial assistance and is subject to Section 504. (SAC ¶ 2; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 10.)

**C. Plaintiff Provided Adequate Notice of His Disability and Need for Accommodation**

Plaintiff provided his treating physician's documentation to the University. Treating Physician's August 13, 2025 letter states: "I am a physician treating David Mullins. He's informed me he does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus, and from what he's described to me, I believe its reasonable for him to have this fear. I have advised him to involve law enforcement for his protection in response to this reasonable fear." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter dated Aug. 13, 2025.) The letter further recommends that "every effort be made to avoid" sharing living space with the individual, and warns that doing so "would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." (*Id.*)

Under *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296 (3d Cir. 1999), a defendant who "has received adequate notice of [the] disability and desire for accommodations" cannot simply ignore that notice and proceed with punitive measures. Here, Plaintiff's medical documentation was explicit: his diagnosed disability—ADHD and depression—required that he avoid proximity to his abuser. Plaintiff explained in multiple communications that his fear of encountering Respondent on campus prevented him from safely attending in-person classes without protective measures. (Exhibit I, Selcer Aug. 25 directive; Exhibit C, External Faculty Witness Declarations.) Selcer's own August 25, 2025 email acknowledges Plaintiff's explanation: "You explained that you do not feel safe on campus because you fear Respondent. You confided that you feel physically intimidated by her, and that you believe she has an unregistered firearm." (Exhibit I.) This constitutes more than adequate notice of both Plaintiff's disability and his need for accommodations to avoid contact with Respondent.

**D. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation—Zoom Access—That Was Denied**

Plaintiff requested Zoom access for class participation as a medically necessary accommodation. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 3.) Zoom was already in active use in the same department. Faculty D taught PHIL 520–420W Religious Platonism in Spring 2025 with a policy stating: "Although we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!" (Exhibit N, Faculty D Syllabus.) Zoom access was not novel, impractical, or contrary to university policy. No university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access; the Faculty Handbook governs faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations. (Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11, SJ 7.3; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 4.)

Despite this, Defendants denied Plaintiff a meaningful, permanent accommodation. On August 25, 2025, Selcer imposed a three-week countdown on Plaintiff's Zoom access, after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person regardless of medical documentation. (Exhibit I; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 6.) This was a "conditional, time-limited measure designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance." (*Id.*) Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding, but could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial. (TRO Tr. at *:*; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7.) Colleague A, another student in Selcer's Graduate Teaching Seminar, separately requested Zoom access and was denied; Selcer "can't explain why" Zoom was not available to her either. (Exhibit L; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 8.)

The denial culminated in Plaintiff's actual removal from Zoom access. Despite Faculty C separately authorizing Zoom for Plaintiff on October 6, 2025 ("Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine"), Selcer subsequently intervened and revoked that access. (Exhibits Q, W, V.) On October 17, 2025, Selcer emailed Plaintiff: "I've discussed your remote attendance of his graduate seminar with Faculty C and he did not identify a compelling reason to continue it. We would both welcome your return to in-person attendance of our seminars." (Exhibit V.) Faculty C later expressed confusion about the revocation, stating: "Colleague B had surgery this past week, so I gave him the opportunity to Zoom for that one day, which you joined. So, please ask Dan about any need for continued online attendance." (Exhibit W.) This demonstrates that Selcer unilaterally terminated an accommodation that Faculty C had authorized, without any individualized assessment of Plaintiff's medical needs.

**E. Defendants Engaged in No Interactive Process and Acted in Bad Faith**

*Taylor v. Phoenixville School District* establishes that "an employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations, increase the disabled employee's job responsibilities, and then simply document the employee's failures." 184 F.3d at 306. To show failure to participate in the interactive process, a plaintiff must demonstrate: "(1) the employer knew about the employee's disability; (2) the employee requested accommodations or assistance for his or her disability; (3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations; and (4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer's lack of good faith." *Id.*

All four elements are satisfied here. First, as detailed above, Selcer and the Philosophy Department knew of Plaintiff's disability and his fear of Respondent. (Exhibits F, I.) Second, Plaintiff repeatedly requested Zoom access as an accommodation. (Exhibits I-1, Q.) Third, Defendants made no good faith effort whatsoever. As the Statement of Undisputed Material Facts confirms: "No interactive process was conducted. Defendants did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 5.) Selcer imposed a unilateral three-week countdown without consulting Plaintiff, without conducting any individualized assessment, and without involving Disability Services. (Exhibit I; Exhibit J.) This is precisely the sort of "blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request" that was condemned in *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 23, 2023). In *Oross*, the court held that the accommodation request was "dead on arrival" because the university interfered with the interactive process. The same is true here.

Fourth, Plaintiff could have been reasonably accommodated but for Defendants' lack of good faith. Zoom was already in active use in the same department for comparable graduate coursework. (Exhibit N.) The Faculty Handbook does not prohibit individual student Zoom access. (Faculty Handbook; SJ 7.3.) Professor Faculty C independently determined that Plaintiff could Zoom into his class. (Exhibits W, Q.) The accommodation was plainly feasible, and Defendants' refusal to consider it was not based on any legitimate pedagogical or policy concern, but on Selcer's personal preference. (TRO Tr. at *:*; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7.) *Taylor* holds that "an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations." 184 F.3d at 316. Here, Plaintiff *was* performing with Zoom—Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had no performance issues (TRO Tr. at *:*; Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 2)—and his access was revoked not because of any academic deficiency but because of administrative retaliation.

**F. Plaintiff Suffered Actual Harm as a Result of the Denial**

Plaintiff suffered concrete harm from Defendants' failure to accommodate. After his Zoom access was revoked, Plaintiff was unable to attend his graduate seminars. (Exhibit Q, Zoom Removal Sequence Oct. 16-17, 2025.) On October 16, 2025, Plaintiff emailed Faculty C: "I attempted to join class through the usual Zoom link, but no meeting was available." (*Id.*) Plaintiff was forced to miss class and was excluded from his educational program. Treating Physician documented that Plaintiff's mental health conditions worsened as a result of the ongoing crisis: Plaintiff "reported to me he ran out of lexapro earlier than planned" and suffered worsening symptoms due to "proximity to his abuser" and "homelessness since June 30th." (Exhibit F, Oct. 1, 2025 Medical Letter.) Professor External Faculty Witness, Plaintiff's academic mentor for 19 years, declared that Plaintiff's "education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability and the conflict with his former roommate" and that Plaintiff "cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress and require hours of recovery time." (Exhibit C, External Faculty Witness Declarations.) The harm is precisely the kind of educational deprivation that Section 504 and the ADA are designed to prevent.

**G. Conclusion**

Defendants knew Plaintiff was a qualified individual with a disability, knew that he needed accommodation to avoid contact with his abuser, and knew that Zoom was a feasible accommodation already in use in the department. Instead of engaging in the interactive process required by *Taylor*, they imposed a retaliatory, time-limited measure designed to expire, refused to conduct any individualized assessment, and ultimately revoked Plaintiff's access without any legitimate basis. Under *Osseo*, no heightened "bad faith" standard applies, but even under *Taylor*'s ordinary standard, Defendants acted in bad faith by failing to engage in the interactive process, imposing a blanket denial, and then reversing an accommodation that a professor had independently authorized. Plaintiff is entitled to judgment as a matter of law on Count III.

**ARGUMENT**

Plaintiff David Mullins is entitled to judgment on Count III because Duquesne University violated his rights under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and the Americans with Disabilities Act by denying his requested reasonable accommodation—Zoom access to class—without engaging in an individualized, good-faith interactive process. The undisputed facts and controlling law establish each element of a failure-to-accommodate claim.

**A. Plaintiff is a Qualified Individual with a Disability.**

Plaintiff’s treating physician, Treating Physician, diagnosed him with ADHD and depression and confirmed that these conditions are exacerbated by the stress of homelessness and the fear of encountering his former roommate. Exhibit F (Medical Letters, Aug. 13 & Oct. 1, 2025). Treating Physician explicitly stated that worsening mental health “is expected to negatively impact his graduate education” and recommended “all measures to protect him from his abuser.” Exhibit F (Oct. 1 letter). Plaintiff’s disability—mental health conditions that substantially limit major life activities including learning and concentrating—renders him a qualified individual under the ADA and Section 504. See 29 U.S.C. § 705(20)(B); 42 U.S.C. § 12102(1)(A). Moreover, Plaintiff was enrolled as a doctoral candidate in good standing with no disciplinary record and no performance issues. TRO Tr. at *:*. He was fully qualified to participate in his graduate program with the accommodation of remote attendance.

**B. Duquesne Received Adequate Notice of Plaintiff’s Disability and His Need for Accommodation.**

Treating Physician’s August 13, 2025 letter notified Duquesne that Plaintiff “does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus” and that the physician believed that fear was “reasonable.” Exhibit F. The October 1, 2025 letter added specific diagnoses and the link between homelessness, stress, and educational harm. Exhibit F. Plaintiff repeatedly communicated his need for Zoom access as a medically necessary accommodation, explaining that in-person attendance without safety measures foreclosed his ability to participate. Exhibits I, I-1. Under *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 312-13 (3d Cir. 1999), an entity “who has received adequate notice of an employee’s disability and desire for accommodations” cannot simply ignore that request. Here, the University had both the physician’s letter and Plaintiff’s explicit requests.

**C. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation.**

On August 25, 2025, Selcer acknowledged that Plaintiff requested remote attendance. Exhibit I. Plaintiff consistently sought Zoom access as a means to continue his education while avoiding the documented safety risk on campus. That request was objectively reasonable. Zoom was already in active use in the same department for comparable graduate coursework. Exhibit N (Faculty D Syllabus: “I will also keep a Zoom meeting running” for any student who is not feeling well). No university policy prohibits individual student Zoom access. The Faculty Handbook governs course delivery modality for faculty, not individual student attendance accommodations. Exhibit Y (Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11). Selcer himself initially authorized Zoom for three weeks, confirming that the accommodation was feasible. Exhibit I.

**D. Duquesne Denied the Accommodation Without Engaging in the Interactive Process.**

Rather than conducting an individualized assessment, the University imposed a rigid, time-limited “concession” that was designed to expire and coerce Plaintiff into returning to campus. Selcer’s August 25 directive authorized Zoom “only for the first three weeks.” Exhibit I. When Plaintiff sought clarification, Selcer made clear that this was not a good-faith accommodation but a coercive deadline: “This is my final directive … we expect you to attend seminars in person from September 15 forward.” Exhibit J. Selcer later terminated Zoom access entirely after the TRO hearing, claiming that Faculty C found “no compelling reason” to continue it—even though Faculty C had independently authorized Zoom just days earlier. Exhibits Q, V, W. This pattern of ad hoc, conditional, and ultimately arbitrary denial mirrors the conduct condemned in *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023), where the university “applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff’s request,” making the accommodation request “dead on arrival.” *Oross* slip op. at *.

Moreover, Duquesne utterly failed to engage in the interactive process. Under *Taylor*, a disabled plaintiff must demonstrate: “(1) the employer knew about the employee’s disability; (2) the employee requested accommodations … ; (3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist … ; and (4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer’s lack of good faith.” 184 F.3d at 312. Here, the University knew of Plaintiff’s disability (Exhibit F), knew he requested Zoom (Exhibit I), yet never assessed his specific limitations, never discussed alternative accommodations, never involved Disability Services, and never offered any genuine alternative. SJ 7.2 (undisputed that “no interactive process was conducted”). Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference: he cited “divides attention” and a post-COVID departmental finding, but could not point to any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard. TRO Tr. at *:*. Another student, Colleague A, was similarly denied Zoom with “I can’t explain why.” Exhibit L. Such conduct is the antithesis of good faith. As *Taylor* holds, “an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform … with accommodations.” 184 F.3d at 313.

The University’s internal processes are no defense. *Oross* rejected the notion that a university can shield itself from ADA liability by pointing to a rigid policy that is never applied to the individual. See *Oross*, slip op. at *. Here, the Faculty Handbook does not prohibit individual Zoom accommodations; indeed, the same department already provided Zoom for Faculty D’s class. Exhibit N. The University’s failure to conduct an individualized assessment is the defining feature of its noncompliance.

**E. The Denial Caused Plaintiff Substantial Harm.**

The consequences of Duquesne’s refusal were severe. Plaintiff was forced to miss in-person classes, was cut off from remote access on October 17, 2025, and experienced increased stress, housing instability, and educational disruption. Exhibit Q (Zoom link removed); Exhibit C (External Faculty Witness Decl.: “his education and research have been severely derailed”); Exhibit F (physician’s warning that educational harm would result). The very purpose of the ADA’s accommodation mandate is to prevent such harm. See *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025) (applying ordinary failure-to-accommodate analysis in educational settings; no heightened “bad faith or gross misjudgment” standard). Under *Osseo*, Plaintiff need not show bad faith—only that the requested accommodation was reasonable and was denied. Here, the University’s conduct satisfies even the higher standard, as it acted without any written policy, individualized assessment, or good-faith engagement.

**F. The Applicable Legal Standard Confirms Liability.**

*Osseo* explicitly repudiates the notion that ADA and Section 504 claims in educational contexts require “a heightened showing of ‘bad faith or gross misjudgment.’” 605 U.S. at ___. Instead, “the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts” govern. *Id.* Under those standards, Plaintiff has established all elements: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) the University knew of his disability and need for accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) the University denied that accommodation without an interactive process; and (5) he suffered harm as a result. *See Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312-13; *Oross*, slip op. at *.

Because Duquesne’s denial of Zoom access was based on an ad hoc, unarticulated preference, and because the University refused to engage in any genuine interactive process even after Plaintiff provided medical documentation, the Court should find that Duquesne violated Section 504 and the ADA. Plaintiff is entitled to judgment on Count III.

**ARGUMENT**

**I. Applicable Legal Standard**

Under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), 42 U.S.C. § 12132, and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, a plaintiff must prove: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) the defendant knew of his disability and his need for an accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) the defendant denied that accommodation; and (5) the denial caused him harm. In *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025), the Supreme Court expressly held that student ADA and Section 504 claims “use the same standard as claims in other settings” and rejected any heightened “bad faith or gross misjudgment” requirement. *Osseo*, 605 U.S. at ___. That holding governs this case. Defendant Duquesne University of the Holy Spirit receives federal financial assistance and is undisputedly subject to Section 504. (SAC ¶ 2.)

**II. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual With a Disability**

Plaintiff David Mullins was at all relevant times an enrolled graduate student in good academic standing with no disciplinary record. Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff “had no performance issues.” (TRO Tr. at *:*.) Plaintiff’s treating physician, Treating Physician, diagnosed him with ADHD and depression—both recognized disabilities under the ADA and Section 504. (Exhibit F, Oct. 1, 2025 letter: “I have diagnosed David with ADHD and depression. I am treating those conditions.”) Treating Physician further opined that Plaintiff’s condition “likely worsens his mental health conditions” and that “worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education.” (*Id.*) The physician also confirmed that Plaintiff’s fear of a specific individual on campus is “reasonable” and that proximity to that individual “would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning.” (Exhibit F, Aug. 13, 2025 letter.)

These medical facts establish that Plaintiff suffers from mental impairments that substantially limit major life activities such as learning, concentrating, and interacting with others. He is therefore a “qualified individual with a disability” within the meaning of the ADA and Section 504.

**III. Defendant Had Notice of Plaintiff’s Disability and Need for Accommodation**

On August 13, 2025, Plaintiff provided Defendant with Treating Physician’s medical letter stating that Plaintiff “does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus” and recommending that “every effort be made to avoid” sharing space with that individual. (Exhibit F, Aug. 13 letter.) On October 1, 2025, Plaintiff provided a second letter in which Treating Physician reiterated that Plaintiff suffers from ADHD and depression, that homelessness and proximity to his abuser exacerbate those conditions, and that “all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing be made.” (Exhibit F, Oct. 1 letter.)

Defendant’s chair, Dr. Daniel Selcer, acknowledged receipt of this medical information. In his August 25, 2025 email, Selcer wrote: “You explained that you do not feel safe on campus because you fear Respondent.” (Exhibit I.) Selcer acknowledged that Plaintiff had “requested” safety measures and that Plaintiff sought remote attendance. (Exhibit I.) Thus, Defendant knew of Plaintiff’s disability and his need for a remote attendance accommodation. See *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 315 (3d Cir. 1999) (employer who “received adequate notice of an employee’s disability and desire for accommodations” cannot fail to engage in the interactive process).

**IV. Plaintiff Specifically Requested a Reasonable Accommodation — Zoom Access for Class Participation**

Plaintiff explicitly requested remote Zoom access to attend his graduate seminars as a medically necessary accommodation. (Exhibit I; Exhibit I-1; Exhibit Q.) That request was reasonable. Zoom attendance was already in active use in the same Philosophy Department for comparable graduate coursework. Faculty D’s Spring 2025 syllabus for PHIL 520–420W states: “Although we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!” (Exhibit N.) No university or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access. The Faculty Handbook 2025-2026 governs faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations. (Faculty Handbook, pp. 10-11; SJ 7.3.) And Selcer himself admitted that Zoom was “possible” for Plaintiff’s classes because “room and class size permits remote participation.” (Exhibit I.)

Thus, the accommodation was not only reasonable but was already implemented by the same department for other students and for one-off health needs. It would not fundamentally alter the nature of the program. *See Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023) (finding accommodation request was “dead on arrival” where university applied blanket no-remote policy without individualized assessment).

**V. Defendant Denied the Requested Accommodation and Failed to Engage in the Interactive Process**

Instead of granting Plaintiff’s reasonable request for indefinite Zoom access, Defendant imposed a conditional, time-limited measure designed to expire. On August 25, 2025, Selcer authorized remote attendance “only for the first three weeks of class,” subject to conditions that Plaintiff “fully engag[e] with the Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety offices.” (Exhibit I.) Selcer’s September 3, 2025 directive made the conditional nature explicit: “This is my final directive . . . Until September 12, you may continue to attend your seminars via Zoom if you wish.” (Exhibit J.) Then, on October 17, 2025, Selcer terminated Plaintiff’s Zoom access entirely, claiming that Professor Faculty C “did not identify a compelling reason to continue it.” (Exhibit V.) But Faculty C had already authorized Zoom on October 6, 2025, writing: “Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine.” (Exhibit W.) Selcer went behind Faculty C’s back, reframed the authorization as a “compelling reason” test, and then reported a negative result. (Exhibit Q, pattern summary.)

These actions constitute a clear denial of a reasonable accommodation. Moreover, Defendant never conducted an individualized assessment of Plaintiff’s specific limitations or possible accommodations. (SJ 7.2: “No interactive process was conducted.”) Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing “divides attention” and a post-COVID departmental finding. Yet he “could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial.” (TRO Tr. at *:*.) Another student, Colleague A, separately requested Zoom access from Selcer, and he denied it, “telling her he ‘can’t explain why’ Zoom was not available.” (Exhibit L.) This demonstrates a pattern of denying remote accommodations with no articulable basis.

Under *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d at 315, a defendant who “has received adequate notice of an employee’s disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process…. [A disabled person] must demonstrate: 1) the employer knew about the employee’s disability; 2) the employee requested accommodations …; 3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations; and 4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer’s lack of good faith.” All four elements are satisfied here. Defendant knew of Plaintiff’s disability and request (Elements 1 and 2). Defendant “did not assess Plaintiff’s specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services” (SJ 7.2) — this is the antithesis of a good faith effort (Element 3). And Plaintiff could have been accommodated through Zoom, which was already in use in the department — “but for [Defendant’s] lack of good faith” (Element 4). *Taylor* further holds that “an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations.” *Id.* at 317.

*Oross v. Kutztown University* is directly on point. There, the court granted summary judgment for the plaintiff where the “university applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff’s request,” and the “accommodation request was ‘dead on arrival’ — interference with the interactive process established.” *Oross*, No. 21-5032, slip op. at 12-13. Here, Defendant similarly applied an ad hoc, unwritten policy against remote attendance, never conducted an individualized assessment, and made the accommodation conditional and time-limited, effectively rendering it “dead on arrival.”

**VI. Defendant’s Denial Caused Plaintiff Harm**

The denial of Zoom access caused immediate and ongoing harm to Plaintiff’s education. After Defendant terminated his remote access on October 17, 2025, Plaintiff could not attend his graduate seminars. (Exhibit Q, Oct. 16 email: “I attempted to join class through the usual Zoom link, but no meeting was available.”) Plaintiff’s academic mentor, External Faculty Witness, declared that Plaintiff’s “education and research have been severely derailed” and that he “cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress.” (Exhibit C, External Faculty Witness declarations.) Treating Physician attested that worsening of Plaintiff’s mental health conditions, caused by the lack of protective measures, “is expected to negatively impact his graduate education.” (Exhibit F, Oct. 1 letter.)

The harm is not hypothetical. Plaintiff was forced to choose between his physical safety and his education. Defendant’s refusal to provide a reasonable accommodation directly impaired Plaintiff’s ability to participate in and benefit from his academic program. This constitutes the quintessential harm that the ADA and Section 504 are designed to prevent.

**VII. No Heightened Standard Applies**

Defendant may attempt to argue that a heightened “bad faith or gross misjudgment” standard applies in the educational setting. *Osseo* squarely forecloses that argument: “Schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims … are not required to make a heightened showing of ‘bad faith or gross misjudgment’ but instead are subject to the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts.” *Osseo*, 605 U.S. at ___. The same reasoning applies to graduate students. Plaintiff need only prove the ordinary elements of a failure-to-accommodate claim, which he has amply done here.

**CONCLUSION**

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff has established each element of his failure-to-accommodate claim under Section 504 and the ADA. Defendant had notice of Plaintiff’s disability, received a specific request for a reasonable accommodation, denied that accommodation through a conditional time-limited measure and subsequent removal, failed to engage in any interactive process, and caused Plaintiff significant educational harm. The Court should accordingly find Defendant liable on Count III.

**ARGUMENT**

**A. Legal Standard**

Under Title II of the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, a plaintiff must establish that (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) the defendant had notice of his disability and his need for an accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) the defendant denied that accommodation; and (5) he suffered harm as a result. *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schs.*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025). The Supreme Court recently made clear that these claims in educational settings are governed by the same ordinary failure-to-accommodate analysis that applies elsewhere; there is no heightened “bad faith or gross misjudgment” standard. *Id.* at *2–3. Accordingly, this Court applies the familiar framework.

**B. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in good academic standing and has been diagnosed by his treating physician with ADHD and depression. (Ex. F, Oct. 1, 2025 Letter from Treating Physician.) Treating Physician further opined that the stress of plaintiff’s housing instability and his fear of a specific individual on campus “worsens his mental health conditions” and “is expected to negatively impact his graduate education.” (*Id.*) The physician expressly recommended “all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing.” (*Id.*) These conditions—ADHD and depression—substantially limit major life activities including learning, concentrating, and interacting with others. Plaintiff is thus an individual with a disability within the meaning of 42 U.S.C. § 12102 and 29 U.S.C. § 705(20).

**C. Defendants Had Adequate Notice of Plaintiff’s Disability and His Need for an Accommodation**

On August 13, 2025, plaintiff submitted medical documentation from Treating Physician stating that plaintiff “does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus” and that it is “reasonable for him to have this fear.” (Ex. F, Aug. 13 Letter.) The same letter recommended “every effort be made to avoid” sharing space with that individual. (*Id.*) Plaintiff thereafter requested remote (Zoom) attendance for his graduate seminars as a medically necessary accommodation. (Ex. I, Mullins Aug. 25 email; Ex. I-1, Sept. 2 email.) The record shows that defendant Duquesne University was aware of plaintiff’s disability diagnosis and his specific requested accommodation. Indeed, Department Chair Selcer acknowledged plaintiff’s “fear of Respondent” and his desire for “safety measures” including remote attendance. (Ex. I, Selcer Aug. 25 email.) Notice is indisputable.

**D. Plaintiff’s Request for a Reasonable Accommodation Was Denied**

Although defendant initially permitted a temporary three-week period of Zoom access, it imposed an arbitrary “countdown” expiring on September 12, 2025, after which plaintiff was required to return to in-person attendance regardless of his medical documentation. (Ex. I, Selcer Aug. 25 email (“This remote seminar attendance … is authorized only for the first three weeks … at this time.”); Ex. J, Selcer Sept. 3 email (“[W]e expect you to attend seminars in person from September 15 forward.”).) This conditional, time-limited measure was designed to expire, making the accommodation “dead on arrival.” *Oross v. Kutztown Univ.*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023) (accommodation request “dead on arrival” where university imposed ad hoc blanket denial). Even after the temporary period ended, Selcer refused to continue Zoom access, telling plaintiff that unless university offices intervened, he must attend in person. (Ex. J.) When plaintiff later tried to attend a seminar via Zoom in October 2025, the professor who had previously authorized remote participation (Dr. Faculty C) was overridden by Selcer, who instructed Faculty C that no “compelling reason” existed and cut off the Zoom link. (Ex. Q, Oct. 16–17 email sequence; Ex. V, Selcer Oct. 17 email; Ex. W, Faculty C authorization and subsequent reversal.) The denial was complete and final.

**E. The Denial Was Unreasonable and Constituted a Failure to Engage in the Interactive Process**

Defendant never conducted an individualized assessment of plaintiff’s limitations or the feasibility of Zoom as an accommodation. *Oross*, slip op. at *4 (“University applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff’s request.”). Selcer testified that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference—“divides attention”—and a vague “post-COVID departmental finding” with no written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard to support the denial. (TRO Tr. at *:*; see also SJ 7.3–7.4.) The Faculty Handbook explicitly states that course modality governs faculty delivery, not individual student attendance accommodations; no policy prohibits individual Zoom use. (Ex. Y, Faculty Handbook 2025–2026 at 10–11.) To the contrary, the same department already uses Zoom for comparable graduate coursework: Faculty D’s syllabus states, “I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!” (Ex. N, Faculty D Syllabus (Spring 2025).) Thus, Zoom was plainly reasonable and feasible.

Defendant also failed to engage in any interactive process. “To show that an employer failed to participate in the interactive process, a disabled employee must demonstrate: 1) the employer knew about the employee’s disability; 2) the employee requested accommodations or assistance for his or her disability; 3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations; and 4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer’s lack of good faith.” *Taylor v. Phoenixville Sch. Dist.*, 184 F.3d 296, 316 (3d Cir. 1999). Here, all four elements are satisfied:

- Defendant knew of plaintiff’s disability (Ex. F; Ex. I);
- Plaintiff repeatedly requested Zoom as an accommodation (Ex. I, Aug. 25 email; Ex. I-1, Sept. 2 email);
- Defendant did not conduct any interactive process: it did not assess plaintiff’s specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives, or involve disability services (SJ 7.2);
- Plaintiff could have been accommodated with Zoom—the same technology already in use by the same department (Ex. N)—but for defendant’s bad faith. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 316 (“[A]n employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations.”).

Defendant’s imposition of a three-week countdown and its refusal to engage further is textbook bad faith. *See* Ex. I (Selcer conditioning continued Zoom on plaintiff’s participation in Title IX and Student Conduct proceedings, not on medical need); Ex. I-1 (plaintiff’s demand for retraction of retaliatory countdown); Ex. J (Selcer’s final directive refusing to extend Zoom). The university cannot “fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations, increase the disabled employee’s job responsibilities, and then simply document the employee’s failures.” *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312. Here, defendant increased plaintiff’s burden by requiring in-person attendance despite medical documentation, and then documented his failure to comply. That is precisely the conduct *Taylor* condemns.

**F. Plaintiff Suffered Harm as a Direct Result of the Denial**

The denial of Zoom access caused plaintiff substantial harm. Without the accommodation, plaintiff was forced either to forgo class attendance or to risk encountering a person whom his physician deemed a reasonable source of fear. This fear and the resulting stress “worsens his mental health conditions” and “negatively impact[ed] his graduate education.” (Ex. F, Oct. 1 Letter.) Plaintiff’s academic mentor, External Faculty Witness, attested that plaintiff’s “education and research have been severely derailed” and that he “cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress and require hours of recovery time.” (Ex. C, External Faculty Witness Decl. ¶ 3; Supp. Decl. ¶ 10.) The encounter on September 17, 2025, when plaintiff’s former roommate “literally waiting outside of second class” (Ex. C, Sept. 17 Facebook message), confirms the palpable, ongoing danger. Plaintiff also faced 90+ days of homelessness while the university refused to implement supportive measures. (Ex. A, Housing Crisis Timeline; Ex. F, Oct. 1 Letter (“He reports homelessness since June 30th … likely worsens his mental health conditions.”).) The denial of the requested accommodation—the simple, feasible provision of Zoom—directly contributed to this cascade of educational and personal harm.

For these reasons, defendant Duquesne University violated Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act. Plaintiff respectfully requests that the Court deny summary judgment and find that defendant failed to accommodate his disability as a matter of law.

**ARGUMENT**

**III. DEFENDANT DUQUESNE UNIVERSITY VIOLATED SECTION 504 OF THE REHABILITATION ACT AND THE ADA BY FAILING TO PROVIDE PLAINTIFF DAVID MULLINS WITH A REASONABLE ACCOMMODATION FOR HIS DISABILITY**

**A. Legal Standard**

The Supreme Court's recent decision in *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025), definitively resolved any lingering ambiguity regarding the standard governing disability discrimination claims in educational settings. The Court held that "schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims related to their education are not required to make a heightened showing of 'bad faith or gross misjudgment' but instead are subject to the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts." *Osseo*, 605 U.S. at ___. As the Court further explained, "[n]othing in the text of Title II of the ADA or Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act suggests that such claims should be subject to a distinct, more demanding analysis." *Id.* Accordingly, Plaintiff's claim under Section 504 and the ADA is governed by the ordinary failure-to-accommodate framework.

To establish a prima facie case of failure to accommodate, Plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) the University had notice of his disability and need for accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) the University denied that accommodation; and (5) he suffered harm as a result. *See Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 306-07 (3d Cir. 1999) (setting forth interactive process requirements). Critically, when the requested accommodation is reasonable on its face, the "employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations[] cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312.

**B. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

The record establishes that Plaintiff David Mullins suffers from diagnosed medical conditions that substantially limit major life activities. Plaintiff's treating physician, Treating Physician, a Doctor of Osteopathic Medicine, has diagnosed Plaintiff with "ADHD and depression." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2, Oct. 1, 2025). Treating Physician further attests that Plaintiff's conditions are exacerbated by the stress of his housing instability and ongoing safety concerns, and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (Exhibit F). The physician specifically recommends "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing be made" to "benefit and protect his mental health and his performance in his graduate program." (Exhibit F).

Plaintiff's disability is directly linked to his need for accommodation. Treating Physician's August 13, 2025 letter confirms that Plaintiff "does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus" and that this fear is "reasonable." (Exhibit F). The physician explicitly states that sharing space with this individual "would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." (Exhibit F). This medical documentation satisfies the first element.

Plaintiff is also "qualified" within the meaning of the statute. At all relevant times, he was an enrolled doctoral candidate in good academic standing with no disciplinary record. Dr. Selcer himself testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff "had no performance issues." (TRO Tr. at *:*). Plaintiff's academic mentor, External Faculty Witness, attests that "[b]ased on my knowledge of David's academic work and communications, his education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability and the conflict with his former roommate." (Exhibit C, Declaration of External Faculty Witness, Sept. 30, 2025). Thus, Plaintiff is a qualified individual with a disability who could perform the essential functions of his academic program with reasonable accommodation.

**C. Plaintiff Provided Adequate Notice of His Disability and Need for Accommodation**

The University received clear and repeated notice of Plaintiff's disability-related needs. Treating Physician's August 13, 2025 medical letter was directed "[t]o whom it may concern" and expressly states that Plaintiff "does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus" and that the physician "believe[s] it's reasonable for him to have this fear." (Exhibit F). This letter, along with Plaintiff's repeated communications to University officials, provided actual knowledge of Plaintiff's condition and his need for accommodation.

In addition, Plaintiff personally informed University officials of his safety concerns. On July 20, 2025, Plaintiff wrote to Dr. Selcer and Dr. Faculty A: "To be blunt: I am scared of this person. I fear for my safety in any context where she knows my whereabouts." (Exhibit R). Plaintiff further disclosed that his former roommate "expressed strong enthusiasm for the idea that, in her view, the Second Amendment should mean firearm regulations should be less of a hassle than they are." (Exhibit R). Dr. Selcer acknowledged receipt and assured Plaintiff that "it won't be a problem to ensure you don't share an office." (Exhibit R).

The notice requirement is satisfied where the University knows "about the employee's disability" and the employee "requested accommodations or assistance for his or her disability." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312-13. Here, Plaintiff provided medical documentation, repeatedly explained his fear, and explicitly identified the safety measures needed to permit his educational participation. The University cannot credibly claim ignorance.

**D. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation**

Plaintiff specifically requested Zoom access for class participation as a medically necessary accommodation. As Treating Physician's medical documentation confirms, Plaintiff's treating physician recommended "all measures to protect him from his abuser." (Exhibit F). Zoom access was a straightforward, low-cost, and readily available accommodation that would permit Plaintiff to continue his coursework while avoiding the triggering environment that exacerbated his disability.

The reasonableness of Zoom access as an accommodation is demonstrated by the fact that the same accommodation is already in active use within the same department. Faculty D's syllabus for PHIL 520-420W — Religious Platonism, Spring 2025, expressly states: "Although we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!" (Exhibit N, Faculty D Syllabus). This demonstrates that Zoom access is technologically feasible, pedagogically acceptable, and already integrated into the department's ordinary practices.

Furthermore, no university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access. The Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11, governs "faculty course delivery modality" and states that "courses designed to be offered in-person must be taught in-person (other than on rare, one-off occasions)." (Exhibit Y). However, this policy addresses the *professor's* teaching modality, not *student* attendance accommodations. The Handbook does not prohibit students from attending remotely where accommodations are warranted. As the record confirms, "no written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard" supports the denial of Plaintiff's request. (TRO Tr. at *:*).

**E. The University Denied the Accommodation and Failed to Engage in the Interactive Process**

The University's response to Plaintiff's accommodation request was not merely inadequate — it was affirmatively obstructive. The record demonstrates a clear pattern of denial, delay, and retaliation.
