First, Dr. Selcer imposed a three-week countdown on Plaintiff's Zoom access on August 25, 2025, after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person "regardless of medical documentation." (Exhibit I, Selcer Aug. 25 directive). Selcer's directive was "conditional, time-limited" and "designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance." (Statement of Undisputed Facts ¶ 6). Selcer's own testimony reveals that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing that it "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding, but he could not identify any written policy or pedagogical study supporting the denial. (TRO Tr. at *:*).

Second, the University never conducted an interactive process. Despite receiving Plaintiff's medical documentation and accommodation request, Defendants "did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services." (SJ 7.2). This constitutes a textbook failure of the interactive process. Under *Taylor*, "an employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312. Where, as here, an "employer acts in bad faith in the interactive process," it "will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations." *Id.* at 317.

Third, the University's denial was not an isolated event but part of a pattern. Colleague A, another student in Selcer's Graduate Teaching Seminar, separately requested Zoom access, and Selcer "denied her request and told her he 'can't explain why' Zoom was not available." (Exhibit L). This demonstrates "a pattern of denial with no articulable basis." (Undisputed Facts ¶ 8).

The Third Circuit's decision in *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023), is directly on point. In *Oross*, the court granted summary judgment for the plaintiff where the university "applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request," and the "accommodation request was 'dead on arrival' — interference with the interactive process established." *Oross*, slip op. at *.* The same is true here. The University's three-week countdown, imposed without any individualized assessment and without engaging disability services, rendered Plaintiff's accommodation request "dead on arrival."

Furthermore, after the TRO hearing, Selcer continued to deny Zoom access. When Plaintiff attempted to attend Professor Faculty C's class remotely — an arrangement Faculty C himself had expressly authorized on October 6, 2025 (Exhibit W, Email 2 of 6: "Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine") — Selcer contacted Faculty C and "reframed it as a 'compelling reason' test Faculty C never knew he was administering." (Exhibit Q, Zoom Removal Sequence). Selcer then reported to Plaintiff: "I've discussed your remote attendance of his graduate seminar with Faculty C and he did not identify a compelling reason to continue it. We would both welcome your return to in-person attendance." (Exhibit V). This back-channel interference with an already-approved accommodation demonstrates bad faith.

**F. The University's Actions Caused Plaintiff Harm**

The denial of accommodation directly caused Plaintiff harm. Treating Physician's October 1, 2025 letter confirms that Plaintiff's homelessness since June 30 "has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions. Worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (Exhibit F). Professor External Faculty Witness's declarations corroborate that Plaintiff's "education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability and the lack of university support." (Exhibit C). The harm is both medical and academic: Plaintiff's conditions worsened, his educational participation was impaired, and he endured compounding stress while his accommodation was systematically denied.

**G. The University Cannot Justify Its Denial**

Defendants cannot defend their denial by pointing to policy or pedagogical concerns, because the record establishes that no such policy exists. The Faculty Handbook governs faculty teaching modality, not student accommodations. Moreover, the University itself permits Zoom access for other students — as demonstrated by Faculty D's syllabus (Exhibit N) and the accommodation provided to another student, Colleague B, for surgery (Exhibit W, Email 5 of 6). The selective denial of the same accommodation to Plaintiff, despite medical documentation, raises serious questions of pretext.

Finally, Defendants cannot claim that Plaintiff failed to engage with disability services, because the University never referred Plaintiff to disability services. Selcer's August 25 directive mentioned disability services as an optional path, but the interactive process requires the *University* to initiate and participate in the accommodation process, not to place the burden entirely on the student. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312-13. The University's failure to involve disability services, conduct an individualized assessment, or propose any alternative accommodation constitutes a failure of the interactive process as a matter of law.

**H. Conclusion on Count III**

Plaintiff has established each element of his failure-to-accommodate claim under Section 504 and the ADA. He is a qualified individual with a disability. He provided notice and requested a reasonable accommodation — Zoom access, which the University already provides to other students. The University denied that accommodation without any individualized assessment, without engaging the interactive process, and without any legitimate pedagogical or policy basis. The denial caused Plaintiff direct harm, worsening his medical condition and impairing his academic performance. The University is therefore liable under Section 504, 29 U.S.C. § 794, and the ADA for failure to accommodate Plaintiff's disability.

**ARGUMENT**

**COUNT III — FAILURE TO ACCOMMODATE UNDER SECTION 504 AND THE ADA**

**A. Legal Standard**

The Supreme Court has squarely rejected any heightened standard for disability discrimination claims in educational settings. In *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025), the Court held that "Schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims related to their education are not required to make a heightened showing of 'bad faith or gross misjudgment' but instead are subject to the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts." The Court emphasized that "[n]othing in the text of Title II of the ADA or Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act suggests that such claims should be subject to a distinct, more demanding analysis." *Id.* Accordingly, the ordinary failure-to-accommodate framework governs this claim.

To establish a prima facie case for failure to accommodate, Plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) Defendant had notice of his disability and need for accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) Defendant denied that accommodation; and (5) Plaintiff suffered harm as a result. *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 306-07 (3d Cir. 1999). Where the employer (or university) fails to engage in the interactive process in good faith, liability attaches if the employee could have been reasonably accommodated. *Id.* at 312.

**B. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

Plaintiff's treating physician, Treating Physician, has diagnosed Plaintiff with ADHD and depression. (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2, Oct. 1, 2025.) Treating Physician further states that Plaintiff "reports homelessness since June 30th which has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions." (*Id.*) The medical documentation confirms that "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing" are necessary to "benefit and protect his mental health and his performance in his graduate program." (*Id.*) These diagnoses substantially limit major life activities including concentrating, sleeping, and interacting with others, thereby constituting a disability under the ADA and Section 504.

Plaintiff was at all relevant times an enrolled graduate student in good academic standing with no disciplinary record. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 2; TRO Tr. at *:* (Selcer testifying Plaintiff had no performance issues).) He is therefore a "qualified individual" under the statutes.

**C. Defendant Had Notice of Plaintiff's Disability and Need for Accommodation**

Notice is established through multiple channels. Treating Physician's August 13, 2025 medical letter states: "I am a physician treating David Mullins. He's informed me he does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus, and from what he's described to me, I believe its reasonable for him to have this fear. I have advised him to involve law enforcement for his protection in response to this reasonable fear. To share a living space with this individual would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning. I recommend every effort be made to avoid this." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 1.)

This documentation was provided to Defendant, and Plaintiff repeatedly communicated his need for safety-related accommodations—including remote participation—to multiple university officials, including Dr. Selcer, Dean Wasilko, and Title IX Coordinator Simpson. (See, e.g., Exhibit I (Aug. 25, 2025 meeting exchange); Exhibit R (July 20, 2025 email to Selcer stating "I am scared of this person"); Exhibit B-0 (June 30, 2025 initial request for emergency housing).) Selcer acknowledged in his August 25 directive that Plaintiff had "explained that you do not feel safe on campus because you fear Respondent." (Exhibit I, Selcer Aug. 25 directive.) This actual knowledge satisfies the notice requirement. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 313 (employer "who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations" cannot fail to engage in the interactive process).

**D. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation**

Plaintiff specifically requested Zoom access for class participation as a medically necessary accommodation. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 3.) This request was made directly to Dr. Selcer, who had authority over course modality. (Exhibit I (Plaintiff's Aug. 25 email stating "remote access is not a choice and does not fulfill the University's obligations" and demanding retraction of the retaliatory three-week cap).) Plaintiff also requested baseline safety measures—no-contact order, campus safety escort, and registrar separation—without which he could not safely attend in person. (*Id.*)

The accommodation was undeniably reasonable. Zoom was already in active use in the same department by Faculty D for comparable graduate coursework. (Exhibit N, Faculty D Syllabus (stating "I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!").) No university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access. The Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11, "governs faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 4; Exhibit Y, Faculty Handbook.) Indeed, Dr. Faculty C independently authorized Plaintiff's Zoom attendance in his Marion & Phenomenology seminar without any objection from the administration. (Exhibit W, Faculty C email Oct. 6, 2025 ("Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine.").)

**E. Defendant Denied the Accommodation and Failed to Engage in the Interactive Process**

Defendant's response to Plaintiff's requests for accommodation was, at every turn, a denial veiled in procedural conditions that were never satisfied—conditions designed to fail.

*First*, no interactive process was conducted. Defendant "did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services." (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 5.) The record is devoid of any individualized assessment of Plaintiff's documented disability. Instead, Defendant imposed a blanket condition: Plaintiff must meet with Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety—offices that had already disclaimed jurisdiction or threatened retaliation. (Exhibit B-6 (Simpson stating Title IX "does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested"); Exhibit B-7 (Sawa threatening conduct charges for "mass emails").) This is the antithesis of good-faith engagement. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312 ("an employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process").

*Second*, the only accommodation offered was conditional, time-limited, and coercive. On August 25, 2025, Selcer imposed a "three-week countdown" on Plaintiff's Zoom access, after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person regardless of medical documentation. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 6; Exhibit I, Selcer Aug. 25 directive.) This was not a good-faith accommodation; it was "a conditional, time-limited measure designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance." (*Id.*) Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding, but could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7; TRO Tr. at *:*.) Such an arbitrary, policy-less denial mirrors the "blanket no-remote policy" that the Eastern District of Pennsylvania found violated the ADA in *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 23, 2023), where the court held that the "accommodation request was 'dead on arrival'—interference with the interactive process established."

*Third*, the pattern of denial extends beyond Plaintiff. Colleague A, a student in Selcer's Graduate Teaching Seminar (Fall 2025), separately requested Zoom access. Selcer denied her request and told her he "can't explain why" Zoom was not available—demonstrating a pattern of denial with no articulable basis. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 8; Exhibit L.) This arbitrary denial of the same accommodation to another student confirms that Defendant's refusal is not based on pedagogical necessity but on an ad hoc, unstated policy of exclusion.

*Fourth*, after Selcer's three-week window expired, Defendant escalated the denial. Selcer surreptitiously contacted Dr. Faculty C—who had independently authorized Zoom—and obtained a reversal by claiming Faculty C "did not identify a compelling reason to continue it." (Exhibit V, Selcer Oct. 17, 2025 email; Exhibit Q, Zoom removal sequence.) Yet Faculty C later expressed confusion, stating he had authorized Zoom and was unaware of any "compelling reason" test. (Exhibit W, Faculty C Oct. 17, 2025 email ("Colleague B had surgery this past week, so I gave him the opportunity to Zoom for that one day, which you joined. So, please ask Dan about any need for continued online attendance.").) This coordinated revocation, after a professor had already granted the accommodation, demonstrates bad faith. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312 ("an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations").

**F. Plaintiff Suffered Resulting Harm**

Defendant's denial directly caused harm. Plaintiff was forced into homelessness for over 90 days. (Exhibit A, Housing Crisis Timeline; Exhibit F, Treating Physician Oct. 1, 2025 letter ("homelessness since June 30th which has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions").) His mental health deteriorated: he "ran out of lexapro earlier than planned" and "was taking more than prescribed" due to "proximity to his abuser." (Exhibit F.) His academic functioning was impaired—Dr. External Faculty Witness, his academic mentor, declared that "his education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability" and that "supportive housing and protective measures are necessary for him to resume his academic work." (Exhibit C, External Faculty Witness Declaration Sept. 30, 2025.) Plaintiff was deprived of in-person access to colleagues, faculty, departmental offices, and the library—all integral to his graduate education. (Exhibit I-1, Plaintiff's Sept. 2, 2025 email.) Finally, Defendant retaliated by issuing a written threat that "performance issues raised by two of the three faculty members" could result in "suspension of your stipend" through 2029. (Exhibit X-8, Selcer Feb. 13, 2026 reassignment letter.) This threat came after Faculty B expressly stated her "door is always open" and that reassignment "will not affect your funding" (Exhibit X-7), and after Selcer himself confirmed in a meeting that Faculty B "doesn't make decisions about your stipend" (Exhibit X-6). The creation of fabricated "performance issues" is direct evidence of retaliation for Plaintiff's pursuit of his rights.

**G. Defendant's Failure to Accommodate Was Clearly Unreasonable**

The Third Circuit's framework in *Taylor* makes clear that an institution that receives notice of a disability and a request for accommodation cannot simply list conditions and then terminate access when those conditions are unmet. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 313. Here, Defendant refused to engage in any individualized assessment, imposed a time limit on a concededly feasible accommodation, denied the same accommodation to another student without explanation, and then surreptitiously revoked the accommodation after a third-party professor had granted it. This is "clearly unreasonable in light of the known circumstances." *Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education*, 526 U.S. 629, 648 (1999); *Oross*, slip op. at *.* (applying similar standard to ADA claim).

**H. Conspiracy (Supporting Opposition to Summary Judgment on Count V)**

To the extent Defendant moves for summary judgment on the conspiracy claim, Plaintiff has demonstrated genuine disputes of material fact on each element: (1) an agreement among Selcer, Faculty C, and/or Faculty B to deny accommodation (Exhibit Q, Zoom removal sequence showing Selcer reframing Faculty C's authorization as a "compelling reason" test; Exhibit X-6, Selcer fabricating "performance issues" after Faculty B requested reassignment without performance complaints); (2) intent to do an unlawful act—denial of federally mandated accommodations and retaliation; (3) overt acts in furtherance—the three-week countdown, the surreptitious Zoom revocation, and the fabricated performance issues leading to stipend threats; (4) malice—the pattern of arbitrary denial, the threat to impose conduct charges (Exhibit B-7), and the stipend threat for future courses through 2029 (Exhibit X-8); and (5) resulting harm—homelessness, educational derailment, and retaliatory stipend jeopardy. *Thompson Coal Co. v. Pike Coal Co.*, 488 Pa. 198, 412 A.2d 466 (1979) (elements of civil conspiracy); *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022) ("convincing mosaic" of circumstantial evidence sufficient to oppose summary judgment).

**CONCLUSION**

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff has established each element of his failure-to-accommodate claim under Section 504 and the ADA. Defendant denied a reasonable accommodation without individualized assessment, without engaging in the interactive process, and on the basis of personal preference and ad hoc policy. The accommodation—Zoom attendance—was demonstrably feasible and already in use. The denial caused severe harm to Plaintiff's mental health, housing stability, and academic progress. Summary judgment should be denied, and this matter should proceed to trial.

**ARGUMENT**

**III. DEFENDANTS FAILED TO PROVIDE PLAINTIFF A REASONABLE ACCOMMODATION IN VIOLATION OF SECTION 504 OF THE REHABILITATION ACT AND THE ADA**

Count III alleges that Duquesne University violated Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, and the Americans with Disabilities Act by failing to provide Plaintiff David Mullins with a reasonable accommodation for his disability. Following the Supreme Court's decision in *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025), Section 504 claims in educational settings are governed by the same failure-to-accommodate analysis applied in other disability discrimination contexts, without any heightened "bad faith or gross misjudgment" standard. *Osseo*, 605 U.S. at ___. To establish a failure-to-accommodate claim, a plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) the defendant had notice of his disability and need for accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) the defendant denied that accommodation; and (5) he suffered resulting harm. As demonstrated below, Plaintiff has established each element and Defendants' motion for summary judgment must be denied.

**A. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

The record establishes that Plaintiff is a doctoral candidate in good academic standing with no disciplinary record, and that his treating physician has diagnosed him with ADHD and depression. Exhibit F (Treating Physician Letter, Oct. 1, 2025). Medical documentation confirms that Plaintiff "reports homelessness since June 30th which has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions," and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." *Id.* The physician expressly recommended "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing be made." *Id.*

Additionally, Plaintiff's treating physician stated under oath that Plaintiff "does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus," and that the physician believes "it's reasonable for him to have this fear." Exhibit F (Treating Physician Letter, Aug. 13, 2025). The physician further opined that "[t]o share a living space with this individual would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." *Id.* This medical documentation confirms that Plaintiff's mental health conditions—ADHD and depression—substantially limit major life activities including learning, concentrating, and interacting with others, rendering him a qualified individual with a disability under Section 504 and the ADA.

**B. Defendants Had Adequate Notice of Plaintiff's Disability and Need for Accommodation**

The Third Circuit has held that "[a]n employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations" cannot simply ignore that request. *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 313 (3d Cir. 1999). The same standard applies to educational institutions under Section 504. *Id.*

Plaintiff provided Defendants with explicit medical documentation of his disability and his need for accommodation. Exhibit F (Aug. 13, 2025 letter from Treating Physician). Plaintiff also repeatedly informed Defendants that he could not safely attend in-person classes without protective measures. Exhibit R (July 20, 2025 email to Selcer stating "I am scared of this person. I fear for my safety in any context where she knows my whereabouts"). Exhibit S (July 21, 2025 email stating "I fled my home at substantial cost in order to protect my own physical safety"). Exhibit I (Aug. 25, 2025 email from Selcer acknowledging Plaintiff's explanation that "you do not feel safe on campus because you fear Respondent").

Plaintiff's specific accommodation request—Zoom access for class participation—was communicated clearly and repeatedly. Exhibit I (Aug. 25, 2025 exchange); Exhibit I-1 (Sept. 2, 2025 demands for retraction of retaliatory Zoom countdown). Plaintiff's medical documentation supported this request, as his physician confirmed that proximity to the individual he feared "is highly likely to worsen his medical condition." Exhibit F (Aug. 13, 2025).

The undisputed facts establish that Defendants had notice: Selcer testified at the TRO hearing that Plaintiff had no performance issues, Plaintiff's medical documentation was provided to Defendants, and Plaintiff repeatedly explained that his inability to attend in person stemmed from documented medical need. *See* Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶¶ 1-2.

**C. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation**

Plaintiff requested Zoom access for his seminar classes as a medically necessary accommodation. Exhibit I (Aug. 25, 2025 Selcer directive). Zoom was already in active use in the same department by Faculty D for comparable graduate coursework, demonstrating that the requested accommodation was administratively feasible and did not fundamentally alter the nature of the program. Exhibit N (Faculty D syllabus providing that "[a]lthough we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!").

Plaintiff's request was not merely a preference; it was a documented medical necessity. Exhibit F (physician letters). The requested accommodation was specifically tailored to address Plaintiff's documented fear of a specific individual on campus, a fear that his treating physician confirmed was reasonable and that, if unaddressed, would impair his academic functioning. *Id.*

**D. Defendants Denied the Accommodation by Failing to Engage in the Interactive Process and Imposing an Unreasonable Time Limit**

**1. Defendants Failed to Conduct Any Interactive Process**

The Third Circuit has established that "[t]o show that an employer failed to participate in the interactive process, a disabled employee must demonstrate: 1) the employer knew about the employee's disability; 2) the employee requested accommodations or assistance for his or her disability; 3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations; and 4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer's lack of good faith." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 315.

Here, the undisputed facts demonstrate Defendants' complete failure to engage: "No interactive process was conducted. Defendants did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services." Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 5. The record confirms that "[n]o university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access," and the Faculty Handbook "governs faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations." *Id.* ¶ 4.

As the court recognized in *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023), where a university applies a "blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request," the accommodation request is effectively "dead on arrival"—constituting interference with the interactive process. *Oross*, slip op. at *.

Here, Selcer admitted under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding, but "could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial." Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7. This is precisely the kind of ad hoc, unreasoned denial that the *Oross* court found impermissible.

**2. The Conditional, Time-Limited Zoom Access Was Not a Reasonable Accommodation**

Selcer's August 25 directive imposed a "three-week countdown on Plaintiff's Zoom access, after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person regardless of medical documentation." Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 6. This was not an accommodation; it was "a conditional, time-limited measure designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance." *Id.*

The Third Circuit has made clear that an employer "cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations, increase the disabled employee's job responsibilities, and then simply document the employee's failures." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 313. Here, Defendants did precisely that: they offered a temporary, contingent measure with a built-in expiration date, conditioned on Plaintiff's compliance with other university processes, and then terminated the accommodation when Plaintiff did not satisfy those conditions. Exhibit J (Sept. 3, 2025 final directive ending Zoom access on Sept. 15).

Moreover, when Plaintiff attempted to continue Zoom access through Professor Faculty C—who had independently authorized it—Selcer intervened and reversed that authorization. Exhibit W (Oct. 6-17, 2025 email sequence showing Faculty C initially authorizing Zoom, Selcer claiming Faculty C "did not identify a compelling reason to continue it," and Faculty C later expressing confusion). Exhibit V (Oct. 17, 2025 email from Selcer denying continued Zoom access). This pattern of interference with alternative accommodation arrangements further demonstrates bad faith.

**3. The Refusal to Accommodate Is Not Justified by Any Legitimate Institutional Interest**

The record establishes that Zoom is already used in the same department by other faculty for comparable coursework. Exhibit N (Faculty D syllabus). Selcer provided no written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting his denial. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 7. When another student, Colleague A, requested Zoom access from Selcer, he denied her request and told her he "can't explain why" Zoom was not available—demonstrating a pattern of denial with no articulable basis. *Id.* ¶ 8.

There is no evidence that Plaintiff's requested accommodation would have imposed an undue burden or fundamentally altered the nature of the program. To the contrary, the accommodation had already been implemented for Plaintiff on a temporary basis, confirming its feasibility. The only reason for its revocation was Defendants' retaliatory response to Plaintiff's pursuit of his legal rights.

**E. Plaintiff Suffered Resulting Harm**

The record documents that Plaintiff's academic progress was severely impaired by Defendants' denial of accommodation. External Faculty Witness, a longtime academic mentor, declared that Plaintiff's "education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability and the conflict with his former roommate," and that "supportive housing and protective measures are necessary for him to resume his academic work." Exhibit C (Sept. 30, 2025 declaration). Professor External Faculty Witness further documented that when Plaintiff encountered his former roommate outside his classroom on September 17, 2025, the incident caused "genuine distress" and "required hours of recovery time." Exhibit C (Supplemental Declaration).

Medical documentation confirms that Plaintiff's homelessness and inability to access campus safely "worsens his mental health conditions" and is "expected to negatively impact his graduate education." Exhibit F (Oct. 1, 2025 letter). The physician recommended "all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing be made" to "benefit and protect his mental health and his performance in his graduate program." *Id.*

Plaintiff was also forced to miss classes and academic activities, and ultimately faced a retaliatory reassignment and threat of stipend suspension. Exhibit X-8 (Feb. 13, 2026 letter from Selcer fabricating "performance issues" and threatening stipend through 2029). The harm to Plaintiff's academic career and mental health is directly traceable to Defendants' refusal to provide the requested accommodation.

**F. The Interactive Process Failure Alone Supports Liability**

Under *Taylor*, "an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 316. Here, the jury could readily conclude that Plaintiff could have continued his studies with the requested Zoom accommodation, which had already been temporarily implemented and was used by other students in the same department. Exhibit N. The record also demonstrates that no other accommodation was ever offered or discussed by Defendants, and that Defendants never involved Disability Services or any other office capable of facilitating an accommodation. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ¶ 5.

Because Defendants "had adequate notice of [Plaintiff's] disability and desire for accommodations" and "fail[ed] to engage [Plaintiff] in the interactive process of finding accommodations," they are liable for the resulting harm. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 313.

**CONCLUSION**

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff has established genuine disputes of material fact on each element of his Section 504 and ADA failure-to-accommodate claim. Defendants' motion for summary judgment on Count III must be denied.

**ARGUMENT**

**A. Plaintiff States a Claim for Failure to Accommodate Under Section 504 and the ADA**

To establish a prima facie case for failure to accommodate under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act (29 U.S.C. § 794) and the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), Plaintiff must show: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) the University had notice of his disability and his need for an accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) the University denied that accommodation; and (5) he suffered harm as a result. *See A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025) (applying ordinary failure-to-accommodate standard, rejecting heightened “bad faith or gross misjudgment” standard). Plaintiff has adduced undisputed evidence on each element, and Defendants’ failure to engage in any interactive process—coupled with an arbitrary, time-limited, and ultimately revoked accommodation—plainly violates federal law.

**B. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

Plaintiff’s treating physician, Treating Physician, diagnosed him with ADHD and depression. (Exhibit F, Medical Letter Oct. 1, 2025.) Treating Physician attested that homelessness and proximity to the individual Plaintiff fears have “worsen[ed] his mental health conditions” and that “[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education.” (*Id.*) The same letter explicitly recommends “all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing.” (*Id.*) An earlier letter from Treating Physician confirmed that Plaintiff “does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus” and that the physician believed this fear was “reasonable”; the letter further warned that sharing a living space with that individual “would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning.” (Exhibit F, Medical Letter Aug. 13, 2025.) Plaintiff’s depression and ADHD—both recognized disabilities under the ADA and Section 504—substantially limit major life activities including learning, concentrating, and sleeping. Dr. Selcer himself testified that Plaintiff had “no performance issues” and was in good academic standing. (TRO Tr. at *:*; SAC ¶ 2.) Plaintiff is therefore a “qualified individual” under both statutes.

**C. The University Had Actual Notice of Plaintiff’s Disability and His Need for an Accommodation**

Notice may be provided by the plaintiff or by the plaintiff’s physician. *Taylor v. Phoenixville Sch. Dist.*, 184 F.3d 296, 313 (3d Cir. 1999). Here, Plaintiff submitted Treating Physician’s medical documentation to the University on August 13 and October 1, 2025. (Exhibit F.) The August 13 letter stated unequivocally that Plaintiff feared a specific individual and that the physician recommended “every effort be made to avoid” sharing space with that person. (*Id.*) Plaintiff also repeatedly informed Dr. Selcer, Dean Wasilko, and other administrators of his disability-related need to avoid in-person contact with his former roommate. (Exhibits R, S, I.) As early as July 20, 2025, Plaintiff wrote to Dr. Selcer: “I am scared of this person. I fear for my safety in any context where she knows my whereabouts.” (Exhibit R.) Dr. Selcer acknowledged the email and did not dispute the severity of the concern. (*Id.*) By August 25, 2025, Dr. Selcer was fully aware that Plaintiff sought to “attend classes remotely” because of a “medical accommodation” need. (Exhibit I.) The University therefore had actual knowledge of Plaintiff’s disability and his need for a remote-learning accommodation more than a month before the September 12 expiration of the temporary Zoom access.

**D. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation**

Plaintiff repeatedly requested permission to attend his graduate seminars via Zoom as a disability-related accommodation. On August 25, 2025, Dr. Selcer acknowledged this request: “You are temporarily authorized to remotely attend the two classes in which you are enrolled this semester … This remote seminar attendance for these FA25 classes is authorized only for the first three weeks of class at this time. … If you are requesting a medical accommodation, it is also conditioned on you fully engaging with the Disability Services Office.” (Exhibit I.) Plaintiff thereafter explicitly asked for indefinite Zoom access as a reasonable accommodation. (Exhibit I-1, demanding that “Zoom access indefinitely” be secured because the three-week cap was “retaliatory.”) On October 6, 2025, Plaintiff requested remote participation from Professor Faculty C, who authorized it. (Exhibit W, Oct. 6 email: “Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine.”) The accommodation request was both clear and repeated.

**E. The University Denied the Requested Accommodation**

Denial is not limited to an outright refusal; imposing a conditional, time-limited measure that is designed to expire and that coerces the employee into abandoning the accommodation is itself a denial. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312–13 (employer cannot “fail to engage the employee in the interactive process … and then simply document the employee’s failures”). Here, the University did precisely that.

On August 25, 2025, Dr. Selcer authorized only three weeks of Zoom access, with the explicit condition that Plaintiff “fully engage with the Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety offices within the next week and beyond.” (Exhibit I.) That condition was not met—Plaintiff had already been threatened by the Student Conduct director, and the Title IX coordinator had disclaimed jurisdiction—and on September 3, 2025, Dr. Selcer issued a “final directive” stating that “[u]ntil September 12, you may continue to attend your seminars via Zoom if you wish” and that “[o]therwise, we expect you to attend seminars in person from September 15 forward.” (Exhibit J.) This was not a good-faith temporary measure; it was a “countdown clock designed to coerce compliance or force attrition.” (Exhibit I-1.)

The denial became outright revocation in October 2025. Although Plaintiff had obtained Zoom authorization directly from Professor Faculty C on October 6, 2025 (Exhibit W), and although Dr. Selcer testified at the TRO hearing on October 15 that Zoom would continue (Exhibit Q, TRO Tr.), on October 16 the Zoom link disappeared. (Exhibit Q, Oct. 16 email: “I attempted to join class through the usual Zoom link, but no meeting was available.”) On October 17, 2025, Dr. Selcer wrote: “I’ve discussed your remote attendance of his graduate seminar with Faculty C and he did not identify a compelling reason to continue it. We would both welcome your return to in-person attendance.” (Exhibit V.) Faculty C later clarified that he was “confused” and had not known his prior authorization was being recharacterized. (Exhibit W, Oct. 17 PM email.) The accommodation was therefore denied—first by a conditional time limit that was not removed, and then by outright revocation without any individualized assessment or interactive process.

**F. The University Failed to Engage in the Interactive Process as Required by Law**

Under *Taylor*, an employer who has “adequate notice of an employee’s disability and desire for accommodations” must engage in a good-faith interactive process to assess the employee’s limitations and explore possible accommodations. *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 312. The Third Circuit holds that a disabled employee must demonstrate: (1) the employer knew about the employee’s disability; (2) the employee requested accommodations; (3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist; and (4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer’s lack of good faith. *Id.* at 312–13.

Here, it is undisputed that “[n]o interactive process was conducted. Defendants did not assess Plaintiff’s specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services.” (SJ 7.2.) No individualized assessment of Plaintiff’s medical documentation was ever performed; instead, Dr. Selcer imposed a three-week, take-it-or-leave-it deadline. (Exhibits I, J.) When Plaintiff asked for a challenge or review, he was told the Zoom access would simply end. (Exhibit I-1.) This is the antithesis of good faith.

In *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023), the court granted summary judgment for the plaintiff where a university denied a remote accommodation “based on ad hoc blanket policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff’s request,” and concluded that the “accommodation request was ‘dead on arrival’—interference with the interactive process established.” *Oross* is directly on point. As in *Oross*, the University here applied a “blanket no-remote policy” (or, more accurately, an ad hoc personal preference) with no written basis, no individualized assessment, and no meaningful engagement. Dr. Selcer testified that his objection to Zoom was based on “divides attention” and a post-COVID departmental finding—but he “could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial.” (TRO Tr.) The Faculty Handbook confirms that course-modality rules govern *faculty* assignments, not individual student accommodations. (Exhibit Y, Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11.) And Zoom is already in active use in the same department for comparable graduate coursework. (Exhibit N, Faculty D Syllabus: “I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely.”) There was no pedagogical barrier, and no showing of undue burden.

**G. The University’s Denial Was Not Based on Any Legitimate, Nondiscriminatory Reason—It Was Arbitrary and Retaliatory**

The record demonstrates that the University’s denial of accommodation was pretextual. Not only did Dr. Selcer fail to articulate a valid pedagogical reason (TRO Tr.), but he also engaged in a pattern of denying Zoom access to other students without explanation. Colleague A, another student in Selcer’s Graduate Teaching Seminar, separately requested Zoom access; Selcer denied it and “told her he ‘can’t explain why’ Zoom was not available.” (Exhibit L.) That is direct evidence that the denial was arbitrary, not the product of any considered policy or individualized assessment.

Furthermore, the University’s imposition of a three-week “countdown” and its subsequent revocation of a previously authorized Zoom link (Exhibits Q, V) are retaliatory. The University conditioned Zoom access on Plaintiff’s engagement with offices that had already dismissed his claims or threatened him with conduct charges. (Exhibits B-6, B-7.) As Plaintiff explained, the conditional deadline was “a textbook example of retaliation masquerading as accommodation.” (Exhibit I-1.) Courts have held that demanding compliance with Title IX is protected activity, *Atkinson v. Lafayette College*, 460 F.3d 447, 457 (3d Cir. 2006), and that a plaintiff need only “reasonably believe” the conduct opposed violates the statute. *Kengerski v. Harper*, No. 20-1307 (3d Cir. 2021). The revocation of Zoom access within two days of Plaintiff’s TRO hearing (Oct. 15, 2025) is “unusually suggestive” temporal proximity sufficient to establish causation. *Jalil v. Avdel Corp.*, 873 F.2d 701, 708 (3d Cir. 1989). In any event, the “convincing mosaic” of circumstantial evidence—the pattern of antagonism, the unexplained revocation, the refusal to engage in the interactive process—more than satisfies the causation requirement. *Canada v. Samuel Grossi & Sons, Inc.*, No. 20-2747 (3d Cir. 2022).

**H. The Accommodation Plaintiff Requested—Zoom Access—Was Reasonable and Would Not Have Imposed an Undue Burden**

A reasonable accommodation is one that enables the qualified individual to enjoy equal access to the educational program. The ADA and Section 504 do not require a perfect accommodation, only one that is effective. Zoom access is an objectively reasonable accommodation that would allow Plaintiff to participate in class discussions, ask questions, and fulfill his coursework requirements. It imposes no cost on the University (Zoom is already available and used in the department), it does not disrupt the classroom (Faculty D’s syllabus demonstrates it can be offered seamlessly), and it does not require the University to alter any fundamental aspect of its program. The Faculty Handbook explicitly permits “rare, one-off occasions” of remote participation, and nothing in the policy prohibits individual student Zoom attendance. (Exhibit Y.) The University has identified no pedagogical or administrative burden that would make Zoom access unreasonable.

**I. Plaintiff Has Suffered Harm as a Direct Result of the Denial**

Plaintiff has suffered substantial harm. The October 17, 2025 revocation forced him to choose between his physical safety and his education. He was unable to attend classes in person because of a documented, reasonable fear of encountering his former roommate—a fear that a physician validated as reasonable. (Exhibit F.) The loss of Zoom access caused Plaintiff to miss class sessions, disrupt his academic progress, and experience worsening mental health. Dr. External Faculty Witness’s declaration confirms that “David cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress and require hours of recovery time.” (Exhibit C). The medical documentation states that homelessness and stress “worsen his mental health conditions” and that “worsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education.” (Exhibit F, Oct. 1 letter.) The harm is ongoing: Plaintiff was deprived of the reasonable accommodation that would have allowed him to continue his education without endangering his mental and physical well-being.

**J. Conclusion**

Plaintiff has established each element of a failure-to-accommodate claim under the ADA and Section 504. He is a qualified individual with a disability; the University had notice of his disability and his need for accommodation; he requested a reasonable accommodation (Zoom access); the University denied that accommodation by imposing a conditional time limit and then revoking previously authorized remote attendance; and Plaintiff suffered educational and emotional harm as a result. The University failed to engage in the interactive process at all, let alone in good faith. Under *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, “schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims related to their education are not required to make a heightened showing of ‘bad faith or gross misjudgment’” but are subject to ordinary analysis. 605 U.S. ___ (2025). Under ordinary analysis, the University’s conduct—no individualized assessment, no interactive process, an arbitrary three-week ultimatum, and a retaliatory revocation—plainly violates the law. Summary judgment should be denied on Count III.

# ARGUMENT

## I. Duquesne University Discriminated Against Plaintiff by Denying a Reasonable Accommodation for His Disability in Violation of Section 504 and the ADA

Plaintiff David Mullins, a doctoral candidate in philosophy with diagnosed ADHD and depression, requested a reasonable accommodation—remote Zoom attendance for his graduate seminars—based on documented medical need and a reasonable fear for his safety on campus. Despite the University's knowledge of his disability, the availability of Zoom in the same department for comparable coursework, and the absence of any contrary policy or undue burden, Defendants imposed a conditional three-week countdown, refused to engage in the interactive process, and ultimately revoked the accommodation entirely. This conduct constitutes a clear failure to accommodate under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and Title II of the ADA, governed by the ordinary standards recently reaffirmed by the Supreme Court in *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025).

### A. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability

Plaintiff's treating physician, Treating Physician, has diagnosed Plaintiff with ADHD and depression—conditions that qualify as disabilities under the ADA and Section 504. *See* Exhibit F (Medical Letter Oct. 1, 2025) ("I have diagnosed David with ADHD and depression. I am treating those conditions."). Treating Physician further opined that Plaintiff's homelessness since June 30, 2025, has "been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions," and that worsening his conditions "is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." *Id.* A prior medical letter confirmed that sharing a living space with the individual Plaintiff feared "would cause him a great deal of stress, would be highly likely to worsen his medical condition, and could impair his academic functioning." Exhibit F (Medical Letter Aug. 13, 2025). Plaintiff was at all relevant times an enrolled graduate student in good academic standing with no performance issues. (TRO Tr. at *:*; Exhibit R, Selcer Email July 20, 2025, acknowledging Plaintiff's concerns; Exhibit C, External Faculty Witness Declarations, confirming severe educational harm from housing instability and lack of university support.)

### B. Plaintiff Provided Timely Notice of His Disability and Need for Accommodation

On June 30, 2025, Plaintiff notified Dean Wasilko, Department Chair Selcer, and others that he had fled his home due to a "credible fear for my physical safety" and needed emergency supportive measures. Exhibit B-0. On July 20, 2025, Plaintiff wrote to Selcer and the Director of Graduate Studies: "I am scared of this person. I fear for my safety in any context where she knows my whereabouts" and explained the threats and stalking. Exhibit R. Plaintiff's treating physician provided documentation directly supporting his need for avoidance: "He's informed me he does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus, and from what he's described to me, I believe it's reasonable for him to have this fear. I have advised him to involve law enforcement for his protection." Exhibit F (Aug. 13, 2025). This documentation explicitly recommended "every effort be made to avoid" sharing space with the individual. *Id.*

Plaintiff formally requested a reasonable accommodation—the ability to attend his seminars remotely via Zoom. Exhibits I, I-1, J, V. He repeatedly explained that his fear of encountering his former roommate on campus, absent any protective measures, made in-person attendance impossible. Exhibit I (Aug. 25, 2025 email requesting safety measures and noting accommodation needed); Exhibit I-1 (Sept. 2, 2025 email demanding retraction of retaliatory three-week cap on Zoom). The *Osseo* Court held that "Schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims related to their education are not required to make a heightened showing of 'bad faith or gross misjudgment' but instead are subject to the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts." *Osseo*, 605 U.S. at ___. Plaintiff's notice was more than adequate under any reasonable standard.

### C. Plaintiff Requested a Reasonable Accommodation That Was Available and Imposed No Undue Burden

Zoom access was already in active use in the Philosophy Department. Faculty D's syllabus for the Spring 2025 course explicitly permits any student to attend remotely: "Although we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely!" Exhibit N (Faculty D Syllabus). The same technology was available for Plaintiff's courses. The Faculty Handbook provides that "Faculty members do not have the latitude to switch courses to an alternative format or modality to suit their personal needs or preferences," Exhibit Y, but this policy governs *faculty course delivery modality*—not individual student attendance accommodations. The Handbook's restriction does not prohibit a single student from attending via Zoom. Indeed, no university or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access. (SJ 7.3; Faculty Handbook pp. 10-11.)

The accommodation Plaintiff sought—continued remote access—was reasonable on its face. It would not have required any changes to the course structure or faculty instruction. It would have merely utilized technology already in place. The *Oross* court squarely rejected the argument that a university could deny a remote accommodation based on an unwritten, ad hoc policy: "University applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request." *Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023). This court further held that the "Accommodation request was 'dead on arrival'—interference with the interactive process established." *Id.* Exactly the same dynamic occurred here: Plaintiff's Zoom access was conditionally granted for three weeks, then revoked at the end of the countdown, then re-granted briefly by Professor Faculty C, then revoked again after Selcer intervened. Exhibits I, J, Q, V, W.

### D. Defendants Denied the Accommodation and Failed to Engage in the Interactive Process

No interactive process was conducted. Defendants did not assess Plaintiff's specific limitations, evaluate possible accommodations, discuss alternatives with Plaintiff, or involve disability services. (SJ 7.2.) Instead, on August 25, 2025, Selcer imposed a three-week countdown on Plaintiff's Zoom access, after which Plaintiff "would be required to attend in person regardless of medical documentation." Exhibit I (Selcer Aug. 25 directive). This was not an accommodation—it was a conditional, time-limited measure designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance. Exhibit I-1 (Plaintiff's letter: "The imposition of a three-week cap is not a supportive measure at all. It is a countdown clock designed to coerce compliance or force attrition, a textbook example of retaliation masquerading as accommodation.").

The interactive process failure is independently actionable. *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296, 317 (3d Cir. 1999), holds that "an employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations, increase the disabled employee's job responsibilities, and then simply document the employee's failures." To show a failure to participate, a disabled employee must demonstrate: "(1) the employer knew about the employee's disability; (2) the employee requested accommodations or assistance for his or her disability; (3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations; and (4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer's lack of good faith." *Id.* at 318. All four elements are satisfied here.

Defendants knew about Plaintiff's disability (Exhibit F, medical documentation; Exhibit R, Selcer email acknowledging Plaintiff's fear). Plaintiff requested remote attendance repeatedly (Exhibits I, I-1, J). Defendants made no good-faith effort: Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding, but could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial. (TRO Tr. at *:*.) Instead, Defendants actively undermined any accommodation: Sawa threatened misconduct charges for emailing officials about safety concerns (Exhibit B-7); Simpson disclaimed Title IX jurisdiction without legal basis (Exhibit B-6); Wasilko denied housing despite admitting rooms were available (Exhibit G). Plaintiff "could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer's lack of good faith"—Zoom was already available and used in the department. *See* Taylor, 184 F.3d at 318; Exhibit N.

Moreover, Defendants' behavior evinces bad faith. The *Taylor* court warned that "an employer who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations." *Id.* at 319. Here, Selcer imposed the countdown, then on September 3, 2025, demanded that Plaintiff either "follow up with and participate in the relevant processes with Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety or to drop your requests for accommodation and return to in-person attendance." Exhibit J. That is an explicit ultimatum to abandon the accommodation request—the antithesis of good-faith interactive process. Finally, on October 17, 2025, Selcer revoked Zoom access altogether, claiming Faculty C "did not identify a compelling reason to continue it," despite Faculty C having previously and independently authorized the very same accommodation. Compare Exhibit W (Faculty C authorizing Zoom on Oct. 6: "Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine.") with Exhibit V (Selcer email Oct. 17 reporting that Faculty C found "no compelling reason" after Selcer reframed the request). This sequence demonstrates not merely a failure to accommodate but an active campaign to defeat the accommodation.

### E. Plaintiff Suffered Resulting Harm

The denial of the accommodation caused direct and concrete harm to Plaintiff's education. He was forced to miss classes, lost the ability to participate in his seminars in meaningful ways, and suffered exacerbation of his mental health conditions. Exhibit C (External Faculty Witness Declarations: "his education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability and the conflict with his former roommate"). Treating Physician's medical letters confirm that homelessness and stress "likely worsens his mental health conditions," which "is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." Exhibit F (Oct. 1, 2025). On September 17, 2025, Plaintiff's former roommate was "literally waiting outside" his classroom, confirming the basis of his fear. Exhibit C (Facebook Messenger conversation). Plaintiff was forced to explain to his professor that he could not attend because "of an unfolding legal matter" and had his Zoom access abruptly cut off on October 16, 2025, after Selcer had promised continued access during the TRO hearing just one day earlier. Exhibit Q (Zoom removal sequence). This is the precise harm that the ADA and Section 504 are designed to prevent—the exclusion of a qualified individual with a disability from educational programs due to an institution's failure to provide reasonable accommodations.

## II. The Supreme Court's Decision in *Osseo* and the Third Circuit's Decision in *Oross* Directly Govern This Case

In *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, the Supreme Court held that "Schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims related to their education are not required to make a heightened showing of 'bad faith or gross misjudgment' but instead are subject to the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts." 605 U.S. ___ (2025). The Court emphasized: "Nothing in the text of Title II of the ADA or Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act suggests that such claims should be subject to a distinct, more demanding analysis." *Id.* This eliminates any argument that student disability claims require a harder standard than other failure-to-accommodate claims.

Under the ordinary failure-to-accommodate framework, *Oross v. Kutztown University* is directly on point and controlling authority in the Eastern District of Pennsylvania. In *Oross*, the university applied a "blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request." *Oross*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 25, 2023). The court held that the "Accommodation request was 'dead on arrival'—interference with the interactive process established." *Id.* Here, Selcer admitted under oath that he could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting his denial. (TRO Tr. at *:*) The only basis was his personal preference that Zoom "divides attention." *Id.* That is precisely the kind of ad hoc, unwritten, and individualized judgment that *Oross* condemns.

The Third Circuit's decision in *Taylor v. Phoenixville School District* underscores the obligations Defendant failed to meet. The duty to engage in the interactive process is not optional. "An employer who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 317. Where, as here, the employer acts in bad faith and the employee could have been reasonably accommodated, liability follows. *Id.* at 319. Defendants' repeated conditioning of accommodations on meetings they knew Plaintiff could not attend, their creation of a three-week countdown, and their eventual revocation of the accommodation after Plaintiff had relied upon it, all demonstrate bad faith.

## III. Plaintiff Is Entitled to a Full Range of Remedies, Including Compensatory Damages and Injunctive Relief

Because Defendant Duquesne University receives federal financial assistance, it is subject to Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act. (SAC ¶ 2.) A private right of action for damages exists under Section 504 for intentional discrimination, and the failure to accommodate claims in the educational context do not require a showing of "bad faith or gross misjudgment." *Osseo*, 605 U.S. at ___. Plaintiff has established a genuine dispute of material fact on each element of his failure-to-accommodate claim. The evidence shows that Plaintiff is a qualified individual with a disability, that he requested a reasonable accommodation, that Defendants denied that accommodation, that they failed to engage in the interactive process, and that Plaintiff was harmed as a direct result. The record contains medical documentation confirming his disability and the reasonableness of his fear (Exhibit F), evidence that Zoom was available and used in the same department (Exhibit N), evidence that Defendants imposed a retaliatory and time-limited "accommodation" (Exhibits I, J), and evidence that Defendants ultimately revoked even that conditional measure (Exhibits Q, V). No legitimate pedagogical justification supports the denial. The motion for summary judgment on Count III must be denied, and Plaintiff is entitled to proceed to trial on his failure-to-accommodate claim under Section 504 and the ADA.

**ARGUMENT**

**III. DEFENDANTS VIOLATED SECTION 504 AND THE ADA BY DENYING PLAINTIFF'S REQUESTED REASONABLE ACCOMMODATION AND FAILING TO ENGAGE IN THE INTERACTIVE PROCESS**

Under *A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools*, 605 U.S. ___ (2025), student ADA and Section 504 claims are governed by the same ordinary failure-to-accommodate analysis that applies in other disability discrimination contexts. The Supreme Court explicitly rejected any heightened "bad faith or gross misjudgment" standard, holding that "[n]othing in the text of Title II of the ADA or Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act suggests that such claims should be subject to a distinct, more demanding analysis." *Id.* Accordingly, Plaintiff must establish: (1) he is a qualified individual with a disability; (2) Defendants had notice of his disability and need for accommodation; (3) he requested a reasonable accommodation; (4) Defendants denied that accommodation; and (5) he suffered harm as a result.

**A. Plaintiff Is a Qualified Individual with a Disability**

Plaintiff David Mullins is a doctoral candidate in the Department of Philosophy at Duquesne University, and at all relevant times was an enrolled graduate student in good academic standing with no disciplinary record. (Statement of Undisputed Material Facts ("SUMF") ¶ 2; TRO Tr. at *:* (Selcer testimony confirming no performance issues).) His treating physician, Treating Physician, diagnosed him with ADHD and depression—conditions that qualify as disabilities under the ADA and Section 504. (Exhibit F (Medical Documentation).) Treating Physician expressly attests that these conditions "likely worsen[] his mental health conditions" and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2 (Oct. 1, 2025).) The medical documentation further confirms that Plaintiff's homelessness—directly caused by Defendants' failures—"has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions." (*Id.*) Plaintiff is thus a qualified individual with a disability under the statutes.

**B. Defendants Had Adequate Notice of Plaintiff's Disability and Need for Accommodation**

Plaintiff provided his treating physician's documentation stating: "I am a physician treating David Mullins. He's informed me he does not feel safe around a specific individual on campus, and from what he's described to me, I believe it's reasonable for him to have this fear." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 1 (Aug. 13, 2025).) Treating Physician further recommended "every effort be made to avoid" sharing space with the individual causing Plaintiff's fear. (*Id.*) This documentation was provided to Defendants, who were thus on notice of Plaintiff's disability-related need for an accommodation that would allow him to participate in his education while avoiding the individual who triggered his documented fear.

*Taylor v. Phoenixville School District*, 184 F.3d 296 (3d Cir. 1999), makes clear that once an employer—or here, a university—receives "adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations," it cannot "fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations." *Id.* at 313. Defendants had such notice no later than August 13, 2025, when Plaintiff provided Treating Physician's medical documentation, and arguably as early as July 2025, when Plaintiff repeatedly communicated his safety concerns and need for protective measures to multiple university officials. (Exhibit B-0 (June 30 email to Wasilko); Exhibit R (July 20 email to Selcer); Exhibit S (July 21 email to Wasilko, Selcer, Faculty A).)

**C. Plaintiff Requested Zoom Attendance as a Reasonable Accommodation**

Plaintiff specifically requested Zoom access for class participation as a medically necessary accommodation. (SUMF ¶ 3.) This request was reasonable and feasible: Zoom was already in active use in the same department by Faculty D for comparable graduate coursework, as evidenced by the Faculty D Syllabus providing that "if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely." (Exhibit N.) The syllabus further states: "Although we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running." (*Id.*) No university policy or departmental policy prohibits individual student Zoom access. (SUMF ¶ 4; Exhibit Y (Faculty Handbook 2025-2026, pp. 10-11) (governing faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations).)

*Oross v. Kutztown University*, No. 21-5032 (E.D. Pa. July 23, 2023), is directly on point. There, the court entered summary judgment for the plaintiff where the university "applied blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request." *Oross* at *1. The accommodation request was "dead on arrival"—a finding of interference with the interactive process. Here, as in *Oross*, Defendants applied an ad hoc, unwritten policy against remote attendance, with no individualized assessment of Plaintiff's medical documentation or specific needs. The accommodation was similarly "dead on arrival."

**D. Defendants Denied Plaintiff's Reasonable Accommodation**

Despite having notice and a specific request, Defendants denied Plaintiff's requested accommodation. On August 25, 2025, Dr. Selcer imposed a three-week countdown on Plaintiff's Zoom access, after which Plaintiff would be required to attend in person "regardless of medical documentation." (SUMF ¶ 6; Exhibit I (Selcer Aug. 25 directive).) This was a conditional, time-limited measure "designed to expire and coerce meeting attendance." (SUMF ¶ 6.) Selcer's September 3, 2025, directive confirmed: "Until September 12, you may continue to attend your seminars via Zoom if you wish. . . . Otherwise, we expect you to attend seminars in person from September 15 forward." (Exhibit J.)

Selcer testified under oath that his objection to Zoom was based on personal preference, citing that remote attendance "divides attention" and a post-COVID departmental finding—but he could not identify any written policy, pedagogical study, or departmental standard supporting denial. (SUMF ¶ 7; TRO Tr. at *:*.) This subjective preference, unsupported by any written policy or evidence, cannot justify denial of a reasonable accommodation under *Oross* and *Osseo*.

The denial was not an isolated instance. When another student, Colleague A, separately requested Zoom access from Selcer for the Graduate Teaching Seminar, Selcer denied her request and told her he "can't explain why" Zoom was not available—demonstrating a pattern of denial with no articulable basis. (SUMF ¶ 8; Exhibit L.) This pattern underscores that Defendants' denial was not based on individualized assessment but on an unwritten, discriminatory policy.

Furthermore, after the TRO hearing on October 15, 2025, where Selcer assured Plaintiff that Zoom would continue, the Zoom link was removed the very next day. (Exhibit Q; Exhibit W.) Selcer then claimed that Professor Faculty C "did not identify a compelling reason to continue" the remote attendance—but Faculty C himself had already authorized Zoom on October 6, writing: "Yes, Zooming in tomorrow would work fine." (Exhibit W, Email 2 of 6.) Selcer's intervention to revoke an already-granted accommodation constitutes further denial and retaliation.

**E. Defendants Failed to Engage in the Interactive Process**

*Taylor v. Phoenixville School District* establishes the framework for the interactive process: "To show that an employer failed to participate in the interactive process, a disabled employee must demonstrate: 1) the employer knew about the employee's disability; 2) the employee requested accommodations or assistance for his or her disability; 3) the employer did not make a good faith effort to assist the employee in seeking accommodations; and 4) the employee could have been reasonably accommodated but for the employer's lack of good faith." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 317. An employer "who acts in bad faith in the interactive process will be liable if the jury can reasonably conclude that the employee would have been able to perform the job with accommodations." *Id.*

Here, no interactive process was conducted. Defendants did not: assess Plaintiff's specific limitations; evaluate possible accommodations; discuss alternatives with Plaintiff; or involve disability services. (SUMF ¶ 5.) Instead, Defendants imposed a unilateral, conditional, time-limited measure with no collaboration or good-faith exploration of alternatives. This is precisely the type of bad-faith conduct that *Taylor* condemns. An employer "who has received adequate notice of an employee's disability and desire for accommodations, cannot fail to engage the employee in the interactive process of finding accommodations, increase the disabled employee's job responsibilities, and then simply document the employee's failures." *Taylor*, 184 F.3d at 313. Here, Defendants not only failed to engage but affirmatively imposed a countdown clock to coerce Plaintiff into abandoning his accommodation request. (Exhibit I; Exhibit J.)

The contrast with the available alternatives is stark. Zoom was already in use in the department. (Exhibit N.) The Faculty Handbook expressly governs faculty course delivery modality, not individual student attendance accommodations. (Exhibit Y.) No written policy prohibited Plaintiff's requested accommodation. (SUMF ¶ 4.) Plaintiff could have been reasonably accommodated but for Defendants' lack of good faith.

**F. Plaintiff Suffered Harm as a Result of the Denial**

Plaintiff's harm is well-documented and severe. He has been homeless since June 30, 2025, with "90+ days of homelessness while university maintains available housing resources but refuses compliance." (Exhibit A (Housing Crisis Timeline).) Treating Physician attests that homelessness "worsens his mental health conditions" and that "[w]orsening his mental health conditions is expected to negatively impact his graduate education." (Exhibit F, Medical Letter 2.) External Faculty Witness similarly declares: "David cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress and require hours of recovery time." (Exhibit C (Supplemental Declaration).)

The denial of Zoom access specifically caused Plaintiff to miss classes, suffer academic disruption, and experience ongoing stress and uncertainty about his ability to continue his program. The three-week countdown was "a textbook example of retaliation masquerading as accommodation" (Exhibit I-1), and the subsequent removal of Zoom access (Exhibit Q; Exhibit W) left Plaintiff unable to participate in his education.

Moreover, the retaliatory threat of stipend suspension by Selcer on February 13, 2026, based on fabricated "performance issues" (Exhibit X-8), demonstrates the escalating harm flowing from Defendants' failure to accommodate. Where Faculty B expressly stated her office door was "always open" and that the reassignment "will not affect your funding" (Exhibit X-7), Selcer unilaterally introduced "performance issues raised by two of the three faculty members" and threatened stipend suspension through 2029 (Exhibit X-8). This pattern of retaliation and harm is directly traceable to the original failure to accommodate.

**G. The Evidence Establishes a Genuine Dispute of Material Fact Precluding Summary Judgment**

*Oross v. Kutztown University* is directly analogous and controlling. There, the court granted summary judgment for the plaintiff because the university's "blanket no-remote policy with no written basis, no individualized assessment of plaintiff's request" and the fact that the "accommodation request was 'dead on arrival'" constituted interference with the interactive process as a matter of law. *Oross* at *1. Here, the same facts exist: a de facto policy against remote attendance; no individualized assessment; and a request that was "dead on arrival" from the start.

*Osseo* confirms that no heightened standard applies. The Supreme Court expressly held that "Schoolchildren bringing ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims related to their education are not required to make a heightened showing of 'bad faith or gross misjudgment' but instead are subject to the same standards that apply in other disability discrimination contexts." *Osseo*, 605 U.S. ___. Accordingly, Plaintiff need only show that his request was reasonable, that Defendants knew of his disability and need, and that Defendants failed to provide the accommodation or engage in the interactive process.

Viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to Plaintiff, a reasonable jury could conclude: (1) Plaintiff has a disability (ADHD and depression, per Exhibit F); (2) Defendants had notice (the medical documentation and repeated communications); (3) Plaintiff requested a reasonable accommodation (Zoom access, which already existed in the department per Exhibit N); (4) Defendants denied that accommodation (the three-week countdown and subsequent removal, per Exhibits I, J, Q, V, W); and (5) Plaintiff suffered harm (homelessness, academic disruption, mental health deterioration, and retaliation, per Exhibits A, F, C, X-8). Summary judgment on Count III must therefore be denied.

**ARGUMENT**

**I. Defendants' Motion for Summary Judgment Must Be Denied Because Genuine Disputes of Material Fact Exist on Every Element of Plaintiff's Fraud Claim**

Under Pennsylvania law, a plaintiff alleging common law fraud must establish: (1) a representation; (2) material to the transaction; (3) made falsely, with knowledge of its falsity or recklessness as to its truth; (4) with intent to induce reliance; (5) justifiable reliance; and (6) injury proximately caused by that reliance. *Gibbs v. Ernst*, 538 Pa. 193, 647 A.2d 882, 889 (1994). Summary judgment is improper where genuine disputes of material fact exist on any element. Defendants' six arguments each fail because Plaintiff has adduced record evidence creating triable issues on all elements.

**A. Defendants' First Argument Fails: Dean Blair's TAP 33 Analysis Was Not "Consistent with Plain Language"—Genuine Disputes Exist as to Falsity and Materiality**

Defendants contend Dean Blair's October 22, 2025 determination that TAP 33 did not apply to Plaintiff's conflict-of-interest disclosure was "consistent with the policy's plain language." (Dkt. 34 at 6-8.) This argument fails on multiple grounds.

First, Blair's own actions belie any claim of consistent application. Exhibit K-2 reveals that when Plaintiff raised a conflict of interest concerning Dr. Selcer—who is both the instructor of a required teaching seminar and a named defendant in Plaintiff's federal lawsuit—Blair's response was to "consult Dr. Selcer as instructor to secure some additional details." She then announced that "Both Dr. Selcer and I strongly concur that it is appropriate for you to remain enrolled." This is not a good-faith policy analysis; it is a process where the conflicted party himself participated in adjudicating whether his own conflict was problematic. Exhibit Z-d, the University's own Title IX training materials, defines a conflict of interest as "[a] conflict between the private interests and the official responsibilities of a person in a position of trust" and mandates that COI be avoided "to ensure fairness" and "[m]aintain the trust, integrity, and credibility of the Title IX process." Blair's consultation with Selcer about his own conflict is precisely the type of self-interested decision-making that TAP 33 and the training (Exhibit Z-d, Slides 46-48) exist to prevent.
